title: Michael casts the Dragon out of Heaven
summary: War broke out in heaven: Michael and his angels fought against the Dragon, and the Dragon and his angels fought back but did not prevail, and there was no longer any place for them in heaven (Revelation 12:7-9). The great Dragon, the ancient serpent called the Devil and Satan, was thrown down to the earth with his host. Michael's martial victory is the pivot that divides the loyal host from the fallen forever.
reenactment_hook: Lead from the front and physically drive the arena's single most powerful adversary off the field entirely (a banish/ejection finisher, not a grind-down), winning the encounter by force of arms without your party ever retreating a step.

epithet_grants.trigger: personally land the ejecting or finishing blow that clears a VVIP/Forsaken-tier table_boss while leading a party to victory
win a VVIP-tier run in which your side never once broke or fled an engagement you started

title: Michael disputes the devil for the body of Moses
summary: Michael the archangel, contending with the devil over the body of Moses, did not dare bring a railing accusation against him, but only said, 'The Lord rebuke thee' (Jude 1:9). He won the contested claim not by force or slander but by restraint and deference to a higher authority's judgment. The episode makes Michael the model of the enforcer who prevails clean.
reenactment_hook: Win a contested claim (a disputed reward, a captured objective, a rival's challenge) without logging a single forbidden or foul action: decline to escalate or trash-talk, defer the ruling to the house adjudicator, and prevail entirely clean.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a contested adjudication or reward dispute with zero forbidden/rule-breaking actions logged for the whole encounter

title: Gabriel interprets the vision of the end
summary: Gabriel is sent swiftly to Daniel to make him understand the vision of the ram and the he-goat (Daniel 8) and, while Daniel prays, to give him the cryptic prophecy of the seventy weeks (Daniel 9). 'Daniel, I am now come to give thee skill and understanding,' he says, decoding what no mortal could read. Gabriel is the herald whose gift is comprehension, not force.
reenactment_hook: Decode a boss's concealed win-condition text or cryptic objective before engaging it, then announce it publicly so the party acts on it exactly as read; win the encounter through understanding rather than damage.

epithet_grants.trigger: read a hidden objective or win-condition and clear the encounter acting on it, with no improvised deviation

title: Gabriel sounds the last trumpet
summary: At the last day the trumpet shall sound, and the dead shall be raised (1 Corinthians 15:52; 1 Thessalonians 4:16). Folk tradition — Christian hymnody and spirituals, 'Blow, Gabriel, blow' — names Gabriel as the horn-blower whose single blast rouses all creation, though scripture leaves the trumpeter unnamed. The herald's one great call commands the whole world's attention at once.
reenactment_hook: At the climax, trigger one arena-wide broadcast / camera-call that rallies your entire side and the crowd simultaneously, and win the deciding phase off that single blast of attention rather than piecemeal chip damage.

epithet_grants.trigger: win an encounter's final phase in the turn immediately following a self-triggered arena-wide hype/camera-call event

sources: 1 Corinthians 15:52; 1 Thessalonians 4:16 (KJV, PD); folk attribution of 'Gabriel's horn' (trumpeter unnamed in scripture)

title: Enoch is translated and remade as Metatron
summary: A righteous man walked with the Most High and was taken, not seeing death; in the Hekhalot books (3 Enoch / Sefer Hekhalot) he is translated to the highest heaven and transfigured into Metatron, the Prince of the Presence, his flesh turned to flame and his stature to that of the world. Enthroned as scribe and vice-regent, he becomes the 'lesser YHWH,' the chief celestial power. It is the paradigm of a base form irreversibly traded up into a higher one.
reenactment_hook: Undergo an irreversible self-transmutation of real cost — permanently surrender your current gear, levels, or role to ascend into a categorically superior form/class — then win the remainder of the run using only the new form.

epithet_grants.trigger: complete a VVIP-tier run after an irreversible self-ascension that forfeited your prior build

sources: 3 Enoch / Sefer Hekhalot ch.3-15 (Hekhalot corpus); Scholem

title: The scribe who sat, and the sixty lashes of fire
summary: Elisha ben Abuyah ('Aher') entered paradise and saw Metatron seated, writing down the merits of Israel, and concluded heretically, 'Perhaps there are two powers in heaven' (Talmud Hagigah 15a). To prove Metatron no rival deity, he was led forth and given sixty lashes of fire for not rising, and was then granted the power to strike Aher's own merits from the record. The recording-angel's authority is that his ledger, not any blade, decides who stands.
reenactment_hook: Win by editing the run's own record rather than out-fighting: spend a costly action (accepting a self-penalty) to strike or rewrite an enemy's logged advantage — delete a boss's accrued buff-stacks or objective progress from the ledger — and finish it in the weakened state you authored.

epithet_grants.trigger: win an encounter by removing an enemy's accrued ledger advantage at a logged cost to yourself

title: Azrael and the ledger of appointed names
summary: The angel of death holds the scroll of mortal fates, recording every name at birth and striking it at the appointed hour, arriving neither early nor late. He is the courteous functionary the desperate try in vain to bargain past their time. His power is not strength but the certainty of the schedule.
reenactment_hook: Land a killing blow on a marked or doomed-flagged target on the exact turn it is scheduled to fall — not one turn early, not one late — honoring the timer instead of rushing or stalling the kill.

epithet_grants.trigger: execute a scheduled target precisely on its appointed turn

sources: folk angel-of-death tradition; cf. malakh ha-mavet

title: The appointment that cannot be fled
summary: Solomon saw the Angel of Death looking sad and learned he was charged to take two of the king's servants; Solomon sent the pair to Luz, the one place the angel held no power (Talmud Sukkah 53a). The next day the angel was glad — the men had been appointed to die in Luz, and by fleeing there they had walked straight into the hour they ran from. It is the root of the 'Appointment in Samarra': the sentence is kept precisely by the flight from it.
reenactment_hook: Mark a fleeing or evasive target with a death-sentence and let it run; instead of chasing, seize and hold the single exit it is bolting toward, so it dies exactly where it fled — on the appointed count.

epithet_grants.trigger: kill a marked fleeing target at the very exit it escaped toward, without chasing it down

title: The fall of the Morning Star
summary: 'How art thou fallen from heaven, O Lucifer, son of the morning!' — who said in his heart he would ascend above the stars and be like the Most High, and was brought down (Isaiah 14:12-15), fused with the Dragon cast to earth (Revelation 12). The light-bringer wagered everything on his own exaltation, lost, and fell. It is the paradigm all-in-and-lose: the original stake that could not be recovered.
reenactment_hook: Go all-in — permanently stake your single most valuable asset (your patron contract, a max-tier item, or your last life) on one Forsaken-tier wager, decline every safe cash-out offered, and win the run outright anyway.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a Forsaken/VVIP run solo after staking something existential and refusing every safe out
recover a run from the underdog/lowest-odds flag after an all-in bet, winning against a foe that outclassed you on paper

title: The Adversary's wager over Job
summary: The Adversary (ha-Satan) presented himself among the sons of God, and when the Most High named Job as blameless, he wagered that stripped of blessing Job would curse God to his face (Job 1-2). Permitted to take Job's wealth, children, and health, he pressed the affliction to its limit — and lost the bet when Job held fast. It is the purest casino myth: a challenger who bets on a contestant's breaking point.
reenactment_hook: As the challenger, stake a real reward on an ally-contestant's resolve: bet that they will hold their objective through escalating voluntary hardship (surrender their buffs and gear a piece at a time), then win the wager by having them endure and prevail stripped.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a wager in which a champion clears the run under escalating voluntary handicaps

title: The oracle of Baal-zebub at Ekron
summary: King Ahaziah, injured by a fall, sent messengers to inquire of Baal-zebub, the god of Ekron, whether he would recover (2 Kings 1:2-3). The fly-lord's shrine still answered even a foreign king — forbidden knowledge from a rotting, demoted godhead. He is the oracle you consult when the clean sources will not speak.
reenactment_hook: Consult a forbidden or corrupt source (a rotting shrine, a demon dealer) to buy hidden intelligence on your fate or a boss's win-condition, paying for it with a contamination (accept an infected/poison condition), then win the encounter using what you learned while carrying that cost.

epithet_grants.trigger: extract a win-condition from a corrupt source and clear the encounter while still carrying the condition it cost you

title: Beelzeboul, last of the fallen, set to cut marble
summary: Compelled by Solomon's ring in the Testament of Solomon, Beelzeboul confesses he was the first angel of the first heaven, now prince of demons, who incites war, jealousy, and murder. Bound and humbled, he is set to saw Theban marble for the Temple, and the other demons howl to see their king brought so low. The proudest fallen power is made to do the meanest labor.
reenactment_hook: Subdue the run's proudest boss-tier enemy without killing it and set it to menial labor for your objective — force the prince to cut your marble: break a wall, haul a load, or turn on its own lieutenants — completing the encounter by its forced service.

epithet_grants.epithet: Lord of the Heavenly Dwelling (Ba'al Zebul)
epithet_grants.trigger: bind a boss-tier proud enemy and complete an objective through its forced labor rather than its death

title: Asmodeus bound to build the Temple
summary: In the Testament of Solomon, Ashmedai the king of demons is bound by the Seal and compelled to labor on the Temple, molding clay for its vessels and revealing the structure's secrets. The demon who knows every load-bearing flaw is made to build — and to hand over the blueprints. He is the architect whose real weapon is the room itself.
reenactment_hook: Win by turning the arena's own structure against its occupants — collapse a wall, trip a hazard, or use a forge/trap the builder left — clearing the encounter through terrain and engineering rather than direct force, and while logging no wrath-triggered action.

epithet_grants.trigger: defeat a boss-tier enemy primarily via the arena's structure/hazards, staying cold (no wrath actions)

title: Asmodeus and the smoke of the fish's heart
summary: Asmodeus, in jealous lust for Sarah, slew each of her seven husbands on their wedding night before the marriage was consummated (Book of Tobit). Guided by Raphael, Tobias burned the heart and liver of a fish on the ashes of incense, and the smoke drove the demon off in flight to Egypt, where he was bound. The demon of wrath is undone not by strength but by one exact, unglamorous counter.
reenactment_hook: Defeat a rage- or lust-scaling boss by refusing to attack in anger (stay cold, letting no wrath action into the log) and applying the single specific consumable counter that drives it off — burn the exact ward-item — rather than trading blows.

epithet_grants.trigger: beat a rage-scaling boss with zero wrath actions logged, using its specific consumable counter

title: 'I will not lie below' — Lilith's refusal and flight
summary: Made equal to Adam from the same earth, Lilith refused to lie beneath him, saying 'We are both equal, for we are both from the earth'; when he would not yield, she spoke the Ineffable Name and flew away (Alphabet of Ben Sira). Three angels — Sanoy, Sansenoy, Samangelof — pursued her, but she would not return, becoming the night that no one commands, sparing only the children whose amulet bears the angels' names. Her defining deed is the refusal itself: sovereignty over submission.
reenactment_hook: Refuse every subordinate arrangement offered — decline all companions, summons, and binding patron buy-ins — and win the run entirely solo and self-governed, escaping any cage/binding encounter by flight rather than fighting through in submission.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a run solo having refused every companion, summon, and binding offer on the board

sources: Alphabet of Ben Sira (8th-10th c., PD)

title: Lilith and Samael, the coupling that must be restrained
summary: In the early Kabbalah (Treatise on the Left Emanation, Isaac ben Jacob ha-Kohen), Lilith and Samael were born intertwined from beneath the Throne of Glory, the dark mirror of Adam and Eve, the Matron and her mate. Their union was a power so fertile it threatened to flood the world with demons, so heaven intervened to keep them apart — Samael was castrated to break the flood. It is the myth of a pairing too strong to be left unchecked.
reenactment_hook: Pair with a single partner into a combo so dominant that the house imposes a handicap or cap on the two of you — then win the run together anyway while restrained, proving the pairing's power even hobbled.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a two-player/paired run under a house-imposed handicap earned by your combo's dominance

sources: Treatise on the Left Emanation (Isaac ben Jacob ha-Kohen, c.1260-80); Scholem

title: The master you cannot serve alongside the other
summary: 'No man can serve two masters... ye cannot serve God and mammon' (Matthew 6:24) — wealth personified as the rival claimant on a servant's whole loyalty. To hold one is to let the other go; there is no dividing the allegiance. Mammon is the second master who will always demand all of you.
reenactment_hook: Make an undivided allegiance choice and win under it: hold a single patron contract for the entire run and refuse every rival gold offer logged against it (or, conversely, take the gold, drop the mission-patron, and still win) — the myth is recreated by serving exactly one master to the end despite at least one buy-out temptation.

epithet_grants.trigger: complete a run holding a single undivided allegiance despite one or more logged buy-out offers

title: Mammon mines Hell to raise Pandaemonium
summary: In Milton's Paradise Lost, Mammon — 'the least erected Spirit that fell from Heaven,' whose eyes were ever downcast on the golden pavement — leads the fallen to ransack Hell's own soil for gold and raise the demon-capital Pandaemonium. In council he argues they seek no war but their own wealth: 'live to ourselves,' drawing riches from the barren deep. He turns the worst ground into a bankroll.
reenactment_hook: Turn the run's most hostile ground into your fortune: extract maximum treasure from a hazard or deprivation zone others avoid, refuse all outside help ('live to ourselves'), and build your win off that self-mined hoard, topping the treasure board.

epithet_grants.trigger: finish a run self-funded from a hazard zone's loot with no external buy-ins, holding the top treasure haul on the board

sources: Milton, Paradise Lost Books I-II (1667, PD)

title: Leviathan and the banquet at the end of days
summary: Leviathan is the twisting sea-serpent no hook can draw out and no spear can pierce (Job 41; Isaiah 27:1), whom God made 'to play with.' At the end of days it and Behemoth will slay each other, and the righteous will feast on their flesh at the final banquet (Bava Batra 74b). The primordial chaos of the sea is unkillable by force — it is undone only by the end itself.
reenactment_hook: Defeat an unkillable-by-force arena beast through the environment alone — recover the great Hook, drain or plug the flooding arena, or bait it into a rival titan so the two destroy each other — never dealing the fatal blow by direct damage.

epithet_grants.trigger: defeat a leviathan-class arena boss without any direct-damage killing blow — win by environment or rival-titan alone, on VVIP tier

sources: Job 41; Isaiah 27:1 (KJV, PD); Babylonian Talmud Bava Batra 74b (PD)

title: The impartial hand of Holy Death
summary: Our Lady of Holy Death answers outlaw and orphan alike, granting protection, healing, and fortune to anyone who petitions her sincerely, for death shows no favoritism between the strong and the weak. Condemned by the Church yet the fastest-growing devotion in the Americas, she is the saint of the nothing-to-lose. Her defining trait is impartiality: she comes equally for all who ask.
reenactment_hook: Show death no favoritism: clear every enemy in an encounter, strong and weak alike, sparing none for a bonus, while carrying a doomed or lowest-odds ally through the whole encounter alive.

epithet_grants.trigger: finish an encounter leaving no enemy alive (spare none) while keeping a doomed-flagged ally standing

title: The broken vow and the swift reckoning
summary: Devotion to Holy Death is contractual and reciprocal: her generosity is lavish and unconditional up front, but a promise broken or a vow unkept is punished swiftly and terribly, her favor withdrawn the instant faith fails (Chesnut). Devotees trade stories of ingratitude repaid with ruin — the jealous saint who keeps every account. Her mercy and her reckoning are the two faces of the same bargain.
reenactment_hook: Swear a self-imposed vow or rule to your patron on camera at run start and keep it to the letter for the entire run; the reckoning for breaking it is immediate forfeit. Win having kept the on-camera vow unbroken end to end.

epithet_grants.trigger: complete a run having kept an on-camera self-imposed vow unbroken from start to finish

sources: Chesnut, Devoted to Death (documented devotional narrative type, not a fixed canonical myth)

title: The saint who moves house to house
summary: Maximón (San Simón), the cigar-smoking, liquor-drinking folk saint, is a carved effigy moved between guardians' houses through the year and plied with moonshine, tobacco, and cash. Petitioners ask him for health, wealth, and love — and, notably, for revenge and success at a rival's expense — favors the orthodox saints will not grant. He is the barstool saint of the crooked favor.
reenactment_hook: Offer a real consumable / vice item (spend something the other patrons would call vulgar — liquor, a cigar, cash) before an encounter, then win it by trickery or ambush rather than a fair fight.

epithet_grants.trigger: win an encounter by ambush or trickery after logging a vice-offering to your patron

sources: widely-published public-sphere folk material (National Geographic; general reference) — cofradía ritual specifics deliberately not collected

title: The clay guardian and the word that unmakes it
summary: In the Prague legend a guardian is shaped from river clay and animated by the written Name — emet, 'truth,' on its brow (or a shem-slip in its mouth) — to protect the community, tireless and unfeeling. It is unmade by erasing the first letter, so that emet ('truth') becomes met ('dead') and the giant collapses to inert clay. Its whole strength and its off-switch are one written word.
reenactment_hook: Animate a summoned construct-ally by inscribing a command word and use it as guardian muscle — or neutralize a rampaging construct enemy by defacing/editing the single character that powers it (turn its 'truth' to 'death') rather than fighting it down.

epithet_grants.trigger: disable a construct boss by editing its animating Name rather than by damage

sources: Prague Golem legend (Scholem; general reference); Rabbi Loew story-only per D-4

title: The forbidden table set for Fortune
summary: 'But ye are they that... prepare a table for Gad [Fortune], and that furnish the drink offering unto Meni [Destiny]' (Isaiah 65:11) — the prophet condemns apostates who set a laden table and mixed wine for the god of luck. It is the first gambling shrine on record: an offering laid out to tilt the odds. Fortune is the patron the house itself descends from.
reenactment_hook: Set an offering at a shrine or table before the run, then win an encounter decided by a genuine low-odds or coin-flip outcome — let the gamble land rather than forcing a sure thing.

epithet_grants.trigger: win an encounter on a genuine low-odds/coin-flip resolution after a pre-run offering

title: 'Fortune has come!' — the naming of Gad
summary: When Zilpah bore Jacob a son, Leah said 'Gad!' — 'Fortune is come' (or 'a troop cometh') — and named the child Gad (Genesis 30:11), tying the fortune-god's name to a birth-omen of good luck. The eponymous naming makes 'Gad' itself a word spoken to greet a lucky break. The name is the omen: to call it is to claim the fortune.
reenactment_hook: Name your fortune before it lands — declare/call a risky outcome out loud (commit to the gamble publicly) before it resolves, then have it break your way and win the encounter off the called shot.

epithet_grants.trigger: win off a called/declared low-odds outcome you committed to before it resolved

title: Arthur draws the sword from the stone
summary: By Merlin's device a sword stood fixed in an anvil upon a stone in a London churchyard, inscribed that whoever drew it was rightwise king born of all England. Barons and knights strained at it in vain, but the boy Arthur, fetching a blade for his foster-brother Kay, drew it out effortlessly and so was revealed as Uther's true heir. It was not strength but rightful worth that freed the blade.
reenactment_hook: Attempt a worth-gated interaction (a locked/attuned weapon or throne-object that rejects the unworthy); pass its worth-check where at least one rival contestant has failed the same check, claim the weapon, and win the run wielding it.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a run wielding a worth-gated/attuned weapon you could claim only by passing its worth-check, where at least one rival failed that check

sources: Malory, Le Morte d'Arthur I (the sword in the stone)

title: The last battle of Camlann and the passing of the king
summary: At Camlann the host of Arthur and the host of the traitor Mordred destroyed one another. Arthur ran his son through with a spear, and Mordred in dying dealt the king a mortal head-wound. The realm fell with them; the wounded Arthur was borne away by ship to the isle of Avalon under the promise that he would one day return — the once and future king.
reenactment_hook: Engage the run's final boss at the cost of a mortal wound — strike the win-condition blow while taking lethal damage yourself — and either survive the exchange, or convert the loss into a banked revival that returns you stronger to a later table.

epithet_grants.trigger: on a VIP table or higher, fall in the final battle after dealing the boss its death-blow, then return in a later run and win — banking the sleeping-king revival

sources: Malory XXI (Camlann); Geoffrey, Historia Regum Britanniae

title: Merlin engineers the begetting of Arthur
summary: King Uther burned for Igraine, wife of Duke Gorlois, shut safe in the castle of Tintagel. Merlin worked a magic that gave Uther the exact likeness of Gorlois, so that he passed the guards and lay with Igraine the very night the true duke died in battle; Arthur was conceived. Merlin took the child as his fee, and out of a bastard's begetting engineered the coming king.
reenactment_hook: Before a key encounter, engineer a disguise or planted deception (impersonate a gatekeeper, forge access) that bypasses an otherwise-impregnable defense, then convert that infiltration into the run's win.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a run whose outcome you secured by a pre-planned disguise or planted deception that bypassed an otherwise-impregnable defense

sources: Malory I (Uther, Igraine, Tintagel); Geoffrey, Historia

title: Merlin foresees his doom and walks into it
summary: Merlin, who saw past and future, foresaw his own undoing yet could not resist his love for the damsel Nimue. Besotted, he taught her his deepest craft, including a spell of binding; and knowing the doom he walked toward, he went to it anyway. His works — the king, the Round Table — outlasted him after he was sealed forever beneath the stone.
reenactment_hook: On a Forsaken table, correctly foresee the run's fatal turn, permanently hand a signature skill/ability to a rival exactly as you predicted, then win via a legacy play (a trap or asset you set up beforehand) that pays out after you are removed from the board.

epithet_grants.trigger: on a Forsaken table, foretell the run's fatal turn, permanently surrender a signature ability as foreseen, and still win via a play you set up beforehand

sources: Malory IV (Merlin sealed under the stone); Robert de Boron / Prose Merlin

title: Morgan steals and drowns the scabbard of Excalibur
summary: Morgan le Fay armed her lover Accolon with the true Excalibur and its scabbard against a disguised Arthur, but Arthur prevailed and Accolon died. In vengeance Morgan seized the scabbard — the greater treasure, for while worn it let no wound bleed — and cast it into a deep lake, lost forever, so that Arthur would one day bleed to death of a wound the sheath would have stanched.
reenactment_hook: Steal or disable an enemy's condition-negating defensive relic (the item that stops bleeding or the like), restoring their vulnerability, then finish the run exploiting the weakness you created.

epithet_grants.trigger: strip an enemy of a condition-negating defensive relic and win the finale by exploiting the vulnerability you restored

title: Morgan ferries the dying Arthur to Avalon
summary: When Arthur lay dying of the wound of Camlann, a black barge came bearing Morgan le Fay and the queens of Avalon. Setting aside her long enmity, Morgan took her brother's head in her lap and bore him across the water to the healing isle, there to tend his hurt against the day of his return.
reenactment_hook: Heal or revive a downed ally (or even a defeated rival) at real, permanent, unrecoverable cost to yourself — spend a stat or resource you cannot get back — and still close out the run.

epithet_grants.trigger: heal or revive a downed ally at permanent, unrecoverable cost to yourself and still close the run

sources: Malory XXI (the queens bear Arthur to Avalon)

title: The Lady of the Lake gives Excalibur
summary: Having lost his first sword, Arthur was rowed by Merlin onto a lake where an arm clothed in white samite rose from the water holding a sword. The Lady of the Lake granted him Excalibur and its scabbard — every such gift a loan against a destined return. Merlin bade him prize the scabbard above the blade, for it kept its wearer from bleeding.
reenactment_hook: Hand your best weapon (or a loaned/destined arm) to a weaker ally and invest the run in their rise, letting them land the finishing blow — then return the weapon to the pool at run's end rather than keeping it.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a run in which you handed your best weapon to a weaker ally and let them land the finishing blow, then surrendered it back

sources: Malory I (the Lady gives Excalibur; the scabbard's virtue)

title: Nimue coaxes out Merlin's craft and seals him under stone
summary: The damsel Nimue, pursued by the aging Merlin, feigned enough favor to draw his every secret from him while granting him nothing. When she had learned his craft she used his own spell of binding to seal him alive beneath a great stone, whence he could never come out — the master undone by the pupil he taught.
reenactment_hook: Feign alliance with a boss or rival to learn its signature technique or win-condition, then defeat it using its own move turned against it.

epithet_grants.trigger: learn a boss's signature technique by feigning alliance, then defeat it using its own move turned against it

sources: Malory IV (Nimue seals Merlin under the stone)

title: Lancelot and Guinevere, the love that cracks the fellowship
summary: Lancelot, the peerless knight of the world, loved Queen Guinevere and she him; when the love was exposed and the queen condemned to burn, Lancelot rescued her from the fire, killing kinsmen of the court in the doing. The very prowess that made him the best knight split the fellowship and lit the civil war that ended Camelot.
reenactment_hook: Complete a run in which the single stat you invested most heavily is the exact thing that dooms an ally (a forbidden bond or overreach), yet you still win — the strength that breaks the fellowship.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a run in which the stat you invested most heavily directly causes an ally's downfall
trigger a hidden bond between two contestants that raises the whole table's stakes, then win the high-stakes fallout

sources: Malory XVIII-XX (the discovery and the rescue from the fire); Chretien, Knight of the Cart

title: Gawain keeps the covenant of the beheading game
summary: A green giant rode into Camelot and offered a beheading game: strike him one free blow, and take his return a year hence. Gawain beheaded him — and the Green Knight picked up his own head and rode off. Keeping the appointment, Gawain resisted the host's wife but hid the girdle she said would guard him; at the return the knight feigned twice and left only a nick, punishing that one concealment. Faith earned the survivable wound; the hidden charm was the only failure.
reenactment_hook: Take a free opening strike on a covenant-boss, then honor its return by standing motionless to receive its blow WITHOUT equipping any hidden protection — win by faith, not by cheating; a concealed charm forfeits the test and takes the wound.

epithet_grants.trigger: honor a covenant-boss's return blow by standing motionless with no hidden protection equipped, and survive — a concealed charm forfeits it

sources: Sir Gawain and the Green Knight (the Gawain-poet): the beheading game and the exchange of winnings

title: Galahad achieves the Holy Grail and is raptured at Sarras
summary: Galahad the sinless, with Percival and Bors, bore the Holy Grail by ship to the city of Sarras. There he beheld the Grail's uttermost mysteries face to face, and at the height of that vision he asked to die; a hand from heaven took up both the vessel and his soul. He alone achieved the quest, and was raptured out of the world at his triumph.
reenactment_hook: Complete a flawless, zero-taboo-break (or zero-damage) run to claim the Grail-tier jackpot, then take the mid-triumph 'rapture' exit that banks maximum payout at the peak.

epithet_grants.trigger: claim the Grail-tier jackpot via a flawless clear and take the peak-exit rapture payout
clear a Forsaken table taking zero taboo breaks

sources: Vulgate Queste del Saint Graal (the achievement at Sarras); Malory XVII

title: Galahad claims the Siege Perilous
summary: At the Round Table stood one empty seat, the Siege Perilous, made by Merlin and deadly to any unworthy who dared sit it. On the day Galahad came to court, letters of gold naming him appeared upon its rim, and he took the seat unharmed — the sign that the destined Grail-knight had come at last.
reenactment_hook: Take a lethal all-in worth-gate (a seat or object that erases the unworthy) on a record clean enough to pass it, surviving where an unworthy contestant would be destroyed, and claim the top-tier reward.

epithet_grants.trigger: take a lethal all-in worth-gate (a seat/object that erases the unworthy) on a spotless record and survive to claim the pot

sources: Malory XIII (Galahad's name appears on the seat he claims); Vulgate Queste

title: Mordred's treason and usurpation
summary: Left as regent while Arthur warred overseas, Mordred forged word of the king's death, seized the crown, and moved to take Queen Guinevere to wife, turning much of the realm against its absent king. His treason drew Arthur home to the ruin of Camlann, where father and traitor-son slew each other.
reenactment_hook: While the table's champion is absent or pinned, seize a contested objective and turn neutral or allied units to your side (subvert the fellowship), then hold the throne to win.

epithet_grants.trigger: seize a contested throne/objective while the champion is absent, subverting neutral or allied units to your side, and hold it to win

title: The endless hunt of the Questing Beast
summary: The Questing Beast — serpent's head, leopard's body, lion's haunches, hart's feet, and in its belly the belling of thirty couple of hounds — ranged the land as a marvel none could take. King Pellinore hunted it all his life, and after him Sir Palamedes, but by every account the beast was pursued forever and caught never.
reenactment_hook: Face a chase-boss that cannot be killed by damage; survive every pursuit phase and win by out-enduring it or trapping it via terrain/lure, reading its audio tell (the belly-noise) to pre-empt each move.

epithet_grants.trigger: defeat a damage-immune chase-boss by out-enduring every pursuit phase or trapping it, never by raw damage

sources: Malory (Beast Glatisant, the belly of thirty couple of hounds); Post-Vulgate

title: Bedivere returns Excalibur to the water
summary: The dying Arthur bade Sir Bedivere cast Excalibur into the lake. Twice Bedivere, thinking so rich a sword too precious to lose, hid it and lied; twice Arthur knew. At the third bidding he cast it true, and an arm rose from the water, caught the sword, brandished it, and drew it under — the loan returned to the Lady at the last.
reenactment_hook: Surrender a high-value relic you are tempted to keep — return it to its rightful owner or to the pool (destroy/bank it) instead of hoarding it — and still win the run on your remaining strength.

epithet_grants.trigger: surrender a high-value relic by returning it to its owner or the pool instead of keeping it, and still win the run

sources: Malory XXI (Bedivere casts Excalibur into the lake; the hand catches it)

title: The Quest of the Holy Grail scatters the fellowship
summary: At Pentecost the Grail passed veiled through Camelot, and the whole fellowship swore to seek it. But the quest was a sieve of worth: most knights wandered and failed, some died, and the questing scattered and broke the Round Table's unity. Only the pure came near the vessel; the flawed who reached for it were ruined.
reenactment_hook: Pursue a hidden ultimate objective that requires a spotless record and defeats most seekers; reach it where the rest of the field falls away, banking the worth-gated prize.

epithet_grants.trigger: reach a hidden worth-gated ultimate objective that defeats most of the field, on a clean record

sources: Vulgate Queste del Saint Graal (the vow and the scattering); Malory XIII-XVII

title: The founding of the Round Table and the Pentecost Oath
summary: Merlin made the Round Table, a table with no head so that no knight might claim precedence over another; it had been Uther's, and came to Arthur as Guinevere's dowry with a hundred knights. About it Arthur seated his fellowship and bound them by the Pentecost Oath — to give mercy, flee treason and outrage, and succour the helpless — renewed each year. Equality was built into its very shape.
reenactment_hook: Opt into a fellowship party-oath (a run modifier constraining your conduct — give mercy, flee treason, succour the helpless) and win the run with the whole party alive and the oath unbroken, sharing the pooled fellowship bonus.

epithet_grants.trigger: opt into a fellowship party-oath and win with the whole party alive and the oath unbroken

sources: Malory III (the table came via Leodegrance as Guinevere's dowry; the Pentecost Oath); Wace, Roman de Brut

title: Whoever Calls the Name Is Delivered
summary: In the Lotus Sutra's twenty-fifth chapter, the Universal Gateway, the Buddha teaches that whoever, caught in any of the great perils — fire, flood, the great ocean, falling, blades and staves, chains, or bandits and demons — single-mindedly calls the name of Avalokiteshvara is delivered at that instant. The bodhisattva takes on whichever of thirty-three forms will save the one who calls. Salvation is unconditional: the name alone suffices, and this is the textual root of Guanyin devotion across East Asia.
reenactment_hook: When any ally on the floor is downed or calls for aid, cross the map and rescue them before they fall — clear the run having revived or saved every imperiled ally at least once, losing no party member, scored on saves rather than kills.

epithet_grants.trigger: Complete a run in which every downed ally is revived or rescued and no party member dies, on a VIP table or higher
Answer every distress call raised on the floor before its caller falls, in a single run

sources: Lotus Sutra ch. 25 'Universal Gateway' (Kern 1884 PD; Watson tr.)

title: The Head That Shattered from Grief
summary: Avalokiteshvara vowed before the Buddha Amitabha never to rest until every being was freed, swearing that if he ever wavered his own body would break into a thousand pieces. Laboring immeasurably, he looked out, saw how many still suffered, and his head split into eleven fragments from grief. Amitabha gathered the pieces, gave him eleven faces to perceive every cry and a thousand arms — each with an eye in its palm — to answer them, and the bodhisattva took up the same impossible task again.
reenactment_hook: Take on an objective the run declares impossible or over-scaled; suffer a real, permanent breaking cost (lose a body part, a stat, or reach the brink of defeat) and, instead of abandoning it, resume the exact same objective and complete it anyway.

epithet_grants.trigger: After being reduced to a fail-state (downed, or a body part permanently disabled), resume and complete the original objective anyway, on a VIP table or higher

sources: Thurman, Tricycle 'Avalokiteshvara in Tibet'; Himalayan Art Resources set 414 (Eleven-Faced Avalokiteshvara)

title: The Five Divine Messengers
summary: In the Devaduta Sutta the wardens of hell bring the newly dead before King Yama, who asks each whether they heeded the five divine messengers sent among mortals — a newborn, an old person, a sick person, a punished criminal, and a corpse — warnings that they too were subject to birth, age, sickness, wrongdoing, and death. Those who ignored the warnings are handed to the consequences of their own deeds, never to Yama's spite. The sutta closes with the judge's private wish: that he might one day be reborn a human while a Buddha walks the world.
reenactment_hook: Read and log every foreshadowing the dungeon posts (the diegetic 'divine messengers' — omens, signage, boss telegraphs) before engaging the boss, touching nothing until all are observed; then, given a beaten enemy at your mercy, judge it by its own recorded conduct — spare the yielding, condemn the unrepentant — rather than reflexively killing.

epithet_grants.trigger: Observe every warning on the floor before engaging the boss, then resolve one yielding enemy with mercy and one unrepentant enemy with condemnation in the same run
Do the above on a VVIP / Forsaken table

title: The Lord of Death Is Conquered and Bound
summary: A Tibetan legend tells that a hermit near enlightenment after fifty years was beheaded by cattle thieves who also slew a buffalo in his cave; enraged at losing the goal at the last moment, he set the buffalo's head on his own body and rampaged across Tibet as Yama, the Lord of Death. The terrified people prayed to Manjushri, who took a form more terrible still — Yamantaka, ten times as fearsome — conquered Yama, and bound him into service as a protector of the Dharma. Wisdom defeated death not by out-fighting it but by out-terrifying it.
reenactment_hook: Face a death-dealing boss stronger than you and win the threat/aggro contest — present a more terrifying aspect than it (out-intimidate, seize its fear-mechanic) — then bind it into your service or turn it to your side rather than destroying it outright.

epithet_grants.trigger: Out-intimidate a death-type boss stronger than you and bind it into your service instead of killing it, on a VIP table or higher

sources: TOP-UP. Tibetan Yamantaka-subjugation legend (Buddha Weekly; Tibetan Buddhist Encyclopedia; Met Museum acc. 37807). Yamantaka is a census-restricted deity — flagged; the myth about Yama himself is public-sphere.

title: The Ward of the Four Kings
summary: Vessavana, Great King of the North and lord of the yakshas, comes with the other three Heavenly Kings and offers the Atanata ward — a protective recitation shielding the virtuous from the yakkhas and demons that would harm them. The Atanatiya Sutta doubles as the charter of the Four Kings' cosmic duty: to stand at the four gates of the world and hold back what preys on the defenceless.
reenactment_hook: Declare and hold a ward-line — a chokepoint, doorway, or protected ally — for a full encounter so that no enemy crosses it to reach the protected, winning the fight without the warded target ever taking a hit.

epithet_grants.trigger: Hold a declared ward-line through a full boss encounter with the protected target taking zero damage, on a VIP table or higher

title: The Flying Storehouse of Mount Shigi
summary: On Mount Shigi lived Myoren, a hermit devoted to Bishamonten. His alms-bowl would fly down each day to the granary of a rich miser in the valley and bring back a single bowl of rice. When the miser trapped the bowl, it lifted his entire storehouse into the air and carried it up the mountain; the hoarded rice was released only once he agreed to give freely. By the same god's power Myoren's prayer later cured the stricken Emperor from a distance. The mountain's temple, Chogosonshi-ji, is Bishamonten's to this day.
reenactment_hook: Locate a hoarding enemy or NPC sitting on a locked stockpile; seize the entire cache by force, then release it back only once they yield and share — clearing the floor by redistributing hoarded resources to the party rather than by killing the hoarder.

epithet_grants.trigger: Seize a hoarder's full stockpile and redistribute it to the party, leaving the hoarder alive after they consent to share

sources: TOP-UP. Shigisan Engi Emaki (Heian miracle scroll of Bishamonten's temple): en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shigisan_Engi_Emaki; japanesemythology.wordpress.com (Myoren, flying granary); Chogosonshi-ji temple.

title: The Jambhalas Take the Blow
summary: As the sage taught on Vulture Peak, his jealous cousin Devadatta rolled boulders down to crush him. The five Jambhalas threw their own bodies over him — the Yellow and White struck on the head, the Black in the belly. Their wounds were soothed with healing nectar, and in return they were charged to spend their inexhaustible wealth eradicating the poverty of all beings. The living rite of pouring water over Jambhala's head, to cool that old wound, re-enacts the bargain.
reenactment_hook: Interpose your own body to take a blow aimed at an ally — absorb the damage or condition meant for another — survive it, and then answer the wound by giving that same ally a resource of real value from your own stock.

epithet_grants.trigger: Take a lethal-tier blow meant for an ally, survive it, and then gift that ally a resource of real value from your own stock

sources: dossier §3.4 (Jambhala) — PRACTICE-LORE grade, flagged (not locatable in a canonical sutra)

title: Compassion Wears a Wealth-Bearing Shape
summary: Seeing beings ground down by poverty and disease, the compassion of Avalokiteshvara took a wealth-bearing form — White Jambhala, said to spring from the bodhisattva's own eye. Riding a turquoise dragon with a jewel-spitting mongoose in his hand, he purifies the karma that keeps beings poor and heals the sicknesses that poverty breeds: wealth wielded as medicine, never as gain.
reenactment_hook: Cure an ally's debilitating affliction (lift an infected/poison/bleeding-type condition) by permanently spending your own accumulated wealth or a valuable consumable — curing others rather than buying advantage for yourself.

epithet_grants.trigger: Cure at least two allies' debilitating conditions in one run using only your own permanently-spent wealth or consumables, never on yourself

sources: TOP-UP. White Jambhala emanation-of-Avalokiteshvara origin — PRACTICE-LORE grade, flagged (thangkatibet.com; arhatrupa.com; Tibetan Buddhist Encyclopedia). Not a canonical sutra.

title: The Householder Who Asked in Order to Give
summary: The householder Sucandra, poor and burdened with a large family, came to the Buddha asking not for himself alone but for the means to feed his household and still have surplus to give as alms. The Buddha taught him the dharani of Vasudhara, and her stream of treasure filled his storehouses that same moment. When the monk Ananda marvelled at the sudden fortune, the Buddha charged him to carry the goddess's rite onward 'for the good of many.'
reenactment_hook: Enter a run poor; accumulate wealth and loot on the floor for the stated purpose of giving it away, and end the run having funded another contestant's or NPC's survival — never spending the windfall on your own build.

epithet_grants.trigger: Acquire a run's windfall and give it entirely away to fund another contestant's or NPC's survival, keeping none for your own build

sources: Vasudhara Dharani, 'The Inquiry of the Layman Sucandra' (Toh 662 family; Cambridge MS-ADD-01355; Samye Translations)

title: The Pearls That Flowed Only Because He Gave Them Away
summary: The Tibetan historian Taranatha records that Vasudhara granted the monk Buddhajnana three hundred pearl necklaces every day, and a steady stream of buyers besides. Buddhajnana kept nothing for himself — he poured it all into his monastery, supporting fellow monks, commissioning statues and ritual objects, and giving to the poor. Because the wealth never stopped at him, the goddess never stopped giving it, for the rest of his life.
reenactment_hook: Secure a recurring per-floor income or boon, then fully spend it on the party each floor — hoard it even once and the source dries up; finish the run having recycled every payout into the group and kept none for yourself.

epithet_grants.trigger: Sustain a recurring per-floor boon across a full run by disbursing every payout to the party and hoarding none

sources: TOP-UP. Taranatha, History of Buddhism in India (Buddhajnana account) — academic reference via en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vasudhara and Rubin Museum Project Himalayan Art (Goddess of Prosperity, Vasudhara).

title: The Goddess Who Married a Dragon on Condition
summary: For centuries the five-headed dragon Gozuryu devoured the children of the Koshigoe villages. Amid storms and strange lights Benzaiten raised the island of Enoshima from the sea and alighted upon it. The smitten dragon proposed marriage; the goddess accepted only on the condition that he end his devouring and turn his strength to guarding the people. He agreed, and the monster became a protector — a plague ended not by slaughter but by a bargain struck in the open.
reenactment_hook: Face an enemy you could fight, and instead broker a public conditional deal — offer terms on camera and win its allegiance so it fights for you or stands down — converting a would-be boss into a guardian rather than killing it.

epithet_grants.trigger: Convert a boss-tier enemy into an ally through a public conditional bargain instead of killing it, on a VIP table or higher

title: The Spring Where Money Doubles
summary: On the day of the snake, in the month of the snake, in the year of the snake, the serpent-god Ugafukujin appeared in a dream to the warlord who founded Kamakura and bade him seek a hidden spring in a western valley and pray there to both the kami and the buddhas. He did, and the land found peace — and the spring's water, it was found, doubled any coin washed in it. Because the vision fell on the snake's day, the shrine was given to Benten, goddess of everything that flows: the one place in the world where money multiplies by being washed.
reenactment_hook: Follow a cryptic omen to a hidden or optional location on the floor, then stake a real resource there to double it — invest a genuine amount of your own wealth into the concealed shrine and walk out with it multiplied.

epithet_grants.epithet: One of the Seven Lucky Gods
epithet_grants.trigger: Follow an omen to a hidden shrine, stake a real resource to double it, and win the run

sources: TOP-UP. Zeniarai Benten Ugafuku Shrine legend, Kamakura (japan-guide.com/e/e3111.html; kamakuraguide.com); Ugajin/Benzaiten fusion (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ugajin).

title: Compassion With Its Teeth Bared
summary: In an age too degenerate for gentle mercy to reach, the compassion of Avalokiteshvara took on its opposite shape — Mahakala, the Great Black One, wrath worn as a service. He is the same compassion that saves by terrifying: chief protector of the Dharma across every Tibetan school, standing in the charnel grounds so the vulnerable need not. His fury is never personal; it is love with its teeth bared.
reenactment_hook: Take the terrifying enforcer role in a fight — draw every enemy's aggression onto yourself so no ally is targeted for the whole encounter — and win without any ally taking damage and without dealing the killing blow for personal loot.

epithet_grants.trigger: Hold all enemy aggression on yourself for a full encounter so every ally takes zero damage, and win without striking a killing blow for personal loot, on a VIP table or higher

sources: dossier §3.7 (Mahakala). GRADE CALL: dossier said 'world_myth-adjacent'; graded heroic_epic here — an emanation/protector origin within the Avalokiteshvara cycle, not a creation/apocalypse pivot.
en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mahakala; samyeinstitute.org/wiki/mahakala; Himalayan Art Resources set 173

title: The God Who Vowed to Feed the Monks
summary: When Saicho climbed Mount Hiei to found his monastery and despaired of feeding three thousand monks, the Great Black One appeared to him as an old man with three faces and six arms and vowed: 'I will provide food for three thousand people daily, and give fortune and long life to those who honor me.' From then Daikokuten — Mahakala's wealth-and-kitchen face — stood in the monastery kitchens as the god who keeps the whole community fed.
reenactment_hook: Take on the quartermaster role for the run — keep the entire party supplied (food, healing, resources never dropping to empty for any member) across a full multi-floor run, provisioning others before yourself.

epithet_grants.trigger: Keep every party member above resource-starvation for a full run while taking your own share last

sources: TOP-UP. Daikokuten Mt. Hiei / Saicho kitchen-god legend (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Daikokuten; onmarkproductions.com/html/daikoku.shtml).

title: The Queen Who Destroyed What She Loved to Keep a Vow
summary: As Remati, queen of Lanka, Palden Lhamo swore to turn her husband the king from his war on the Dharma or else end his royal line herself. She failed to turn him — and their son was being raised to be the one who would finally extinguish Buddhism. So while the king hunted she killed the boy and rode away for good on her mule, flaying his skin for a saddle-blanket and taking his skull for a cup. The king's parting arrow struck the mule's flank; she caught the wound and made it into an eye. From her saddle hang black-and-white dice, with which she reads the fates.
reenactment_hook: Swear a run-binding vow on camera; when keeping it demands destroying your single most valuable asset (your best skill, your strongest gear, a key ally-bond), do it — permanently sacrifice that asset to honor the vow — and finish the run handicapped without it.

epithet_grants.trigger: Keep a camera-sworn vow by permanently destroying your single most valuable asset, then clear the run without it, on a VIP table or higher
Do the above on a VVIP / Forsaken table

sources: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Palden_Lhamo; Nebesky-Wojkowitz, Oracles and Demons of Tibet

title: The Oracle Lake That Shows the Next Life
summary: Palden Lhamo promised the first Dalai Lama that she would guard his incarnation lineage down the centuries, and her pledge lives in the oracle lake Lhamo La-tso. Its surface shows visions to those who meditate on its shore in the right frame of mind — letters, roofs, and houses that point the way to where the next incarnation has been born. In 1935 a regent read three letters and a jade-and-gold roof on the water, and by them the child who would be the fourteenth was found.
reenactment_hook: Consult a divination point (a scrying pool or oracle) that reveals a hidden objective only as a cryptic clue, then interpret the clue to find and reach a concealed target the dungeon never marks on the map.

epithet_grants.epithet: Protectress of Tibet and the Dalai Lamas
epithet_grants.trigger: Use an oracle vision to locate and reach an unmarked hidden objective, then complete the run

sources: TOP-UP. Lhamo La-tso oracle lake (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lhamo_La-tso; tsemrinpoche.com Palden Lhamo). References the Dalai Lama lineage as a legend cycle only — no historical person as an entity (D-4).

title: Patience Is the Stronger Weapon
summary: The captured asura king Vepacitti heaped abuse on Sakka, king of the gods, to his face, and Sakka answered every insult with patience. When his own charioteer and court called forbearance the mark of a coward, Sakka argued them down: the one who, knowing he could crush his foe, chooses restraint out of understanding is the stronger — 'there is no higher forbearance than this.' He won the age's argument without throwing a blow.
reenactment_hook: Under sustained provocation — a taunting enemy, a jeering audience, allies urging violence — decline to retaliate, take the hits without striking back at the provoker, and win the encounter by another route, then sway the crowd to your restraint.

epithet_grants.trigger: Win a sustained-provocation encounter without a single retaliatory strike while the audience meter rises, on a VIP table or higher

title: The Chariot That Turned for the Nests
summary: Routed by the asuras and fleeing in his thousand-horse chariot, Sakka came to the silk-cotton wood where the garudas nest. Rather than smash through and leave the young birds nestless, he told his charioteer Matali to turn the chariot around — 'better we surrender our lives to the asuras than destroy these nests.' The pursuing asuras, seeing the king of the gods wheel to face them, took it for a counterattack and fled. Mercy won the battle that force had lost.
reenactment_hook: In a running retreat where the fastest escape route would destroy a vulnerable, non-combatant feature (a nest, civilians, an ally's fallen body), take the costlier path that spares them instead — and turn the retreat into a win, the pursuer breaking off, without harming the protected.

epithet_grants.trigger: During a forced retreat, decline the escape route that would destroy a vulnerable non-combatant feature, take the costlier path, and still win the encounter

title: The Mortal Who Kept Seven Vows and Won a Throne
summary: Sakka was not always king of the gods. As the young man Magha he kept seven lifelong observances — he supported his parents, honored his elders, spoke gently and never divisively, lived free of stinginess and gave freely, told the truth, and mastered his anger. By those seven vows, kept without a single lapse, a mortal earned the throne of the Thirty-Three.
reenactment_hook: Declare a set of personal restrictions at run start (never flee, always give, never strike first, speak only truth to NPCs, and so on) and keep every one of them unbroken through the entire run to the boss — a self-imposed vow-set completed flawlessly.

epithet_grants.trigger: Declare a personal vow-set at run start and keep every vow unbroken through the entire run to the boss

title: The Five Omens of a God's Death
summary: Even the king of the gods holds an expiring office. The Itivuttaka names the five omens that appear when a deva's merit runs out: his flower-garlands wither, his once-spotless clothes soil, sweat breaks from his body, his radiance dims, and his own throne no longer feels like home. When these show on Sakka himself, the gods wish him a good rebirth — for heaven is a suite one is eventually comped out of, and the highest seat at the table is still a seat you must one day leave.
reenactment_hook: When you hold a peak buff or commanding advantage that the run signals is about to expire (a timed empowerment, a decaying lead), spend it all in one decisive all-in play before it fades — wagering your entire accumulated advantage rather than hoarding it to watch it wither.

epithet_grants.trigger: When a peak advantage is about to expire, spend it entirely in one decisive all-in that wins the encounter, on a VVIP / Forsaken table

title: Fifty-Three Teachers and a Leap Into Fire
summary: The youth Sudhana set out to learn the way and was sent from teacher to teacher — fifty-three in all: monks and kings, a night-goddess, a ship's captain, a courtesan, a small boy, and great bodhisattvas — each teaching what they knew and passing him onward. At the brahmin Jayosmayatana's, the lesson was a razor-edged mountain and a pit of blazing fire; told to climb and leap in, Sudhana did — and the flames turned to the coolness of tranquil bliss. Faith proven by the leap, not by proof beforehand.
reenactment_hook: Seek out and interact with every teacher-class or lore NPC on the floor before the boss (leave none unspoken-to), then commit to one leap of faith the dungeon gives no proof is safe — jump into an unmarked hazard or gate on faith — and clear the run by it.

epithet_grants.trigger: Speak with every teacher-class NPC on the floor before the boss, then take a blind leap of faith into an unverified hazard that clears the run

sources: Gandavyuha / 'The Stem Array' (84000.co/post/the-stem-array-gandavyuha); Jayosmayatana fire-leap (Cleary tr.); Britannica (Gandavyuha)

title: The Army That Broke Against a Man Who Would Not Move
summary: On the night before the great awakening, Mara — lord of the desire realm — threw his ten armies at the still figure seated beneath the tree: sensual craving, discontent, hunger and thirst, longing, sloth, cowardice, doubt, spite, greed for gain and fame, and self-exaltation. Each was named aloud and refused. Mara demanded by what right the seat was claimed; the figure touched the earth, and the earth itself answered as witness. Mara's mounted host broke, and the tempter withdrew, defeated by one being who would not move.
reenactment_hook: Against a boss that grows stronger every time you attack it or accept its offers, hold a single position (the earth-witness altar) unmoved for the whole encounter — refuse every escalating buff, bribe, or temptation it dangles, striking nothing — until its assault exhausts itself and it withdraws.

epithet_grants.trigger: Defeat a temptation-boss by holding the earth-witness position unmoved and refusing every escalating offer, striking nothing, on a VIP table or higher

sources: dossier §3.11 (Mara). The excluded founder is staged off-screen/unnamed per binding — 'the sage under the tree.'
Sn 3.2 Padhana Sutta; SN 4 Mara-samyutta (accesstoinsight.org; suttacentral.net)

title: The Daughters Who Could Find No Purchase
summary: After Mara was beaten, his three daughters — Craving, Aversion, and Passion — offered to finish what their father could not. They approached the awakened one in the forms of maidens of every age, hundreds of shapes of desire, and asked why he sat alone and unmoved. He was beyond their reach; not one form found purchase. The daughters conceded their father's defeat was total and withdrew.
reenactment_hook: After clearing a boss, resist the victory-lap temptation the run offers — decline every enticing bonus, shortcut, or loot-lure that appears in the aftermath, each of which secretly weakens you — and leave the floor having taken none of the bait.

epithet_grants.trigger: Decline every post-boss victory-lure on a floor, taking none of the bait

sources: dossier §3.11 (Mara). Founder staged off-screen/unnamed per binding.

title: The Hungry Dead at the Doorpost
summary: The hungry dead stand just outside the walls of their old homes, at the crossroads and doorposts, waiting — unseen and unremembered by the kin still feasting inside. Their needle-throats and mountain-bellies let nothing in, so they cannot feed themselves. But when the living dedicate a gift of food and drink in their name, the merit reaches them and their long hunger is eased. So the Tirokudda Sutta teaches the rite of remembering the dead by giving on their behalf.
reenactment_hook: Face a hostile, endlessly-respawning mob that cannot be cleared by damage; instead dedicate a food or resource offering at a floor shrine in their name and pacify the whole room by generosity rather than killing a single one.

epithet_grants.trigger: Pacify an endlessly-respawning hungry-ghost room by dedicating an offering rather than killing

title: The Morrígan's offer, and her threefold beast-assault at the ford
summary: During his single-handed defense of Ulster the Morrígan comes to Cú Chulainn as a young noblewoman, offering her love and her wealth; he refuses her scornfully, and she vows to hinder him at the ford. In his next combat she attacks in three forms at once — an eel that coils his legs, a grey she-wolf that stampedes cattle over him, a hornless red heifer leading the herd — and he wounds each in turn. Later, disguised as an old woman milking a cow, she wins three healing blessings from him and so is cured of the three wounds.
reenactment_hook: Refuse an NPC's on-camera offer of aid or a gift, then win the very engagement you were warned would turn against you while beset by three summoned beast-form ambushes (grapple, stampede, lure) — surviving all three mid-duel without accepting the spurned help.

epithet_grants.trigger: spurn an NPC's offered aid and win the warned engagement while withstanding all three beast-form ambushes on a VIP table or higher

sources: Táin Bó Cúailnge (Dunn tr. 1914, PD); Táin Bó Regamna prelude

title: The Dagda and the Morrígan unite at the ford of the Unshin
summary: At Samhain the Dagda finds the Morrígan straddling the ford of the Unshin, washing. They unite there, and she pledges her aid against the Fomorians before the great battle — promising to take the blood and heart of Indech the Fomorian king, and to rouse the wizards of Ireland. The threshold union seals the Tuatha Dé's fortune for the coming war.
reenactment_hook: Broker a binding pact with a powerful NPC at a threshold room (a ford or gate) that hands you the enemy commander's weakness and a pledge of summoned reinforcements for the decisive fight, then cash the pact by winning that fight.

epithet_grants.trigger: broker a threshold pact granting enemy-leader intel and win the run's decisive battle with it

sources: Cath Maige Tuired §§84–85 (Stokes tr. 1891, PD, CELT T300010)

title: The Second Battle of Mag Tuired — Lugh's sling-stone through Balor's eye
summary: The Tuatha Dé Danann and the Fomorians meet on the plain of Mag Tuired. Restored to kingship, Nuada falls to Balor's destroying eye; Lugh Samildánach then slays his own grandfather Balor by driving a sling-stone clean through the venomous eye so its annihilating gaze falls back on the Fomorian host. Bres, the deposed half-Fomorian who raised the enemy, is captured and spared, and the Morrígan chants the closing prophecy — first peace to the sky, then the long unravelling of the world.
reenactment_hook: During a Large+ boss's telegraphed annihilation ultimate, land one perfectly-timed strike on the single briefly-exposed vulnerability (Balor's opening eye) in the only window available, redirecting or reflecting the attack onto the enemy formation to win the run.

epithet_grants.trigger: fell a Large+ boss with one window-strike on its exposed weakness during its ultimate wind-up, on a VIP table or higher

sources: Cath Maige Tuired (Stokes tr. 1891, PD, CELT T300010)

title: Lugh at the gate of Tara — master of every art
summary: Arriving at Tara, Lugh is turned away by the doorkeeper because every craft he names — wright, smith, champion, harper, warrior, poet, historian, sorcerer, physician, cupbearer, brazier — is already filled at the king's court. He then asks whether any single man holds ALL of them; none does, and he is admitted as Samildánach, master of every art. He proves it by winning every stake at the fidchell boards of Tara.
reenactment_hook: Clear a gatekeeper challenge not by force but by versatility — pass checks across many different skill categories in one attempt (the jack-of-all-trades key no single party member can supply alone), then win the board/game-of-skill sub-challenge for the stakes.

epithet_grants.trigger: satisfy every skill category a gate demands in one attempt and win the ensuing game-of-skill for the stakes

sources: Cath Maige Tuired §§53–74 (Stokes, PD); Fidchell/Lugh attribution in MacKillop, Dictionary of Celtic Mythology

title: The blood-price Lugh set the sons of Tuireann
summary: When the sons of Tuireann kill his father Cian, Lugh exacts an éraic of near-impossible treasures — a healing pig-skin, a poisoned spear, a chariot and steeds, seven pigs, the hound-whelp Failinis, a cooking-spit, and three shouts on a guarded hill. The brothers achieve every task but are mortally wounded on the last; Lugh refuses them the very healing pig-skin they themselves fetched, and they die with the price paid in full.
reenactment_hook: Take on a blood-debt penalty and discharge it by clearing a chain of near-impossible acquisition objectives without failing one, then deny a downed enemy the captured healing item that would save them to seal the victory.

epithet_grants.trigger: complete every objective in a multi-stage debt-quest without a failed task

sources: Oidheadh Chloinne Tuireann in P.W. Joyce, Old Celtic Romances (1879, PD)

title: Lugh founds the funeral games of Tailtiu
summary: Lugh's foster-mother Tailtiu clears the forests of Ireland into arable plain and dies of the labor. In her honor Lugh founds the Óenach Tailten — funeral games of racing, contest, and assembly — held at Lughnasadh on the first of August, a harvest festival that endured in Ireland into modern memory.
reenactment_hook: When an ally is eliminated, convert the loss into an audience event — stage commemorative funeral games / a memorial challenge that turns grief into crowd hype and a party buff, then win the round dedicated to the fallen.
sources: Metrical Dindshenchas 'Tailtiu' (Gwynn tr., PD); Lebor Gabála Érenn (Macalister, ITS)

title: Lugh heals his son the Hound with a three-day sleep
summary: Battered and wounded from the single-handed defense of the ford, Cú Chulainn is visited by Lugh, who reveals himself as the hero's true otherworld father. Lugh sings him into a three-day healing sleep and fights in his place while he recovers, so the Hound returns to the fight whole.
reenactment_hook: Deliberately bench your most-wounded champion for a full multi-round recovery cycle, holding the line with a stand-in, then return them at full strength to finish the run.

title: Brigid keens for Ruadán — the first lament in Ireland
summary: Brigid's son Ruadán, sent by the Fomorians to spy on and spear the smith Goibniu, is killed at the forge. Brigid comes and shrieks and weeps over him — and this cry is the first keening ever heard in Ireland, the origin of the funeral lament.
reenactment_hook: On an ally's on-camera elimination, perform a public keen — an ability that converts the death into a broadcast lament, turning audience hype-loss into a party buff (mourning staged as spectacle).

epithet_grants.trigger: perform the keen on an ally's on-camera elimination and complete the run

title: The three Brigids — poetry, healing, and smithcraft under one name
summary: Cormac's Glossary names Brigit, daughter of the Dagda, as a triple goddess: Brigit the poetess, honored by the filid; and her two sisters of the same name, Brigit the healer of leech-craft and Brigit the smith. Between them they hold poetry, healing, and smithcraft — the three sacred arts of Ireland gathered under one name, 'whose name among all Irishmen a goddess was called Brigit.'
reenactment_hook: In a single run, personally exercise all three of Brigid's arts with the same contestant — compose or perform to gain hype, heal or revive an ally, and forge or reforge an item at a station — the threefold mastery.

epithet_grants.trigger: in one run, personally perform an act of poetry (hype), healing, and smithcraft (forge)

sources: Sanas Cormaic / Cormac's Glossary (tr. O'Donovan/Stokes, PD)

title: The Dagda eats the Fomorians' porridge-trench
summary: Sent to the Fomorian camp under truce to buy time before the battle, the Dagda is mockingly served a king's ransom of porridge — eighty gallons of milk, whole sheep, goats and pigs — poured into a pit in the ground, on pain of death if he leaves any. He eats the entire trench-full with a ladle big enough for a man and woman to lie in, scrapes the gravel, then waddles off bloated — and the delay does exactly its work.
reenactment_hook: Accept an NPC's 'eat it all or die' gorging challenge — take on the exhausted / overstuffed condition for an outsized buff or to stall an enemy timer, and survive the debuff long enough to win the fight.

epithet_grants.trigger: clear a gorging challenge and win the ensuing fight while carrying the exhausted debuff

title: The Dagda's harp and the three strains
summary: After the battle the Dagda, Lugh and Ogma follow the fleeing Fomorians to recover the Dagda's harp Uaithne, hung on the wall of the enemy hall. The Dagda calls the harp by name; it flies to his hand, killing nine Fomorians in flight. Then he plays the three strains upon it — the strain of weeping, the strain of laughter, and the strain of sleep — and the whole host falls asleep, so the three escape.
reenactment_hook: Recover a stolen signature item from deep in enemy territory, then use a performance/tempo mechanic (the three strains) to control the entire enemy group — inflict fear (weeping), swing hype (laughter), and mass-sleep (sleep) — to clear or escape the room.

epithet_grants.trigger: recover a stolen signature item and clear a room using the three-strain performance control

sources: Cath Maige Tuired (Stokes, PD); Dagda material indexed in MacKillop

title: Manannán rides the sea that is to him a flowering plain
summary: As Bran mac Febail voyages westward, Manannán mac Lir meets him riding a chariot across the waves. The god declares that what Bran sees as a heaving sea is to him a flowering plain, Mag Mell — salmon are calves and lambs, orchards and chariots stand where Bran sees only water, and the Otherworld lies just beneath the surface. Bran sails on into a timeless country where a year passes as a day.
reenactment_hook: Reveal a hidden safe path across a lethal-looking hazard (a chasm or flood room) by reading its true nature with perception or lore — cross where others see only death, using insight rather than force.

epithet_grants.trigger: cross a lethal environmental hazard via a hidden true-path reveal instead of brute force

sources: Immram Brain / The Voyage of Bran (Meyer tr. 1895, PD)

title: Manannán's cloak of forgetfulness parts Fand and the Hound
summary: Cú Chulainn falls into a wasting sickness and enters a love-affair with Fand, wife of Manannán. When Manannán comes to reclaim her, Fand chooses to return to her husband; Manannán shakes his cloak of forgetfulness between Fand and Cú Chulainn so the two shall never meet again, and the hero is dosed with a draught of forgetting to end his grief.
reenactment_hook: Cleanly sever a costly attachment mid-run — give up a relationship-based buff or a bonded item (the cloak of forgetting) to purge a wasting / charm debuff, then finish the run unencumbered by it.

epithet_grants.trigger: sever a bonded buff to cure a wasting debuff and complete the run

sources: Serglige Con Culainn / The Sick-Bed of Cú Chulainn (Leahy, Heroic Romances of Ireland 1905, PD)

title: The Crane Bag of the Fianna
summary: Manannán's Crane Bag is made from the skin of Aoife, a woman transformed into a crane and kept until her death; it holds his treasures — shirt, knife, shears, helmet, and a strip of whale-bone — and is full at high tide, empty at the ebb. The bag passes down a line of keepers to Cumhall and at last to his son Fionn, holding the treasures of the Fianna.
reenactment_hook: Acquire and carry a legendary container whose contents are accessible only under a specific condition (a 'high tide' window) — time your access to the store so its gear is available exactly when the decisive fight lands.

epithet_grants.trigger: hold the conditional store and unlock its loadout at the decisive moment to win the run

sources: Duanaire Finn (ed. MacNeill 1908); storyarchaeology.com 'The Treasure Bag of the Fianna'

title: Manannán, first ruler of Man, and the rent of green rushes
summary: In Manx tradition Manannán is the first ruler of the Isle of Man, wrapping the island in mist to hide it from invaders. He takes as his only tax a bundle of green rushes brought to him at South Barrule each Midsummer Eve — the custom of 'paying the rent,' still enacted in Manx memory.
reenactment_hook: Hide your party or an objective from an incoming threat by raising concealment (fog-of-war / mist) over a zone to skip an encounter, then collect a small toll or tribute for safe passage — defending by hiding, not fighting.

epithet_grants.trigger: avoid a threatening encounter via a concealment mechanic and complete the zone without engaging

sources: 'Supposed True Chronicle of Man' (16th c.) + Manx Traditionary Ballad

title: Nuada Airgetlám — the king unmade and remade by a lost arm
summary: At the First Battle of Mag Tuired, Nuada loses his sword-arm to the Fir Bolg champion Sreng. Because the law of kingship bars a blemished man from the throne, Nuada must abdicate to Bres. The physician Dian Cécht and the wright Creidhne fashion him a working arm of silver — Nuada of the Silver Hand — and in time his own flesh regrows over it, so he reclaims the kingship the wound cost him.
reenactment_hook: Fight on and win an engagement while a limb or body part is permanently disabled (a body part held at 0 HP in the per-limb sim), then restore or replace it with a crafted prosthetic to reclaim your standing.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a fight with a body part disabled at 0 HP, then restore or replace it

title: The first satire ever spoken unmakes Bres
summary: King Bres, whose stinginess starves the Tuatha Dé, lodges the poet Cairbre in a cramped dark hut with no fire, no bed, and three dry cakes. In revenge Cairbre speaks the first satire ever made in Ireland — a verse cursing the king's hospitality. From that hour Bres declines: red blisters bloom on the face of the most beautiful of kings, and a blemished king cannot reign. Words alone depose him.
reenactment_hook: Take down a foe by performance rather than damage — deliver an on-camera satire or taunt that applies a stacking 'blemish'/shame debuff (blisters), collapsing a status-dependent boss's defenses and turning the audience against it until it is undone.

title: Bres bargains his existence back from the table in four bids
summary: Captured unprotected at the end of the Second Battle, the deposed Bres bargains for his life. He offers that the cows of Ireland will always give milk — refused; that there will be a harvest every quarter — refused; until at last he buys his survival with pure agronomy, telling the Tuatha Dé the right days to plough, sow, and reap. A god who talked his existence back from the table in four bids.
reenactment_hook: When caught dead-to-rights and about to be eliminated, buy your survival through escalating offers — negotiate down from a losing position by trading successive resources or pieces of information until the captor accepts, then survive the run on the deal.

epithet_grants.trigger: survive a would-be elimination by winning a multi-offer bargain with your captor

sources: Cath Maige Tuired, closing sections (Stokes, PD; Gray 1982 as academic check)

title: The Cattle Raid of Cooley — one boy holds the ford against an army
summary: When Queen Medb of Connacht invades Ulster to seize the great bull Donn Cúailnge, the men of Ulster lie helpless under a debilitating curse. Seventeen-year-old Cú Chulainn alone defends the province, invoking the right of single combat at the fords to hold Medb's whole army for months — killing a champion a day until Ulster wakes.
reenactment_hook: Solo-hold a chokepoint (a ford or corridor) against an overwhelming force by serial single combat — bottleneck the enemy so only one engages at a time, and survive round after round alone while the rest of the party is disabled or out of the fight.

epithet_grants.trigger: solo-hold a chokepoint against a full enemy wave by serial single combat while the party is disabled

title: Cú Chulainn ties himself to the stone to die on his feet
summary: Trapped between two geasa — never refuse hospitality, never eat dog-flesh — Cú Chulainn is tricked by three hags roasting a hound into eating it, and his strength ebbs. Mortally wounded in his last battle, he binds himself upright to a standing stone so he may die on his feet, sword in hand. For three days no enemy dares approach, until a scald-crow — the Morrígan in her death-aspect — settles on his shoulder, and only then do they know the Hound is dead.
reenactment_hook: At near-death, lash yourself to a fixed point and hold a defiant standing posture that denies enemies the kill-confirm — keep a zone held by presence alone past lethal damage until the round's victory trigger resolves.

epithet_grants.trigger: complete a last-stand that denies the enemy a kill-confirm at lethal HP until the round resolves in your favor

sources: Aided Con Culainn / Cú Chulainn's Death (Stokes tr., Revue Celtique 3, 1877, PD)

title: Fionn burns his thumb on the Salmon of Knowledge
summary: The poet Finnéces catches the Salmon of Knowledge after seven years' watching and sets the boy Fionn to cook it, forbidding him to eat any. Fionn burns his thumb on the cooking fish, sucks it to ease the pain, and so tastes the salmon first — taking for himself all the wisdom of the world. Ever after he need only put his thumb to his mouth to know any hidden thing.
reenactment_hook: In a 'cook it but don't taste it' custody event, seize the forbidden first taste — accept a small burn penalty to unlock a permanent oracle ability (query one hidden fact, e.g. a boss's weakness), then win the run using what it reveals.

epithet_grants.trigger: take the forbidden taste and win the run using the hidden knowledge it grants

sources: Macgnímartha Finn / The Boyish Exploits of Finn (Meyer tr., PD)

title: Fionn lets the healing water run through his fingers
summary: Gráinne, betrothed to the aging Fionn, lays a geas on the young warrior Diarmuid to elope with her, and Fionn pursues the couple across Ireland for years.
