When Diarmuid is at last gored by the enchanted boar of Beann Ghulban, Fionn — whose cupped hands of well-water can heal any wound — twice carries the water and twice lets it slip through his fingers, until Diarmuid dies. Old jealousy kills where a god's gift could have saved.
reenactment_hook: Deliberately let a heal or revive you are able to give a downed rival lapse — decline the revive window as a cold choice to eliminate the rival cleanly, and win the run on the advantage it buys.

epithet_grants.trigger: deliberately withhold a revive from a downed rival to secure the run's victory

sources: Tóraigheacht Dhiarmada agus Ghráinne in P.W. Joyce, Old Celtic Romances (1879, PD)

title: Lleu's impossible death and his singing back to life
summary: Cursed by his mother Arianrhod to have no name, no arms, and no human wife, Lleu is given all three through Gwydion's tricks — including Blodeuwedd, a wife conjured from oak, broom, and meadowsweet. Blodeuwedd and her lover Gronw contrive his death by an impossible loophole: neither indoors nor out, neither afoot nor mounted, by a spear a year in the forging. Struck in that exact pose, Lleu becomes an eagle and rots in an oak until Gwydion finds him and sings him back to human form with three verses; Lleu then repays Gronw with a cast through the very stone Gronw hides behind.
reenactment_hook: Defeat a foe immune under all ordinary conditions by assembling the exact stacked loophole its immunity leaves open — a precise position, timing, and equipment combination — and landing the one strike its own rules permit; and, if felled, be sung back by an ally's scripted revival sequence.

epithet_grants.epithet: The Fair-Haired One of the Skillful Hand
epithet_grants.trigger: kill an otherwise-immune enemy by satisfying its full stacked-condition vulnerability window in one strike

sources: The Mabinogion, Fourth Branch / Math fab Mathonwy (Guest tr., PD)

title: The hunt of Twrch Trwyth — shear the boar that cannot be killed
summary: To win Olwen, Culhwch must fetch the comb, razor, and shears lodged between the ears of Twrch Trwyth, a king transformed into a monstrous boar for his sins. Arthur's whole warband hunts the boar and its seven piglets from Ireland across Wales; the razor and shears are snatched at the Severn mouth, the comb only in Cornwall, and the boar — never slain — is at last driven into the sea. His piglets die one by one defending him.
reenactment_hook: Beat an unkillable boss by objective rather than damage — strip three specific items off it in timed snatches while it rampages, and drive it off the map, never landing a kill.
sources: Culhwch and Olwen (Guest tr., PD; verified via EBSCO Research Starters)

title: The Field of Boliauns — the leprechaun's letter-exact trick
summary: Tom Fitzpatrick catches a cluricaune and forces it to reveal where its gold is buried — under a single ragwort (boliaun) in a great field. Having no spade, Tom ties his red garter to the plant and makes the fairy swear not to remove it, then runs home for a shovel. When he returns, every ragwort in the forty-acre field wears an identical red garter — the promise kept to the letter, the gold lost forever.
reenactment_hook: Win a wager by exploiting its exact wording against its intent — honor a promise to the letter while defeating its spirit (mark every option identically so the 'marked' answer becomes unfindable); or from the captor's side, hold the trickster's gaze and never look away for the single frame that forfeits the pot.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a letter-exact wager by the loophole, or hold the gaze without looking away to claim the crock

sources: T. Crofton Croker, Fairy Legends and Traditions of the South of Ireland (1825, PD; Gutenberg #39752), 'The Field of Boliauns'

title: The Bunworth Banshee keens at the window
summary: The night before the Reverend Charles Bunworth of Buttevant dies, a bean sí keens outside the house — clapping her hands and crying his name at the window through the dark, heard by all the household though nothing violent occurs. The banshee harms no one; she only announces, keening for the old family before the death is sealed.
reenactment_hook: Publicly herald an incoming elimination one round before it locks in — perform a keen that broadcasts which contestant or enemy is doomed next, converting foreknowledge into audience drama and a positioning edge, without striking a blow.

epithet_grants.trigger: correctly herald the next elimination one round before it resolves

sources: T. Crofton Croker, Fairy Legends and Traditions (1825, PD; the Bunworth Banshee, Co. Cork, 1770)

title: The Great Race that fixed the zodiac order
summary: To assign the twelve years of the calendar, the Jade Emperor holds a race across a great river, the first twelve animals to cross taking the qualifying slots. The Rat, no swimmer, rides across on the diligent Ox's head and leaps off at the bank to finish first, cheating a stronger racer of the top spot. Cunning, not speed, wins the crown of the zodiac.
reenactment_hook: Win a timed qualifying race or floor-rush against rival contestants not by having the highest movement stat but by mounting or tethering to a faster contestant's momentum and detaching at the line to log first place.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a qualifying race by riding another contestant's momentum to first place

sources: Chinese zodiac Great Race folk narrative (chinesenewyear.net zodiac-origin; Wikipedia 'Chinese zodiac', verified)

title: Havoc in Heaven — the Monkey King besieges the celestial court
summary: Snubbed by the celestial bureaucracy and titled only a stable-hand, Sun Wukong revolts, proclaims himself Great Sage Equal to Heaven, and routs every host the Jade Emperor sends against him. His rampage empties Heaven's armies and besieges the throne room until the court is forced to petition the Buddha. It is the greatest solo assault on divine authority in the corpus.
reenactment_hook: Single-handedly clear every enforcer wave the arena authority throws at you on a VVIP floor — rout the celestial guard, empty the boss's summon pool, and be the last one standing in the throne room.

epithet_grants.trigger: rout every celestial-enforcer wave and hold the throne room solo on a VVIP floor

sources: Journey to the West chs. 4–7 (Wu Cheng'en, 1592; PD)

title: Zhao Gongming is slain by the straw effigy
summary: In the Investiture of the Gods, the marshal Zhao Gongming rides his black tiger into the war on the losing Shang side, dominating the Zhou immortals with his iron whip and Sea-Quelling Pearls. Unable to beat him in the field, Jiang Ziya builds a straw effigy, burns incantations over it for twenty-one days, and shoots peachwood arrows through its eyes and heart — the Nail-Head Seven Arrows rite — killing Zhao from afar. He is deified after death as Lord of the Dark Altar, the God of Wealth.
reenactment_hook: Commit fully to backing a doomed faction or objective the odds say will lose, wield a signature weapon to beat several higher-tier foes, and fight on until you are unmade — then be re-staked (resurrected) by a patron as refinanced debt.

epithet_grants.trigger: fall backing a doomed objective after defeating three higher-tier foes, then return to the table re-staked

sources: Fengshen Yanyi chs. 46–51 (Ming); Wikipedia 'Zhao Gongming', verified one level down

title: Guardian of the Celestial Master's elixir furnace
summary: In the older Taoist tradition, before he was a wealth god Zhao Gongming was a marshal of plague and the underworld. The Heavenly Emperor dispatched him to Mount Longhu to guard Zhang Daoling's alchemical furnace while the Celestial Master refined the elixir of immortality. For his faithful watch he was given the finished elixir, gained the power to change shape at will, and was raised into a guardian marshal — the office that later became the God of Wealth.
reenactment_hook: Guard a single high-value objective — a furnace, vault, or relic — through the whole run without abandoning your post, and claim at the end the treasure you protected plus the power it confers.

epithet_grants.trigger: guard a high-value objective through the whole run without abandoning it and claim its reward

sources: Gan Bao, In Search of the Supernatural (Jin); Yuan–Ming Taoist Longhu-shan furnace-guardian tradition (Wikipedia 'Zhao Gongming'; daoinfo.org 'The Spirit of Wealth', verified)

title: The honest official who became the Earth God
summary: Zhang Fude, a scrupulously honest and gentle minor official of the Zhou era, served the common people faithfully all his long life and died at a great age. A poor man raised him a tiny shrine of three stones — one roof, two walls — and prospered so unexpectedly that the village pooled money to build him a proper temple. His lifelong virtue in a small office earned him the ubiquitous godhood of the local land.
reenactment_hook: Complete an entire floor with a perfect conduct record — share supplies, guard every non-combatant NPC, take no bribe, cheat no one — and receive the quiet windfall reserved for the scrupulously honest.

epithet_grants.epithet: Correct God of Blessing and Virtue
epithet_grants.trigger: clear a full floor with a perfect honesty-and-generosity record

sources: Taipei Times 'The god that guards the earth' (2023); taiwanese-gods.org / Guandu Temple Fude-god legend, verified

title: The servant who froze to shield his master's daughter
summary: In a competing origin legend, the loyal servant Zhang (Ming-de / Fude) escorts his master's daughter across country to reach her father. Caught in a killing snowstorm, he strips off his own clothes to wrap and shield the girl and freezes to death protecting her. Nine words naming him as a true spirit appear in the sky; the touched emperor honors him with a chancellor's cap, and he becomes the Earth God — deified for a single act of self-sacrifice rather than a lifetime of office.
reenactment_hook: Permanently give up your own armor or defensive item to a weaker teammate or non-combatant NPC exposed to an environmental hazard, endure the damage yourself, and bring them through alive.

epithet_grants.trigger: sacrifice your own armor to shield a weaker NPC through a hazard and bring them out alive

sources: Guandu Temple 'Fude God' legend (kuantu.org.tw); Wikipedia 'Tudigong' snow-sacrifice servant variant, verified

title: King Mu feasts with the Queen Mother at the Jade Pool
summary: King Mu of Zhou drives his eight fabulous steeds to the far west and is received by Xiwangmu at the Jade Pool on Mount Kunlun. Host and king feast and exchange composed songs, she toasts him and he pledges to return when his realm is at peace. It is the courtly, refined face of the Queen Mother — the guest list, the poem-duet, and the invitation that is her highest favor.
reenactment_hook: Reach the map's most exclusive, highest-point location and win entry to a gated banquet through a courtly or performance challenge rather than by force; leave holding the invitation token.

epithet_grants.trigger: earn a gated banquet invitation at the map's highest point without resorting to force

sources: Mu Tianzi Zhuan (Tale of King Mu, Son of Heaven); chinaknowledge.de 'Mu Tianzi zhuan', verified

title: The Monkey King wrecks the Peach Banquet
summary: Left off the guest list for Xiwangmu's Peach Banquet, Sun Wukong slips into the deserted feast hall, gorges on the immortal peaches, drains the wine, and blunders into Laozi's chamber to swallow the elixir pills as well. Drunk and immortal beyond killing, he flees back to his mountain — the theft that turns Heaven's grievance against him permanent.
reenactment_hook: Break into an event or vault you were locked out of and consume or seize its rarest reserved consumables before the owner returns — a heist scored on what you take, not who you kill.

epithet_grants.trigger: seize an exclusive event's rarest reserved consumables and escape before the owner returns

title: Nüwa mends the broken sky
summary: When the pillar of heaven was shattered and the sky cracked, fire raged unquenched and floodwaters would not recede. Nüwa smelts stones of five colors to patch the torn sky, cuts the legs from a giant tortoise to prop up the four cardinal poles, and dams the black flood with the ashes of burnt reeds. Her repair restores the ordered cosmos.
reenactment_hook: Repair a collapsing arena structure — a bridge, pillar, or seal — under active bombardment, restoring it before the hazard spreads, and finish the floor standing on the mended ground.

epithet_grants.trigger: repair a collapsing arena structure under bombardment and finish the floor on the mended ground, on a VIP table or higher

sources: Huainanzi ch. 6 (Lanming, c. 139 BCE); verified via Wikipedia 'Nüwa' index one level down

title: Nüwa molds humanity from clay
summary: Lonely in the new world, the serpent-bodied Nüwa kneels by the yellow river and shapes the first humans one by one from clay. Tiring of the slow work, she drags a knotted cord through the mud and flings the droplets wide, and where each falls a person springs up — which is why (the myth says) some are finely made and some rough. She is the maker and matchmaker of the human race.
reenactment_hook: Build a party from nothing mid-run — recruit and equip fresh allies or summons from raw materials found on the floor — and clear the floor with a team you created rather than one you found.

epithet_grants.trigger: clear a floor with a party you built and equipped from scratch mid-run

sources: Han clay-molding tradition (Fengsu Tongyi; Chuci allusion); verified via Wikipedia 'Nüwa' / newworldencyclopedia.org

title: Nüwa sends the fox spirit against King Zhou
summary: Visiting Nüwa's temple, the tyrant King Zhou of Shang scrawls a lewd poem lusting after the goddess on her wall. Offended, Nüwa does not smite him herself — she summons the nine-tailed fox spirit and sends it, in the guise of the beauty Daji, into his palace to bewitch him and rot the dynasty from within. Her vengeance is delegated, patient, and total: she staffs a saboteur rather than striking.
reenactment_hook: Rather than confront a blasphemer or boss directly, plant a recruited saboteur inside its ranks to corrupt its forces from within, then take the weakened target.

epithet_grants.trigger: topple a boss using a planted saboteur rather than a direct assault

sources: Fengshen Yanyi ch. 1 (Ming); verified via Wikipedia 'Nüwa'/FSYY summaries

title: The Monkey King strikes his name from the ledger of death
summary: Dragged before the Ten Kings when his mortal span ends, Sun Wukong refuses the sentence, seizes the register of life and death by force, and crosses out every monkey name — his own above all — before storming back to the living world. He audits the underworld's own accounts and voids his death, an outrage the courts never forgive.
reenactment_hook: Permanently strike a death-penalty or 'marked-for-death' condition from your own run record by seizing and editing the arena's death ledger, then finish the run using the fate you erased.

epithet_grants.trigger: permanently erase a death-penalty from your own run record and finish the run

sources: Journey to the West ch. 3 (PD); journeytothewestresearch.com 'Monkey and the Summoners of Hell'

title: King Yama demoted for mercy
summary: Yanluo Wang once presided over the First Court of the underworld, the gate every soul first passes. Moved to pity by those who had died unjustly or with good works unfinished, he kept sending them back to the living world to set things right — so many that Heaven judged him too lenient. The Jade Emperor demoted him to the harsh Fifth Court, and the story becomes the etiology of the Fifth Court's severity: a magistrate punished for compassion.
reenactment_hook: Spare a condemned or marked target you were ordered and rewarded to execute — accept the scored penalty to your own standing — and still clear the run on that act of mercy.

epithet_grants.trigger: spare a marked target at a scored cost to your standing and still clear the run

sources: Yuli Baochao (Jade Record) Ten Kings tradition; Britannica 'Yanluo Wang' (demotion for leniency), verified

title: Zhong Kui devours the demon in the Emperor's dream
summary: Feverish, Emperor Xuanzong dreams of a small imp stealing his treasures, then of a huge ragged scholar-ghost who seizes and eats the imp. The ghost names himself Zhong Kui: an examinee who, cheated of first place because of his ugliness, dashed his head on the palace steps in protest and vowed in death to rid the empire of evils. The Emperor wakes healed and has Wu Daozi paint the dream — the apotheosis of the demon-queller.
reenactment_hook: Turn a personal defeat — a failed check or public humiliation — into power: hunt down and finish the hidden cheater or demon stalking the floor, exposing what the house pretended not to see.

epithet_grants.trigger: after a failed check, hunt and kill the hidden demon or cheater on the floor

sources: Shen Kuo, Bu Mengxi Bitan (1031–1095); chinaknowledge.de 'Zhong Kui', verified

title: Zhong Kui marries off his sister
summary: Grateful to his friend Du Ping, who gave his corpse a proper burial, the ghost Zhong Kui returns on New Year's Eve with his tamed demon retinue to marry his sister to Du Ping. The demons bear the sedan and torches in a night-parade wedding procession, and Zhong Kui gives his demon-slaying sword as the dowry — gratitude and a debt honored, staged as a ghost festival.
reenactment_hook: Repay a debt of honor to the ally who helped you by escorting their vulnerable charge through a night-parade gauntlet, staffing the escort with enemies you have tamed or recruited.

epithet_grants.trigger: escort an ally's charge through a night gauntlet using tamed enemies

sources: 'Zhong Kui marries off his sister' folk/opera cycle; chinaknowledge.de + Sotheby's 'Return of Zhong Kui' survey, verified

title: Nezha slays the dragon prince Ao Bing
summary: Bathing in the sea, the child Nezha shakes the Dragon King's palace with his Red Armillary Sash. Ao Bing, third son of the East Sea Dragon King, storms up to punish him; Nezha kills the prince in single combat and draws out his dragon-sinew tendon as a trophy. A playground quarrel becomes a killing that will bring the four seas' vengeance down on his family.
reenactment_hook: Provoke and defeat a boss's higher-tier kin in single combat that you started, then claim a trophy from the body (the drawn tendon).

epithet_grants.trigger: defeat a boss's higher-tier kin in single combat and claim a trophy from the body

sources: Fengshen Yanyi chs. 12–14 (Ming); verified via Wikipedia 'Nezha'

title: Nezha returns his flesh and is reborn from lotus
summary: To spare his parents the Dragon Kings' retribution for Ao Bing's death, Nezha takes his own life — carving off his flesh to return to his mother and his bones to his father, settling the god's grievance with his whole body. His master Taiyi Zhenren gathers his spirit and rebuilds him from lotus root and leaf, giving him a new deathless body, the fire-tipped spear, and the wind-fire wheels. He is unmade by debt and remade stronger.
reenactment_hook: Permanently sacrifice a core stat or a body part to erase a debt or save your party, then accept a full-body rebuild that returns you to the run with new signature gear.

epithet_grants.trigger: sacrifice a core stat to clear a debt or save the party, then complete the run after a full rebuild

sources: Fengshen Yanyi chs. 12–14 (Ming); chinese-mythology.com FSYY summaries, verified

title: Xingtian dances on, headless
summary: Xingtian contended with the Supreme God for sovereignty and lost; the god struck off his head and buried it at Changyang Mountain. Refusing to yield even in defeat, Xingtian grew eyes from his nipples and a mouth from his navel and rose again, and to this day he brandishes his axe and shield in an eternal war-dance. As Tao Qian wrote, 'his fierce will endures forever' — the purest myth of never-surrender in the corpus.
reenactment_hook: Keep fighting after a body part is disabled or a limb is lost — refuse every surrender and ransom offer — and land the killing blow while still maimed.

epithet_grants.trigger: land a killing blow while maimed, having refused every surrender offer in the fight

epithet_grants.epithet: He Who Was Punished by Heaven
epithet_grants.trigger: win three consecutive fights while under a disabled body part

sources: Shanhaijing (Classic of Overseas West); Tao Qian 'Reading the Shanhaijing'; verified via Wikipedia 'Xingtian'

title: The wager on the Buddha's palm
summary: To end the havoc, the Buddha wagers Sun Wukong the throne of Heaven itself: if the Monkey King can somersault out of the Buddha's palm, he wins. Wukong leaps a hundred and eight thousand li to five pink pillars at the edge of the world, scrawls graffiti and urinates at their base to prove he was there, and returns boasting — only to learn the pillars were the Buddha's fingers. The hand turns over and pins him under the Five Elements Mountain for five hundred years: the house legend of the all-in that was lost.
reenactment_hook: Stake your entire run position on a single decisive gamble against the house's strongest — one all-or-nothing leap — winning the throne outright, or losing everything and going under the mountain.

epithet_grants.trigger: win an all-or-nothing single-gamble against the house's strongest opponent

sources: Journey to the West ch. 7 (PD); journeytothewestresearch.com 'Buddha palm' / Five-Elements-Mountain

title: Three strikes against the White Bone Demon
summary: The cadaver-spirit Baigujing tries three times to snatch the monk Tang Sanzang, appearing first as a village girl bearing food, then as her searching mother, then as her frail old father. Each time Sun Wukong's fiery golden eyes see the skeleton beneath the disguise and he strikes it down — but the monk, seeing only slain innocents, curses and banishes him. The deed is discernment: to see the true bones before the blow, at the price of being disbelieved.
reenactment_hook: Expose a disguised enemy's true skeleton with true-sight before you strike; kill an unexposed disguise instead and the broadcast reads it as murdering an innocent, crashing your audience score and turning the party against you.

epithet_grants.trigger: expose and slay a three-phase disguised boss without ever striking an innocent form

title: Houyi shoots down the nine suns
summary: When ten suns — the sons of Di Jun — rose into the sky together, their heat scorched the crops and boiled the seas and the world began to die. The archer Houyi drew his vermilion bow and shot down nine of the ten three-legged sun-crows one by one, leaving a single sun to warm the earth. For killing the god's sons he was later stripped of his immortality: the champion punished for saving everyone.
reenactment_hook: Down all-but-one of a set of overhead targets with precision fire, discovering the hidden mercy rule that exactly one must be left alive; killing the last target fails the floor.

epithet_grants.trigger: down all-but-one of the overhead targets, discovering and honoring the mercy rule, on a VIP table or higher
complete the nine-suns precision trial without a single missed shot

title: Chang'e takes the elixir and flees to the moon
summary: Houyi, mortal again, obtained from Xiwangmu a single elixir of immortality — enough for one to ascend, or two to live long. His wife Chang'e drinks the whole draught herself and rises weightless out of the world, coming to rest on the cold moon, where she remains alone. The theft of the shared prize becomes the etiology of the moon and of the Mid-Autumn parting.
reenactment_hook: Consume a single shared or reserved immortality prize entirely for yourself, ascending out of the run to safety and leaving the rest of the party behind.

epithet_grants.trigger: consume a shared immortality prize alone and ascend out of the run

sources: Huainanzi (Chang'e flight); verified via chinaknowledge.de / chinahighlights 'Chang'e and Houyi'

title: The beast whose wealth can never come back out
summary: The Pixiu, a winged lion-dragon, gluttonously devoured gold and silver and then broke a law of Heaven. As punishment the Jade Emperor sealed its hindquarters shut, so that wealth may go in but never come out. Doomed to swallow treasure it can never expel, the Pixiu became the talisman gamblers rub for luck: an appetite that only ever accumulates.
reenactment_hook: Devour and hoard heavy valuables you can never spend — build a locked treasury and try to win by sheer intake; the contestant too greedy to release any of it bloats immobile and cannot win.

epithet_grants.trigger: amass a locked treasury and win a floor by intake alone

sources: Wikipedia 'Pixiu' verified against feng-shui reference compilations one level down

title: The two constables who died keeping an appointment
summary: The constables Xie Bi'an (tall, white) and Fan Wujiu (short, dark) were separated while escorting a prisoner and agreed to meet again under a bridge. A sudden flood rose, but Fan drowned at the spot rather than abandon the appointed place; Xie, arriving to find him dead, hanged himself from the bridge in grief. Moved by a faithfulness kept unto death, the Jade Emperor made them the Black and White Impermanence, escorts of the dead.
reenactment_hook: Hold a fixed position or appointed spot through a lethal hazard rather than abandon it — stand your ground in the rising flood — and let the timer, not your feet, decide the outcome.

epithet_grants.trigger: hold a fixed post through a lethal hazard without ever retreating from it

sources: journeytothewestresearch.com 'Monkey and the Summoners of Hell'; utkasianreligions.org 'Heibai Wuchang', verified

title: The broth of forgetfulness at the Bridge of Helplessness
summary: At the Naihe Bridge, the last stop before rebirth, the old woman Meng Po ladles out a five-flavored broth to every soul that has ever lived. One bowl erases the whole of a life's memories — its loves, grievances, and lessons — so that each soul returns to the world clean and remembering nothing. Her station is the universal hinge of reincarnation: the forgetting that makes the wheel turn.
reenactment_hook: Permanently drink away a learned advantage — erase your own memory of a boss's attack pattern, or spend a permanent knowledge asset — to earn a clean reset that the house rewards.

epithet_grants.epithet: Lady of the Bridge of Helplessness
epithet_grants.trigger: permanently erase a learned boss-pattern advantage for a rewarded reset, on a VIP table or higher

sources: Yuli Baochao (Jade Record, Qing); verified via Wikipedia 'Meng Po' / The China Project profile

title: Thoth wins the moon's light at draughts
summary: Playing draughts against the Moon, Thoth won a seventieth part of each of her days' light, and out of the winnings fashioned five extra 'epagomenal' days set outside the ordinary 360-day year. On those loophole-days the sky goddess Nut bore the children forbidden to be born on any day of the year — Osiris, Horus the Elder, Set, Isis, and Nephthys. The Moon, having gambled away a share of her light, has waned and waxed ever since. (Plutarch's primary text names Hermes and Selene; 'Thoth beat the Egyptian moon-god Khonsu' is the standard modern gloss of the Egyptian roles.)
reenactment_hook: Stake earned resources (hype) in a high-odds table side-game against the house, win, and convert the pot into extra turns ('days') for the run — then clear the floor using the banked action economy you gambled for.

epithet_grants.epithet: Reckoner of Times and of Seasons
epithet_grants.trigger: win a staked side-game to bank extra turns, then clear the floor using them, on a VIP table or higher

title: The eighty-year tribunal for the throne
summary: For eighty years Horus and Set litigated before the divine tribunal for Osiris's vacant throne, Thoth recording and refereeing while the Ennead deadlocked. Petitioned by letter, the goddess Neith ruled for Horus; the rivals settled nothing and turned to contests — a breath-holding duel as hippopotamuses and a stone-boat race that Set lost when Horus's gypsum-painted pinewood boat floated and Set's true stone boat sank. In the end Horus was awarded the throne, and Set was given the storm and the desert.
reenactment_hook: Win a drawn-out contested objective by trickery and attrition rather than force — outlast a rival across every round and beat them at the decisive moment with a disguised object or rules exploit (the false-stone-boat gambit), conceding no round and landing no cheap kill.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a multi-round contested objective by a decisive trick, conceding no round, on a VIP table or higher

sources: Papyrus Chester Beatty I (The Contendings of Horus and Seth)

title: Khonsu's statue heals the princess of Bakhtan
summary: When Princess Bentresh of far-off Bakhtan fell ill, possessed by a spirit no physician could drive out, Pharaoh sent the healing statue of Khonsu-the-Provider across the world. At the god's approach the possessing spirit confessed its fault and fled, and the princess was cured. The prince of Bakhtan tried to keep the statue, but Khonsu appeared to him in a dream and demanded to be sent home, and was returned to Thebes with rich offerings.
reenactment_hook: Travel to reach an afflicted teammate and purge a condition ordinary means can't remove — cleanse an unremovable status (a boss-inflicted possession/charm/curse) off an ally, then depart rather than linger, and win the run having healed rather than dealt the decisive blow.

epithet_grants.trigger: purge an otherwise-unremovable condition from a teammate and end the run with that teammate alive

title: Khonsu the butcher of the lords
summary: In the oldest layer of his myth, Khonsu the Moon is the divine butcher of the Cannibal Hymn: at the dead king's command he slays the lords, cuts their throats, and takes out what is in their bellies, so the king may devour the gods and swallow their powers and magic. The one who becomes what he eats rises supreme over the cosmos. This berserk slaughterer is the face beneath his later mask of the gentle healer.
reenactment_hook: Kill powerful enemies and 'consume' them to absorb their strength — land killing blows on multiple elite/named foes and claim a permanent stacking buff or a signature ability from each, snowballing raw power across the run and winning with the accumulated might.

epithet_grants.trigger: kill three or more elite enemies in one run and win using powers absorbed from them

title: Set's rigged chest kills Osiris
summary: Set secretly measured a beautiful chest to Osiris's exact body, then at a banquet promised it to whoever fit inside. When Osiris lay down in it, Set and seventy-two conspirators slammed the lid, sealed it with molten lead, and cast it into the Nile — a murder committed as a rigged party game. Osiris drifted away dead and Set seized the throne, until Isis recovered the body and the long revenge began. It is the first crooked table in recorded myth.
reenactment_hook: Win by luring a target into a baited trap rigged in advance — pre-place a hazard or container fitted to a specific enemy (a mimic-sarcophagus or terrain kill-box), bait it in on camera, and spring it for a kill in which you deal no direct damage yourself.

epithet_grants.trigger: kill a boss or elite with a pre-placed trap you baited it into, dealing no direct damage yourself

title: The nightly unmaking of Apep
summary: Each night, as the sun-barque sails through the underworld, the vast serpent Apep — chaos incarnate, never worshipped, only cursed — ambushes it to swallow the sun and end the world. Set stands at the prow and spears him, and the gods bind, knife, and burn the serpent, so that by dawn he is destroyed. Yet he cannot truly die: he reforms and attacks again the next night, and so the ritual of overthrowing him must be performed forever.
reenactment_hook: Defeat an enemy that cannot be killed by damage by performing the correct ritual sequence — survive its assault, then discover and execute the ordered execration steps (spear, bind, cut, burn) within phase windows while protecting an escort ('barque') objective until dawn, rather than trying to out-damage a foe that reforms nightly.

epithet_grants.trigger: defeat a reform-immune boss by completing its ritual execration sequence in order while keeping an escort objective alive until dawn, on a VIP table or higher

sources: Book of Overthrowing Apep (P. Bremner-Rhind, BM EA 10188); Amduat

title: The dismemberment and rising of Osiris
summary: After Set murdered Osiris and scattered his body into fourteen pieces across Egypt, Isis and Nephthys gathered the parts and Anubis performed the first embalming, binding Osiris back into wholeness as the first mummy. Revived just long enough to conceive Horus, Osiris did not return to the land of the living but descended to become First of the Dead — king and judge of the underworld. The god who was unmade came back holding a greater office than the one he had lost.
reenactment_hook: Come back from a downed state and win after reassembling something scattered — be revived from a knockout at least once, collect every piece of a split objective (a body or relic broken into parts across the floor), and finish the run standing with the highest score.

epithet_grants.trigger: return from a downed state, reassemble every piece of a split objective, and finish the run standing, on a VIP table or higher
recover the full split objective with no piece lost in the same run

sources: Plutarch, De Iside et Osiride §§13-19; Pyramid Texts resurrection complex (Faulkner)

title: Atum unmakes the world
summary: In Book of the Dead 175, the creator Atum declares that one day he will destroy everything he has made and let the world sink back into Nun, the primeval flood from which it rose. When that end comes, only two beings will remain, both in the form of serpents: Atum himself and Osiris, lord of the dead — the origin and the ledger, the last two survivors of the wager that was all of creation.
reenactment_hook: Stake everything and win as the last one standing at the very end — go all-in on a Forsaken table (wager the whole run's winnings or your continued participation), lose every teammate and asset but yourself, and still clear 100% of the floor's objectives and extract as the sole survivor.

epithet_grants.trigger: go all-in on a Forsaken table, survive as the last one standing, and clear 100% of objectives
extract as sole survivor with every objective complete on a VVIP/Forsaken table

title: The weighing of the heart against the feather
summary: At the end of every soul's journey it comes to the Hall of Two Truths, where Anubis sets its heart on the great balance against the feather of Ma'at while Thoth records the verdict and Osiris presides; Shai and Renenutet stand by as fate and fortune. A heart lighter than or equal to the feather passes on to the fields of the blessed; a heart weighed down by sin is thrown to Ammit the Devourer and suffers the second death — final erasure. First the dead must recite the forty-two declarations of innocence before the assessor gods.
reenactment_hook: Pass a run-end audit with a clean ledger — accrue zero taboo/sin-weight across the run, report every kill and loot count honestly at the weigh-in, and survive a judgment event where a heavier ledger is 'devoured' (eliminated). Win by integrity, not force.

epithet_grants.trigger: reach the run-end weighing with zero falsified declarations and a sin-weight lighter than the feather
pass the weighing on a VIP table or higher with a perfectly honest manifest

title: The tale of the two brothers
summary: Bata, a virtuous herdsman falsely accused by his elder brother Anubis's wife, hid his own heart in the blossom of a cedar tree so that no one could truly kill him. Betrayed and slain, he returned again and again — his brother reviving his heart, then Bata reborn as a great bull, as two persea trees, and finally, from a splinter that flew into the queen's mouth, as the crown prince — until he became king and his betrayer was judged. Innocence kept through three deaths won a throne.
reenactment_hook: Stash your life-force off-body and come back from death through it — before the run, hide a 'heart' object at a concealed node; when downed, return through it (and only its destruction can truly end you), surviving multiple deaths in escalating forms before winning the run alive.

epithet_grants.trigger: return from death at least twice via a hidden off-body object and finish the run alive
survive a false-accusation/betrayal event and win the run with the betrayer defeated

sources: Papyrus D'Orbiney (Tale of Two Brothers), BM EA 10183; Moldenke ed.

title: The Eye of Ra and the flood of red beer
summary: When humanity plotted against the aging sun-god Ra, he sent his Eye as the lioness Sekhmet to punish them, but her slaughter grew so unquenchable she threatened to wipe out the species entirely. To stop her the gods flooded the fields with seven thousand jars of beer dyed blood-red with ochre; Sekhmet, taking it for blood, drank until she collapsed drunk and woke pacified as gentle Hathor. Humanity survived — not by defeating her, but by out-thinking her thirst.
reenactment_hook: Unleash overwhelming, unstoppable destruction — land the single largest damage instance of the run and put a condition (burn/bleed/infected) on every enemy in an encounter, refusing to retreat once blood is drawn, forcing a massacre only appeasement could halt.

epithet_grants.trigger: land the run's single largest damage instance and inflict a condition on every enemy in one encounter without retreating
clear three encounters back-to-back without resting, on a VIP table or higher

sources: Book of the Heavenly Cow (The Destruction of Mankind)

title: The raging goddess danced home
summary: In the myth of the Distant Goddess, the Eye of Ra — the raging lioness in her wrathful Sekhmet form — quarrels with the sun-god and flees south to Nubia, and without her the land withers into chaos. Ra sends emissaries to coax her home: Thoth soothes her with words, and at Philae the dwarf-god Bes flatters her with drumming, dancing, and music until her fury softens and she returns as gentle Hathor, restoring the inundation and order. She is brought back not by force — nothing could force her — but by delight.
reenactment_hook: Defeat a raging boss by pacifying it with performance instead of force — win a 'calm the rage' event through camera-call taunts, music/dance actions, or feeding its desire, so the enraged enemy disengages and turns ally, rather than being out-damaged (damage only worsens it).

epithet_grants.trigger: pacify a raging boss to disengagement using performance/charm actions, landing no killing blow
complete the pacification with the party's weakest member never downed

sources: Philae Hathor-temple reliefs; the Myth of the Eye of the Sun (Distant Goddess tradition)

title: Setne and the Book of Thoth
summary: Thoth's own book of two great spells — one to understand the speech of all animals, one to see the gods — lay locked in nested chests at Coptos, guarded by a deathless serpent. Naneferkaptah seized it, and Thoth's revenge drowned his wife and son and then himself; the book was buried with him. Generations later Prince Setne stole it from the tomb, and the dead owner's ghost challenged him to a board game for it — each losing game magically hammered Setne deeper into the ground, to his legs, his waist, his ears — until he escaped only by a protective amulet, and was finally forced to return the book in penitence.
reenactment_hook: Acquire forbidden run-wide knowledge at a real, escalating cost, then either win with it or pay it back — take a cursed relic that reveals every hidden win condition and enemy intent, endure a permanent stacking penalty each floor (each lost 'ghost game' drains a body-part HP tier), and either master it by winning the final rigged game or return it to its tomb for a payout before the cost kills you.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a run using the cursed book's revealed intel, or return it to its tomb after surviving the ghost-game penalty, on a VIP table or higher

sources: Setne I (P. Cairo 30646), Griffith 1900, Stories of the High Priests of Memphis

title: Atum brings forth the first gods
summary: Before anything existed there was only Nun, the dark formless flood. Atum, the self-created one, willed himself into being and rose as the first mound of dry land at Heliopolis. Alone, he brought forth the first divine couple from his own body — spitting out Shu, god of air, and expectorating Tefnut, goddess of moisture — and from them descended the whole Ennead and the ordered world. The creator who completes himself made everything that is out of himself.
reenactment_hook: Build something lasting from nothing, alone — start a solo run from the minimum loadout with no allies and no starting resources, generate your own assets mid-run (craft or summon your own support), and clear the floor using only what you created.

epithet_grants.trigger: clear a floor solo using only resources generated during the run, starting from the minimum loadout, on a VIP table or higher

epithet_grants.epithet: Lord to the Limits of the Sky
epithet_grants.trigger: fully reveal and complete every objective on the floor in that same solo, self-supplied run

sources: Pyramid Texts, Utterance 600 (Heliopolitan cosmogony); Coffin Texts / BD (self-creation, Shu and Tefnut) — web-verified this session

title: The cosmos divided by lot
summary: After overthrowing the Titans, the three brothers drew lots to divide the cosmos: sky fell to Zeus, sea to Poseidon, and the underworld to Hades, with earth and Olympus held in common. Hades drew the least-desired share and honored the bargain without complaint. The universe itself was settled by a three-way draw — the casino's own founding legend.
reenactment_hook: At a run's start, let a blind random draw assign each party member their share of loot, territory, or role; publicly accept the worst lot without complaint, then win the run playing that bad hand.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a VIP-table-or-higher run after accepting the worst share from a blind opening draw and never re-rolling it

title: The Titanomachy — the ten-year war for heaven
summary: For ten years the Olympians under Zeus warred against Kronos and the Titans. Zeus freed the Cyclopes and Hundred-Handers from Tartarus, gaining the thunderbolt as their gift, and with their strength cast the defeated Titans down into the abyss. The Olympian order was founded on that victory.
reenactment_hook: Overthrow the leader that rules a floor or faction by first freeing its own imprisoned enemies to arm and reinforce yourself, then defeating it in open battle and taking its seat.

epithet_grants.trigger: dethrone a boss-faction leader on a VIP table or higher after recruiting its own imprisoned enemies to your side

title: Kronos unmans Ouranos with the sickle
summary: At Gaia's urging, the Titan Kronos lay hidden in ambush and, with a great adamantine sickle, castrated his father Ouranos as he descended upon the Earth — severing sky from earth and ending Ouranos's tyranny. From the spilled blood sprang the Furies and Giants, from the sea-foam Aphrodite. Kronos seized the throne of the cosmos.
reenactment_hook: Lie concealed and strike a superior enemy the instant it exposes itself, crippling it in a single ambush blow, then seize the position or territory it was guarding.

epithet_grants.trigger: cripple a superior enemy with a single concealed ambush strike and take its role or position, on a VIP table or higher

title: Kronos devours his children
summary: Warned that one of his own children would overthrow him as he had overthrown his father, Kronos swallowed each infant at birth. Rhea hid the newborn Zeus and fed Kronos a swaddled stone in his place. Grown, Zeus forced his father to disgorge the swallowed siblings, who joined the war that unseated him.
reenactment_hook: Preemptively consume or neutralize every drop, leader-unit, and power source that could later be used against you — kill each commander before its subordinates, claim each power-up before anyone else can — and win by leaving nothing to grow.

epithet_grants.trigger: clear a run having claimed or consumed every enemy power-up and killed every leader-unit before its subordinates, on VIP or higher

title: Athena springs armored from Zeus's head
summary: Warned that the goddess Metis would bear a child mightier than himself, Zeus swallowed her whole, taking her wisdom into himself and averting the prophesied overthrow. In time a splitting headache seized him; his skull was cloven, and Athena leapt out full-grown and fully armored, sounding a war-cry. Wisdom was thus born already perfected and bound to Zeus's rule.
reenactment_hook: Enter a boss fight already completely prepared for it — arrive with the exact counter-loadout, full intel, and a finished plan from the outset (no mid-fight adaptation) — and win the opening engagement flawlessly on foreknowledge alone.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a boss's opening engagement flawlessly using only pre-fight intelligence and a prepared counter, on VIP or higher

title: Athena and Poseidon contest for Athens
summary: Athena and Poseidon competed to become patron of the new city of Attica, each offering a gift to be judged. Poseidon struck the Acropolis rock and brought forth a salt spring; Athena planted the first olive tree. The judges ruled the olive the more useful gift, and the city took Athena's name.
reenactment_hook: Win a contested prize by presenting the most useful lasting offering to a panel of judges or the audience rather than by force — beating a rival's flashier play with a more valuable practical one and taking the vote.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a judged or audience-voted contest against a rival contestant by the utility of your offering rather than by combat

title: Arachne's weaving contest
summary: Arachne, a Lydian weaver of humble birth, boasted her skill surpassed Athena's and refused the goddess any credit. In a weaving duel Arachne's tapestry — depicting the gods' own crimes — was flawless, but her impiety enraged Athena, who struck her. Arachne was transformed into a spider, condemned to spin forever.
reenactment_hook: Craft or upgrade a masterwork item mid-run that visibly out-classes anything a god-patron granted you, then win a fight with it — publicly out-doing your own patron's gift.

epithet_grants.trigger: craft a masterwork that measurably outperforms your patron's granted gear and win an encounter using it

title: The abduction of Persephone
summary: Hades seized Persephone to be his queen in the underworld. Her grieving mother Demeter blighted the earth until Zeus ordered the girl's return; but Persephone had eaten pomegranate seeds below, and by that exact contract-law she must spend a portion of each year in the underworld. So the cycle of the seasons was made — the whole bargain resolved on the literal wording of what was swallowed.
reenactment_hook: Bind a target to your side by the exact letter of a contract — get an enemy or ally to consume or accept a single marked item that, by its precise stated terms, forces them to serve or return to you — then win the run using that binding.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a run after binding an enemy or ally to you by the exact stated terms of a single item, on VIP or higher

title: Orpheus descends for Eurydice
summary: When his bride Eurydice died, Orpheus went down alive into the underworld, charming Charon, Cerberus, and the dead with his lyre. Moved by his song, Hades and Persephone granted her return on a single condition: he must not look back at her until both had reached the upper world. At the last threshold Orpheus turned to look — and lost her forever.
reenactment_hook: Pacify encounters designed to be fought using a performance or hype action instead of combat, win a boon under a single behavioral restriction, and carry it all the way to the exit without once breaking the condition.

epithet_grants.epithet: the Voice That Moved the Dead
epithet_grants.trigger: reach a run's exit carrying a one-condition boon you never broke, having bypassed a boss by performance alone

title: Hermes steals Apollo's cattle on his first day
summary: On the very day of his birth Hermes left his cradle, invented the lyre from a tortoise shell, and stole Apollo's cattle — driving them backward to disguise the trail. Hauled before Zeus, the infant charmingly perjured himself, then settled the whole lawsuit by trading Apollo the lyre, winning both Apollo's friendship and the office of herald. He profited from the theft and from the settlement alike.
reenactment_hook: Steal a valuable item from an elite or boss without triggering combat (covering your trail so the theft can't be traced back), then when confronted, talk your way out and settle the dispute by trading a lesser crafted item for goodwill.

epithet_grants.trigger: steal from an elite or boss without combat and then resolve the fallout by negotiation, in a single run

title: Hermes wins five days from the Moon at draughts
summary: Playing draughts against the Moon, Hermes won a seventieth part of each of her monthly illuminations, and from the winnings assembled five extra days, which he added to the 360-day year — the five epagomenal days on which the gods could be born. The god literally gambled new time into existence. (Plutarch names the player Hermes; the tale is an interpretatio graeca of the Egyptian Thoth, recorded here with that syncretic caveat for Wave-4 cross-linking.)
reenactment_hook: Win a game-of-skill wager against a superior opponent for a stake that permanently adds a resource to your run (extra time on the Clock, an extra turn, an extra action slot), then win the run using the time you won.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a wager for a permanent extra-time or extra-action resource and clear the run with it, on VIP or higher

sources: Plutarch, Isis and Osiris 12 (verified, penelope.uchicago.edu; Thoth interpretatio graeca caveat)

title: Fortune and the sleeper by the well
summary: A workman thoughtlessly fell asleep at the very edge of a well. Fortune woke him, warning that had he rolled in and drowned, everyone would have blamed her rather than his own carelessness. The fable states Fortune's own blame-taboo: mortals credit their ruin to luck and their success to themselves.
reenactment_hook: Survive a run-threatening bad Forced-Action or random result, recover the room without an escape item, and never once curse your luck on camera — owning the outcome as your own.

epithet_grants.trigger: recover from a failed Forced Action and win the room without blaming your luck aloud on camera

sources: Aesop, Perry Index 174 / Babrius 49 (verified, mythfolklore.net/aesopica fable 470)

title: The lots of Praeneste
summary: At Praeneste a nobleman named Numerius Suffustius, prompted by repeated dreams, split open a flint rock — and out tumbled oak lots inscribed with archaic letters. From these the famous lot-oracle of Fortuna Primigenia was founded, its responses drawn by a child and always technically true. The cult began with one man obeying an omen and breaking open a stone.
reenactment_hook: Act on a cryptic in-run omen by breaking open an unmarked wall, rock, or chest exactly where the sign points, revealing a randomized fortune-item, and use its reading to win a later room.

epithet_grants.trigger: uncover a hidden lot or fortune-item by following an omen and win a later encounter on its reading

title: Apollo plies the Fates with wine for Admetus
summary: When Admetus was fated to die, Apollo — grateful for the king's hospitality — got the Moirai drunk on wine and won from them a reprieve: Admetus could escape death if someone else would die in his place. Only his wife Alcestis volunteered. It is the one recorded time the office of Fate was ever bent, and then only on a substitute stake.
reenactment_hook: Charm or bribe the run's unbending rules-enforcer (a hard system gate or fixed-doom NPC) into a one-time exception by offering something intoxicating or valuable — but only by putting up a substitute stake (another asset or party slot dies in your place), then win with the reprieve.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a one-time exception from a hard rules-gate by paying a substitute stake, on VIP or higher

title: The firebrand of Meleager
summary: At Meleager's birth the Moirai foretold he would live only as long as a particular log on the hearth remained unburnt. His mother Althaea snatched the brand from the fire and hid it. Years later, when Meleager killed her brothers in the quarrel over the Calydonian boar's spoils, Althaea cast the log back into the flames — and Meleager died as it was consumed. His life was an external, visible object all along.
reenactment_hook: Defeat a boss by locating and destroying its external life-source (a phylactery-like object hidden in the arena) rather than out-damaging it — or, defensively, stake your own survival on a hidden token you must protect for the whole run.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a boss fight by destroying its external life-object instead of winning a damage race

title: Pan at Marathon
summary: Running to Sparta for aid before the battle of Marathon, the courier Pheidippides met Pan on the mountain road. Pan asked why the Athenians neglected him, though he was their friend and would help them. After the Athenian victory — which Pan was believed to have won by striking the Persians with the panic that bears his name — the city founded him a cave-shrine below the Acropolis, with annual sacrifice and a torch-race.
reenactment_hook: Pay respect to an overlooked minor NPC or patron everyone else ignores, and later trigger a mass panic that routs three or more enemies at once — winning the fight by breaking morale rather than by kills.

epithet_grants.trigger: honor a neglected NPC or patron and later win a battle by routing three or more enemies with panic

title: The death of Great Pan
summary: In the reign of Tiberius a divine voice called across the sea to the sailor Thamus, commanding him to proclaim at Palodes that 'Great Pan is dead.' When he did, a great lamentation of unseen voices rose from the shore. Antiquity took it as the one attested death of a god — later read as the passing of the whole age of the old gods, and, for Pan, the setup for a comeback staked on his remaining existence.
reenactment_hook: After being counted out — publicly 'killed' on camera or eliminated to the loser's side-table — claw back into the run and win it, staking your remaining standing on the comeback.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a run after being publicly eliminated or declared dead, on VIP or higher

title: Pan and Syrinx
summary: Pan pursued the chaste nymph Syrinx, who fled to a riverbank and begged the water-nymphs to change her. As Pan seized her, he clutched only a stand of marsh reeds; when he sighed in disappointment, the wind through them made a plaintive music. He bound reeds of unequal length together and so invented the pan-pipes, named for her — a memorial to a chase that failed.
reenactment_hook: After a pursuit or objective fails and the prize slips away, salvage the remains into a new usable tool or instrument (craft something from the failure) and win a later room with it.

epithet_grants.trigger: craft a working item from the debris of a failed objective and use it to clear a later room

title: The rigged sacrifice at Mecone
summary: At Mecone, gods and mortals met to fix forever which portions of a sacrifice each would receive. Prometheus butchered an ox into two portions: the good meat and innards hidden inside the ugly stomach, and the bare bones dressed in glistening fat. Zeus chose the fat-wrapped bones — so mortals ever after kept the meat and burned only bones for the gods. The god-mortal relationship was priced by a rigged presentation.
reenactment_hook: Set up a rigged choice for an enemy or the house — present two options where the worse-looking one is actually better for you — and win a permanent run-advantage when they take the bait.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a permanent run-advantage by baiting the house or an enemy into a rigged two-option choice, on VIP or higher

title: Prometheus steals fire for mankind
summary: After Zeus hid fire from mortals, Prometheus stole it back — carrying a glowing ember concealed in a hollow fennel stalk — and gave it to humanity along with craft and civilization. For this defiance Zeus chained him to a crag where an eagle devoured his liver, which regrew each night, and sent Pandora as the retaliatory gift. He gave everything away at infinite personal cost.
reenactment_hook: Steal a run-critical resource or boon from the house or a boss and hand it to another contestant at real cost to yourself — accepting the house-flag or punishment that follows — then still win the room.

epithet_grants.trigger: give a stolen or hard-won run-critical boon to another contestant at cost to yourself and win the room, on VIP or higher

title: Prometheus bound and freed
summary: Chained to a Caucasus crag, Prometheus endured an eagle tearing out his liver every day for ages, never recanting, and held in reserve the secret that a son of Thetis would surpass Zeus. In time Heracles, passing on his labors, shot the eagle and broke the chains — with Zeus's tacit consent, for his son's glory — and Prometheus went free.
reenactment_hook: Free an imprisoned or tormented NPC by defeating the enemy that torments it, gaining an ally who then joins you — or, on the enduring side, survive a repeating damage or penalty cycle without ever using an escape and win anyway.

epithet_grants.trigger: endure a repeating damage cycle without escaping and free a captive ally by killing its tormentor, in one run

title: The Twelve Labors of Heracles
summary: In penance for a madness-killing, Heracles served King Eurystheus and completed twelve seemingly impossible labors — slaying the Nemean lion and the Hydra, capturing the Erymanthian boar and Ceryneian hind, cleaning the Augean stables by re-routing a river, and finally hauling Cerberus up from the underworld (overpowering Charon's ferry on the way). Two labors were annulled on technicalities by his cheating taskmaster, yet he finished all twelve and earned immortality.
reenactment_hook: Complete a chain of distinct, escalating challenge-objectives under a hostile taskmaster who moves the goalposts — clearing every one (including clever solutions like re-routing a hazard, not just force) and never quitting when a clear is annulled on a technicality.

epithet_grants.trigger: complete a full multi-stage labor-chain including at least one clear annulled on a technicality, on VIP or higher

title: Heracles wrestles Death for Alcestis
summary: Alcestis had died in her husband Admetus's place. Heracles, a guest in the grieving house, ambushed Death (Thanatos) at the tomb, seized him in a wrestling hold, and would not let go until Death surrendered the queen — bringing Alcestis back to the living by main force. Death itself was beaten in a fair grapple.
reenactment_hook: Physically pin or grapple the entity that embodies a run's death mechanic (a reaper-type enemy or the elimination event) and hold it until it releases a fallen teammate — reviving an ally by force rather than with an item.

epithet_grants.trigger: revive a fallen teammate by defeating or pinning the run's reaper-entity in melee, on VIP or higher

title: Perseus slays Medusa
summary: Guided by Athena and armed with a mirror-bright bronze shield, winged sandals, and a cap of invisibility, Perseus approached the Gorgon Medusa without ever meeting her petrifying gaze — watching only her reflection in the shield. He beheaded her as she slept; from her severed neck sprang the winged horse Pegasus and the giant Chrysaor. The whole kill turned on never facing her directly.
reenactment_hook: Defeat a gaze or look-at-me boss that can't be beaten by direct engagement — win only by using a reflective surface or indirect targeting, never facing it directly, turning its own attack back on it.

epithet_grants.trigger: kill a petrification or gaze boss via its own reflected gaze, without ever facing it directly, on VIP or higher

title: Oedipus answers the Sphinx
summary: The Sphinx besieged Thebes, devouring any traveler who failed her riddle: what walks on four legs at dawn, two at noon, and three at dusk? Oedipus answered — Man, who crawls as an infant, strides upright in his prime, and leans on a staff in old age. On hearing the correct answer the Sphinx flung herself from her rock and died, and Thebes was freed.
reenactment_hook: Beat a boss that can't be out-fought — it regenerates while its riddle or puzzle stands — by solving its puzzle interface, gathering the answer-clues scattered across the preceding floor and answering correctly to trigger its self-destruction.

epithet_grants.trigger: defeat a riddle-boss by solving its puzzle rather than by damage, on VIP or higher

title: Sisyphus cheats Death twice
summary: Sisyphus twice outwitted death: first he trapped Death (Thanatos) in chains, so that no one on earth could die until Ares freed him; then, having told his wife to neglect his funeral rites, he talked Persephone into letting him return to the upper world to set things right — and simply never went back. Caught at last, Zeus condemned him to roll a boulder uphill forever, only to watch it roll back down each time.
reenactment_hook: Cheat the run's death or elimination mechanic through cunning rather than a revive item — disable the elimination trigger for a Clock, or talk an underworld NPC into a release — and escape a guaranteed loss to win the run anyway.

epithet_grants.trigger: escape a guaranteed elimination by disabling or tricking the death mechanic without a revive item, on VIP or higher

title: Ganesha breaks his own tusk to keep writing the Mahabharata
summary: When the sage Vyasa needed a scribe fast enough to transcribe the entire Mahabharata as he dictated it, Ganesha agreed on the condition that Vyasa never pause — and Vyasa countered that Ganesha must understand each verse before writing it. Mid-dictation Ganesha's stylus snapped, and rather than break the unbroken flow he tore off one of his own tusks and wrote on with it, earning the name Ekadanta, the One-Tusked.
reenactment_hook: During a long channeled or multi-turn objective (a ritual, a hack, an escort), when your tool or weapon breaks or a gate demands a price, permanently sacrifice an equipped item rather than pause or abandon the task, and finish the objective without a single interruption.

epithet_grants.trigger: complete an uninterrupted multi-turn objective after permanently sacrificing an equipped item of real value, on a VIP table or higher

title: Ganesha wins the race around the world by circling his parents
summary: Shiva and Parvati offered a prize fruit to whichever son could circle the whole world first. Kartikeya sped off on his peacock to race around the cosmos, but Ganesha simply walked a slow circle around his parents, declaring that they were his entire world — and won by redefining the contest rather than out-running it.
reenactment_hook: Facing a timed traversal or footrace against a faster rival, win it not by out-moving them but by solving the win-condition laterally — trigger a shortcut, satisfy the objective's true requirement in place, or reinterpret the goal — beating an opponent with higher movement or speed.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a traversal or race objective by a lateral solution instead of out-running the rival

title: Ganesha curses the moon for mocking him
summary: The moon god Chandra laughed at Ganesha after the pot-bellied god stumbled, and in wounded pride Ganesha cursed him: any who gaze on the moon on Ganesha's festival day will be struck by false accusation. The curse's most famous victim was Krishna, wrongly blamed for stealing the Syamantaka jewel — vindicated only by publicly proving where it had gone.
reenactment_hook: When a rival contestant is downed or eliminated on camera, choose an honoring or aiding action over a taunt (and if already saddled with a False Accusation debuff, clear it by publicly proving the truth), winning the crowd through grace rather than mockery.

epithet_grants.trigger: defeat a rival and honor them on camera instead of taunting, clearing or averting the False Accusation debuff

sources: Puranic Ganesha Chaturthi tradition; Vishnu Purana IV.13 (Wilson tr., PD) for the Syamantaka showcase

title: Shiva loses everything to Parvati at dice
summary: Shiva and Parvati sat down to a game of dice, Parvati quietly backed by the wealth-goddess Lakshmi. Round after round Parvati won, and the reckless Shiva kept doubling down — staking his ornaments, then everything, down to the clothes on his back — until he had lost it all. Parvati then decreed that gambling on the night of Diwali would be auspicious forever.
reenactment_hook: In a table or wager event, stake everything you carry — all currency and loot — on a single high-stakes bet and win it, ideally with an allied backer staking you, against a higher-tier opponent.

epithet_grants.trigger: win an all-in wager against a higher-tier opponent on a VIP table or higher
stake everything you carry on a single wager and win the run

sources: Skanda Purana ch. 34 'Śiva Loses to Pārvatī in a Game of Dice' (wisdomlib tr.); Padma Purana

title: The churning of the ocean and Shiva's blue throat
summary: Gods and demons together churned the ocean of milk, using Mount Mandara as the churning-rod and the serpent Vasuki as the rope, to win the nectar of immortality. The churning first spat up the halahala, a poison that could destroy all creation — and Shiva drank it, holding it in his throat, which turned blue, earning him the name Neelakantha. From the same ocean rose Lakshmi, goddess of fortune.
reenactment_hook: When a floor-wide poison or burn hazard threatens the whole party, voluntarily absorb it onto yourself (the halahala clause), hold the condition without dying, and survive to complete the run.

epithet_grants.trigger: absorb a floor-wide poison or burn hazard meant for the party and survive to complete the run on a VVIP table or higher

sources: Mahabharata Adi Parva (Ganguli tr., PD); Vishnu Purana I.9 (Wilson tr., PD)

title: Shiva destroys Daksha's sacrifice
summary: Daksha held a grand fire-sacrifice and pointedly excluded his son-in-law Shiva; his daughter Sati, unable to bear the insult to her husband, cast herself into the sacred fire. In grief-fueled wrath Shiva loosed the fury Virabhadra, who razed the sacrifice and beheaded Daksha, and Shiva carried Sati's body across the world.
reenactment_hook: When an event or boss snubs, excludes, or insults you, retaliate by destroying it outright — overwhelming and razing the encounter the game expected you to appease or negotiate with — and win through wrath.

epithet_grants.trigger: destroy the event or boss that snubbed or excluded you rather than appease it

title: Parvati's austerities win Shiva
summary: To win the ascetic Shiva, who had burned the love-god Kama to ash rather than be swayed, Parvati renounced comfort and undertook austerities of escalating severity — fasting, enduring heat and cold, at last refusing even fallen leaves, which earned her the name Aparna, the Leafless. Shiva, testing her disguised as a wandering brahmin who mocked himself, found her devotion unbreakable and accepted her.
reenactment_hook: Retry a single hard objective three or more times, giving up a buff, consumable, or comfort each attempt (escalating self-denial), never abandoning it, until it finally clears.

epithet_grants.trigger: clear one objective after three or more escalating-hardship retries without ever abandoning it

sources: Kalidasa, Kumarasambhava canto V (PD tr.); Shiva Purana

title: Annapurna feeds a starving world
summary: When Shiva declared the material world — food included — to be mere illusion, the offended Parvati withdrew as Annapurna, goddess of nourishment, and vanished. The world starved, gods and mortals alike wasting away, until Shiva came to Kashi with a begging bowl and conceded that sustenance is no illusion, and Annapurna fed him from her own hand.
reenactment_hook: During a resource-scarcity event with starving NPCs or teammates, give away your own food or consumables to feed them rather than hoard, and still complete the run.

epithet_grants.trigger: feed a starving NPC or teammate from your own supplies during a scarcity event and complete the run

sources: Skanda Purana, Kashi Khanda tradition (late attestation — flagged)

title: Lakshmi abandons the gods
summary: The sage Durvasa gave Indra a divine garland, which Indra carelessly let his elephant trample. Enraged, Durvasa cursed the gods to lose their fortune, and Lakshmi withdrew from heaven into the ocean, leaving the devas powerless and impoverished — a loss so total it could only be undone by churning the ocean to bring her back.
reenactment_hook: Continue and win a run after your patron abandons you mid-run — your aura and boons stripped away — pressing on with no divine backing, then reclaim favor by the finale.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a run after your patron abandons you mid-run and reclaim favor by the finale

title: Shani's gaze severs the infant Ganesha's head
summary: At the newborn Ganesha's blessing, the planet-god Shani kept his eyes lowered, warning that his gaze — cursed to destroy whatever it fell upon — was too dangerous to turn on the infant. Parvati insisted he look; Shani's reluctant glance severed the child's head, which Vishnu then replaced with an elephant's.
reenactment_hook: Carry a power or curse-gaze that harms whatever it touches (an uncontrolled damaging aura or line-of-sight beam) and win by keeping it suppressed until you can unleash it on exactly the correct single target at the decisive moment.

epithet_grants.trigger: win an encounter by suppressing a harm-everything power until unleashing it on the correct single target

sources: Brahma Vaivarta Purana, Ganapati Khanda (verified via index)

title: Shani's seven-and-a-half-year trial of King Vikramaditya
summary: King Vikramaditya, asked to judge which planet-god was greatest, tactfully ranked Shani lowest — and Shani warned him he would now endure the seven-and-a-half-year affliction of Sade Sati. Falsely accused of theft, mutilated, exiled, and reduced to laboring at an oil-press, the king bore every undeserved blow with patience; when the term ended Shani restored him greater than before.
reenactment_hook: Accept a severe run-start handicap (a curse or maiming debuff) offered by an event and complete the entire run without ever cleansing it (the sade-sati clause), collecting the back-loaded payout at the end.

epithet_grants.trigger: clear a full run under an uncleansed run-start handicap without ever removing it

sources: Shani Mahatmya (Marathi devotional text — devotional-literary attestation, flagged)

title: Kubera's eternal wedding loan to Venkateswara
summary: To pay for his wedding to Padmavati, the god Vishnu — in his form as Venkateswara of Tirupati — borrowed an immense fortune from Kubera, treasurer of the gods, repayable with interest until the end of the present age. Devotees' offerings at Tirumala are understood to help service that eternal, still-compounding debt.
reenactment_hook: Take a divine line of credit at run-start (advance boons as a loan) and repay it in full with interest from your winnings before its deadline, ending the run in profit.

epithet_grants.trigger: repay an in-run divine loan in full with interest and still finish the run in profit

sources: Venkatachala Mahatmya tradition / Tirumala temple lore (living practice, verified)

title: The rising and expelling of Alakshmi
summary: From the churning of the ocean, before her radiant sister Lakshmi, rose Alakshmi — the elder, goddess of misfortune, ruin, and quarrel. A living tradition acknowledges her only to drive her out: at Diwali she is ritually expelled, the Sri Sukta prays for her destruction, and lime-and-chili wards are hung to keep her from the door.
reenactment_hook: Refuse every purification, ward-charm, and shrine for a full run — deliberately keeping an uncleansed curse or negative status — and win the run in that degraded, everyone-else-flees-it state.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a full run carrying an uncleansed curse without ever purifying it

sources: Padma Purana churning account; Sri Sukta khila ('alakṣmīr me naśyatāṁ'); Diwali expulsion rite

title: Alakshmi's bargain: where misfortune is allowed to live
summary: Married to a sage, Alakshmi found she could not bear to remain anywhere touched by worship, virtue, or cleanliness, and asked where she might live. She was granted dwelling only where there is quarrel, filth, gambling, dishonesty, and disrespect of elders — the terms that ever after define where misfortune settles, and the conduct that invites her in.
reenactment_hook: Accept a pact that confines your strength to the worst ground — cursed rooms, quarrel or hazard tiles, squalor — and win by fighting encounters exclusively on that terrain where the bargain makes you strongest.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a run's encounters fought exclusively on hazard or cursed terrain under a confining pact

sources: Linga Purana ch. 6 'The origin and activities of Alakṣmī' (wisdomlib tr.); Padma Purana (Duhsaha/Uddalaka marriage), web-verified 2026-07-18

title: Ravana seizes the Pushpaka from Kubera
summary: Kubera held the flying palace-chariot Pushpaka, crafted by Vishwakarma and given to him by Brahma. His half-brother Ravana, envious and stronger, assaulted Kubera's city of Alaka, defeated him, and carried off the Pushpaka by force — a prize Ravana kept until his death, when it at last returned.
reenactment_hook: Have a rival seize a signature item or mount from you, then recover it before the run ends (or defend it so it is never taken), finishing with the prized asset back in your hands.

epithet_grants.trigger: recover a signature asset seized by a rival, or defend it uncaptured, and end the run holding it

sources: Valmiki Ramayana, Uttara Kanda (Ravana seizes Alaka and the Pushpaka from Kubera), web-verified 2026-07-18

title: Nala's ruin and the Heart of Dice
summary: The dice-demon Kali, spurned when Damayanti chose the mortal king Nala, waited twelve years for a lapse in Nala's ritual purity, then possessed him and drove him to gamble away his entire kingdom throw by throw. Exiled and disguised as a cook, Nala traded his mastery of horses for another king's aksha-hridaya, the Heart of Dice — and armed with that hidden knowledge he expelled the demon and won everything back in a single all-in rematch.
reenactment_hook: After losing all your currency and loot to a rigged or possessed wager, acquire the event's hidden mechanic (learn to read or count it — the Heart of Dice), then win it all back and more in a single decisive all-in rematch within the same run.

epithet_grants.trigger: lose everything to a wager, then win it all back and more in a single rematch within the same run

sources: Mahabharata Vana Parva, Nalopakhyana (Ganguli tr., PD; sacred-texts.com/hin/m03/m03077.htm)

title: The rigged dice game that doomed a dynasty
summary: Bound by the warrior code never to refuse a challenge, Yudhishthira sat down to dice against Shakuni, whose loaded dice could not lose. He staked and lost his wealth, his kingdom, his brothers, himself, and finally his wife Draupadi, whose humiliation in the hall sealed a thirteen-year exile and made the great war inevitable. The rigged table cannot be beaten by rolling — only by refusing it or exposing the cheat.
reenactment_hook: Facing a rigged wager you cannot beat by rolling, expose the loaded dice (via an audit or insight ability) or change the game's terms, and walk away with the pot instead of being cleaned out.

epithet_grants.trigger: expose and beat a rigged wager without losing your stake, on a VIP table or higher

sources: Mahabharata Sabha Parva, the dyuta (Ganguli tr., PD)

title: The Yaksha's questions at the lake
summary: Thirsty in exile, the four younger Pandavas each drank from a lake despite a yaksha's warning, and each fell dead. Yudhishthira came last, answered the spirit's searching riddles on duty and truth without a single error, and the yaksha — revealed as his divine father Dharma — restored his brothers to life.
reenactment_hook: Solve a lethal riddle or knowledge gate — where wrong or silent answers keep teammates dead — with zero errors, reviving your fallen party by wisdom rather than force.

epithet_grants.trigger: revive fallen teammates by solving a riddle or knowledge gate with zero wrong answers

sources: Mahabharata Vana Parva, Yaksha Prashna (Ganguli tr., PD)

title: Yudhishthira refuses heaven without his dog
summary: Renouncing their kingdom, the Pandavas and Draupadi walked toward heaven, and one by one they fell along the road until only Yudhishthira remained, followed by a faithful dog. At heaven's gate Indra offered him paradise but not the dog; Yudhishthira refused to enter without his loyal companion — and the dog revealed itself as Dharma, the final test passed.
reenactment_hook: At the run's final reward gate, refuse a top prize offered on the condition that you abandon a loyal companion, teammate, or summon — keep the companion, forgo the prize, and complete the run anyway.

epithet_grants.trigger: forgo a run's top reward rather than abandon a loyal companion, and still complete the run

sources: Mahabharata Mahaprasthanika & Svargarohana Parva (Ganguli tr., PD)

title: The beheading of Rahu and the eternal eclipse
summary: During the sharing-out of the immortality nectar, the asura Svarbhanu disguised himself among the gods to steal a sip. The Sun and Moon exposed him and Vishnu struck off his head with the discus — but the nectar had already passed his throat, so the severed head lived on as Rahu, forever chasing and swallowing Sun and Moon in revenge, the light escaping through his open neck to end each eclipse.
reenactment_hook: Infiltrate a guarded one-time reward (disguise into a restricted line, steal a sip of a permanent buff) and retain the permanent gain even after being caught, downed, or penalized for the theft — a heist paid for with a body-part that cannot be undone.

epithet_grants.trigger: steal a one-time permanent buff from a guarded source and keep it even after being downed or penalized for the theft

sources: Mahabharata Adi Parva, Astika section (Ganguli tr., PD); Vishnu Purana (Wilson tr., PD)

title: Durga slays the buffalo-demon
summary: The buffalo-demon Mahishasura, protected by a boon that no god or man could kill him, conquered the heavens. So the gods pooled their energies and weapons to forge the goddess Durga, who fought him through every shape he took — buffalo, lion, man, elephant — and beheaded him mid-transformation, fulfilling the one exclusion his own boon had left open.
reenactment_hook: Defeat a boss immune to all conventional attackers by fulfilling its hidden exclusion condition — forge a party-pooled 'Assembled Goddess' power (every member donates a weapon or ability) or field the one attacker type it cannot resist — and land the kill it was warded against.

epithet_grants.trigger: kill a boss via its hidden exclusion condition rather than a damage race, on a VVIP table or higher
