sources: Devi Mahatmya / Markandeya Purana (Pargiter tr. 1904, PD)

title: The twenty-five tales of the Vetala
summary: A sorcerer set King Vikramaditya to carry a corpse possessed by a vetala from its tree. Each time, the spirit told a riddle-tale and posed a question: if the king knew the answer and stayed silent his head would burst, but answering sent the corpse flying back to the tree. After twenty-four such rounds the vetala, won over, warned the king that the sorcerer meant to sacrifice him, and told him how to turn the ambush around.
reenactment_hook: Run a lethal riddle-gauntlet where staying silent when you know the answer kills you but answering resets the challenge: answer every riddle you can, endure the repeated resets, and complete the full cycle to earn the spirit's warning that flips a coming ambush.

epithet_grants.trigger: complete the full riddle-gauntlet cycle and turn the warning it yields against an ambush

sources: Vetala Panchavimshati / Kathasaritsagara bk. 12; Burton, Vikram and the Vampire (1870, PD, Gutenberg #2400)

title: Krishna clears his name over the Syamantaka jewel
summary: The Sun's jewel Syamantaka poured out gold for a worthy keeper but destroyed the unworthy. When it passed by way of a lion to the bear-king Jambavan, Krishna — falsely accused of stealing it because he had glimpsed the cursed moon — tracked it down, fought Jambavan for twenty-eight days, recovered the gem, and publicly returned it to clear his name.
reenactment_hook: Under a false-accusation or theft debuff, track down and recover a stolen high-value item through pursuit and prolonged combat, then publicly return or prove it — clearing your name rather than merely pocketing the loot.

epithet_grants.trigger: recover a stolen high-value item and publicly clear a false-accusation debuff, on a VIP table or higher

sources: Vishnu Purana IV.13 (Wilson tr., PD); Bhagavata Purana X.56–57

title: The Rock-Cave of Heaven
summary: Outraged and defiled by Susanoo's rampage — a flayed heavenly pony flung through the roof of her weaving-hall, killing a weaver — the sun goddess Amaterasu seals herself inside the heavenly rock-cave, and the world goes dark. The eight hundred myriad gods gather outside; Ame-no-Uzume dances a bawdy dance atop an overturned tub until the whole assembly roars with laughter. Curious why they cheer with the sun gone, Amaterasu peeks out; a mirror thrust before her shows her own radiance, and the strongman Ame-no-Tajikarao hauls her free — light returns to the world.
reenactment_hook: With the audience/hype meter pinned at its floor and the arena effectively 'dark', stage a comeback within a single encounter: execute a performance or taunt action that spikes hype from bottom to a full roar in one turn, then use a light source or mirror to flush a hidden/sealed enemy into the open and finish the floor.

epithet_grants.trigger: Recover the hype meter from its floor to a maximum roar and clear the floor in the same run, on a VIP table or higher

sources: Kojiki Sect. XVI 'The Door of the Heavenly Rock-Dwelling' (Chamberlain tr., 1882)

title: The Oath of the Exchanged Regalia
summary: When Susanoo ascends to heaven, Amaterasu suspects his intent and arms herself. To prove his heart is true, he proposes a formal wager: each will produce children from the other's regalia, and the outcome will decide who is sincere. Amaterasu chews and spits Susanoo's sword to bear three goddesses; he chews her string of magatama to bear five gods — the ancestral kami of the imperial and clan lines. The declared stakes settle his sincerity by adjudicated result.
reenactment_hook: Before an encounter, publicly stake something of real value (a skill or item put up as forfeit) on a named, specific outcome declared aloud on camera, then win the encounter exactly as sworn — the result must match the declared terms to claim the pot; a mismatch forfeits the stake.

epithet_grants.trigger: Win an on-camera wager whose stake was a real skill/item, with the sworn outcome matching the result, on a VIP table or higher

title: The Slaying of the Food Goddess
summary: Amaterasu sends the moon-god Tsukuyomi to the food goddess Ukemochi. The goddess produces a banquet from her mouth and body — rice from her cheeks, fish from the sea, game from the land; disgusted at being served from her body, Tsukuyomi kills her. From her corpse spring the staple grains, silkworms, and cattle. Appalled at the killing, Amaterasu banishes him from her sight forever, and so day and night are sundered. (In the Kojiki variant the deed belongs to Susanoo and the food goddess Ōgetsu-hime — the record carries both attributions honestly.)
reenactment_hook: Refuse a tainted or defiling offering and strike down its giver, then harvest from the slain body a cache of consumable resources (loot the corpse for the run's staple supplies) — accepting the permanent loss of an allied patron's favor as the price of the killing.

epithet_grants.trigger: Slay a host/food-type enemy and harvest a resource cache from its body while accepting the loss of an allied patron's favor, on a VIP table or higher

sources: Nihongi Book I (Aston tr., 1896); Kojiki variant (Susanoo / Ōgetsu-hime)

title: Izanagi in the Land of Yomi
summary: After Izanami dies birthing fire, her husband Izanagi follows her into Yomi, the land of the dead, to bring her home. She agrees to petition its gods but forbids him to look at her. Impatient, he lights a tooth of his comb and sees her rotting body swarming with maggots and the Eight Thunder-gods. Horrified, he flees; shamed, she looses the hags of Yomi and the thunders in pursuit. He escapes by casting away comb and fruit to delay them and seals the pass of Yomotsu Hirasaka with a boulder. Across it she vows to strangle a thousand people a day; he answers he will cause fifteen hundred births.
reenactment_hook: Enter a death-zone to recover something lost, break a 'do not look' rule to reveal hidden information, then survive the pursuit that follows by discarding valuable items to delay pursuers and sealing the exit behind you (block a corridor or portal) — the pursuers cannot be killed, only escaped and walled off.

epithet_grants.trigger: Escape a death-zone pursuit by sealing the exit after breaking a look-taboo to gain intel, on a VIP table or higher

title: The Fire-Child That Killed Its Mother
summary: Izanami bears the fire-god Kagutsuchi and is fatally burned in the birth — the first death among the creator gods. Wasting in her sickness, she brings forth still more deities from her vomit, urine, and excrement before she dies and departs to Yomi. The mother of the islands is undone by the one child that is pure heat, and death enters the world of the gods.
reenactment_hook: Unleash a one-time burn/fire effect so powerful it removes you from the fight (self-KO or permanent HP loss), where that same effect spawns lasting battlefield features or resources — persistent hazard tiles or a supply cache — that let your surviving allies finish the run.

epithet_grants.trigger: Trigger a self-removing fire effect that creates persistent hazards or resources and still win the run, on a VIP table or higher

title: Izanagi Beheads the Fire-Child
summary: Grief-maddened at Izanami's death, Izanagi draws the ten-grasp sword Ame-no-Ohabari and beheads the infant fire-god Kagutsuchi. From the blood that spatters the rocks and from his dismembered body spring a host of new deities — sword-gods, the line of the thunder-warrior Takemikazuchi, and the gods of the mountains. The blaze is cut down, but its unmaking seeds the world with fresh powers.
reenactment_hook: Avenge a fallen ally by landing a single overwhelming execute-strike on the enemy that killed them — a strike that shatters the target into several weaker foes born of its 'blood', which your party must then mop up to claim the reward.

epithet_grants.trigger: Kill an enemy with a single execute-strike that spawns adds, then clear every add spawned, on a VIP table or higher

sources: Kojiki 'birth of the fire-deity' — beheading with Ame-no-Ohabari (Chamberlain tr., 1882)

title: Susanoo and the Eight-Forked Serpent
summary: Exiled to Izumo, Susanoo finds an old couple weeping: the eight-headed serpent Yamata-no-Orochi has devoured seven of their daughters and comes now for the last, Kushinada-hime. Susanoo has them brew eight vats of eight-fold refined sake and set them behind eight gates. The serpent plunges a head into each vat, drinks itself insensible, and Susanoo hews the drunken coils apart. In its tail he finds a great sword, Ame-no-Murakumo — later Kusanagi — which he sends up to Amaterasu.
reenactment_hook: Rather than trade blows with a multi-headed boss, place bait — a valuable consumable or lure — to inflict the exhausted/intoxicated condition on it, then strike each head or segment while it is incapacitated, and recover the hidden jackpot buried in its final segment (the fourth-tail cache).

epithet_grants.trigger: Defeat a colossal multi-part boss by first incapacitating it with bait instead of raw damage, and recover its tail-cache, on a VIP table or higher

sources: Kojiki Sect. XVIII 'The Eight-Forked Serpent' (Chamberlain tr., 1882)

title: The Fining of the Impetuous God
summary: For the rampage that drove Amaterasu into the rock-cave, the eight hundred myriad gods sit in judgment on Susanoo. They impose on him a fine of a thousand tables of restitution, cut off his beard, pull out the nails of his fingers and toes, and expel him from heaven with a divine expulsion. Stripped of everything, the storm god descends to Izumo to rebuild from nothing — the mythology's first champion to lose all his tables and start over.
reenactment_hook: Forfeit the bulk of your accumulated run resources — surrender your gold/'tables' or have a key item confiscated as a penalty — then complete the remaining floor from that stripped state, winning back more than you gave up.

epithet_grants.trigger: Win a floor after voluntarily forfeiting the majority of your run resources, on a Forsaken or VIP+ table

sources: Kojiki — the thousand-tables fine and expulsion (Chamberlain tr., 1882, verified quote)

title: The Three Noble Children Born of Purification
summary: Fouled by the pollution of Yomi, Izanagi bathes in a river to purify himself, and deities are born from his cast-off garments and his body. As he washes his left eye, the sun goddess Amaterasu comes forth; from his right eye, the moon-god Tsukuyomi; from his nose, the storm-god Susanoo. Rejoicing, he divides the cosmos among the three noble children — the plain of high heaven, the realm of night, and the seas. The purification rite of misogi and the three ruling kami are born together.
reenactment_hook: After being loaded with conditions in a death-zone (three or more stacked debuffs/curses), immerse in a purification tile or spring to strip every negative condition at once, and emerge with one chosen elemental boon — light, night, or storm — that persists for the rest of the run.

epithet_grants.trigger: Cleanse three or more stacked debuffs in a single purification action and finish the floor, on a VIP table or higher

sources: Kojiki — misogi purification and birth of the three precious children (Chamberlain tr., 1882)

title: Inari and the Smith's Little Fox
summary: Emperor Ichijō commands the swordsmith Sanjō Munechika to forge an imperial blade, but Munechika has no partner of equal skill to strike the hammer with him. He prays at the shrine of his clan's guardian, Inari. The deity comes — first as a mysterious boy who steadies his heart, then as the fox-spirit itself — and hammers as his co-smith. Together they forge the blade Kogitsune-maru, 'Little Fox', which bears both their signatures.
reenactment_hook: Mid-run, pray or make an offering at a shrine to summon a temporary divine ally, then bring it to a forge station and craft a unique weapon in partnership — the crafted item carries a bonus obtainable only with the summoned co-smith.

epithet_grants.trigger: Summon a divine partner and forge a signature weapon with it within a single run

title: The Rice-Cake and the White Bird
summary: The wealthy Hata no Irogu, grown rich on stored sheaves of rice, idly set a rice-cake as an archery target. Struck, the mochi turned into a white bird and flew to a mountain peak, where rice sprang up — a sign of the god. Repenting his waste of the grain's bounty, Hata raised a shrine on that peak; from 'ine nari' ('rice grew') came the name Inari, and the shrine (711 CE) grew into the head of some thirty thousand Inari shrines.
reenactment_hook: Refuse to squander a windfall: instead of spending or consuming a rare resource for a quick gain, 'plant' it — consecrate it at a shrine or hidden altar node — to unlock a compounding prosperity boon that pays out steadily across the rest of the run.

epithet_grants.epithet: Great God of the Five Grains
epithet_grants.trigger: Forgo an immediate reward by consecrating a rare resource, then collect the larger compounded payout by the end of the run

sources: Yamashiro no Kuni Fudoki fragment (~713 CE); Fushimi Inari Taisha founding tradition (verified 2026-07-18)

title: The White Hare of Inaba
summary: Trailing behind his eighty cruel brothers on their way to court Princess Yakami, Okuninushi finds a flayed, weeping hare — tricked by the brothers into rubbing salt on its raw skin after it lost a wager with the crocodiles. Where the brothers mocked it, Okuninushi tells the hare to wash in fresh water and roll in cattail pollen. Healed, the grateful hare prophesies that it is Okuninushi, not his brothers, who will win the princess.
reenactment_hook: Stop to heal a wounded neutral NPC or creature you could safely ignore — spend a real healing resource on a non-ally — and be rewarded with hidden intelligence: the creature reveals a boss's weakness or a concealed route in gratitude.

epithet_grants.trigger: Heal a neutral creature and receive and act on the intel it gives you within the same run

sources: Kojiki 'The White Hare of Inaba' (sacred-texts kj028, Chamberlain tr., 1882)

title: The Trials in the Root-Land
summary: Killed twice by his jealous brothers and twice revived by his mother, Okuninushi flees to Ne-no-kuni, the root-land, and falls in love with Susanoo's daughter Suseri-hime. Susanoo tests him with a chamber of snakes and a chamber of centipedes and wasps — both survived with protective scarves Suseri-hime secretly gives him — and a burning field, from which a mouse shows him the escape. He steals Susanoo's sword, bow, and koto and flees with his bride; half-admiring, Susanoo shouts a blessing after him, naming him ruler of the land.
reenactment_hook: Survive a sequence of lethal hazard-rooms — a snake pit, a swarm room, a burning field — by using consumable gifts from an ally at exactly the right moment, then steal a guarded treasure and escape the dungeon before its owner catches you.

epithet_grants.trigger: Clear a gauntlet of three or more distinct hazard rooms and extract a guarded item without dying, on a VIP table or higher

title: The Ceding of the Land
summary: The heavenly kami send envoys — culminating in the thunder-warrior Takemikazuchi, who plants his sword upright on the waves and sits cross-legged on its point — to demand that Okuninushi surrender the Central Land of Reed-Plains. After consulting his sons, Okuninushi yields rather than fight, on one condition: that a great shrine be raised for him at Izumo and that he be granted lordship over the 'unseen' affairs. The tradition's great negotiated buy-out.
reenactment_hook: When outmatched by a boss, negotiate a surrender instead of fighting: concede the contested objective in exchange for a permanent hidden reward — a lasting boon or shrine buff — winning the deal rather than the battle.

epithet_grants.trigger: Resolve a boss encounter through a negotiated concession that yields a permanent buff, on a VIP table or higher

sources: Kojiki / Nihongi — Kuni-yuzuri, the transfer of the land (Chamberlain & Aston tr.)

title: The Leech-Child Set Adrift
summary: At the first wedding rite of Izanagi and Izanami the bride spoke first, breaching the proper order, and their first child Hiruko was born boneless — the 'leech-child' — and set adrift in a reed boat. Medieval tradition tells that the castaway drifted ashore at Nishinomiya and was raised there to become Ebisu, the laughing god of fishermen and merchants: heaven's own discard, floated home and holding no grudge. Because he is hard of hearing, his worshippers clap loudly to get his attention.
reenactment_hook: Recover from being discarded: after being downed or removed from the fight and drifting back in, land a windfall — announce your wager out loud (declare a bet), then fish up a jackpot from a chance node, turning a near-wipe into a payday.

epithet_grants.trigger: Return from a downed or removed state and claim a declared windfall in the same run

sources: Kojiki (Hiruko birth); medieval Nishinomiya identification with Ebisu (Britannica: Hiruko, verified)

title: The Fisher Who Yielded at Miho
summary: In the tradition that identifies Ebisu with Kotoshironushi, son of Okuninushi: when heaven's envoy came for the land, Okuninushi deferred to his son, who was away fishing at Cape Miho. Told of the demand, Kotoshironushi assented at once, then made a reverse 'heavenly clap', overturned his fishing boat into a green brushwood fence, and hid himself within it. His fishing at Miho is the root of Ebisu's fisher iconography, and Miho Shrine his seat.
reenactment_hook: Concede a contested objective without a fight, then immediately convert your own large equipment (a vehicle or bulky item) into defensive cover — a barricade — and hide behind it from a stronger pursuer until the threat passes.

epithet_grants.trigger: Yield an objective, create cover from your own gear, and survive a pursuit undefeated

sources: Kojiki — Kotoshironushi at Cape Miho in the Kuni-yuzuri; Ebisu/Kotoshironushi identification (Miho Shrine tradition, verified 2026-07-18)

title: Benzaiten and the Five-Headed Dragon
summary: For centuries a five-headed dragon, Gozuryū, terrorized the villages of the Sagami coast, devouring the children of the people. The goddess Benzaiten descends from the clouds, raising the island of Enoshima beneath her feet. The dragon, smitten at the sight of her, proposes marriage; she refuses, and — famed above all for her eloquence — argues him into shame at his own cruelty. Reformed, he turns to face her island in penance forever and becomes Dragon's-Mouth Hill.
reenactment_hook: Defeat a boss without landing a killing blow: use dialogue and persuasion actions (a talk-down mechanic) to shame or pacify it into surrender, resolving the entire encounter through charm and taking zero kills.

epithet_grants.trigger: Pacify a boss into a non-lethal surrender purely through persuasion actions, dealing no killing blow, on a VIP table or higher

title: The Money-Washing Spring
summary: In the year, month, and day of the snake, the harvest-god Ugafukujin appeared in a dream to Minamoto no Yoritomo as a human-headed serpent, bidding him seek a hidden spring in a western valley and worship there to bring peace to his new shogunate. Yoritomo found the spring and enshrined Benzaiten beside it. Later the regent Hōjō Tokiyori taught the faithful to wash their coins in the cave spring, promising the goddess would multiply them — the origin of the money-washing rite of Zeniarai Benten.
reenactment_hook: Wash your winnings to multiply them: deposit the run's currency into a spring or sacred node, locking it away and unusable for a stretch, then reclaim it multiplied at run's end — leaving it longer multiplies it more, but a wipe forfeits the whole pot.

epithet_grants.trigger: Consecrate at least half your currency at a spring node and reclaim the multiplied payout at the end of the run

sources: Zeniarai Benzaiten Ugafuku Shrine founding legend (Yoritomo 1185 dream; Hōjō Tokiyori 1257 money-washing, verified 2026-07-18)

title: The Cycle of the Brave of Yamato
summary: Prince Ōusu, sent against enemies by a father who fears him, slays the Kumaso chieftains disguised in his aunt's maiden robes — earning the name Yamato Takeru from a dying foe. Armed by his aunt Yamatohime with the sword Kusanagi and a fire-striker bag, he is lured onto a burning plain; he mows the grass with the sword and sets a back-burn to survive. His consort Ototachibana calms a raging sea by casting herself into the waves. Grown arrogant, he mocks the god of Mount Ibuki barehanded, is felled by its hail-sickness, and dies at Nobono — his soul departing as a great white bird.
reenactment_hook: When trapped in a spreading-hazard tile — a fire field advancing on you — use a bladed weapon to clear the fuel around you and set a controlled back-burn, turning the enemy's own hazard into a firebreak that shields you and burns the attackers.

epithet_grants.trigger: Escape a fire-field encounter by clearing fuel and setting a back-burn with a bladed weapon
Complete a full heroic multi-stage run despite taking a fatal-hubris setback, on a VIP table or higher

sources: Kojiki Middle Volume; Nihongi Book VII (Chamberlain & Aston tr.)

title: Momotarō and the Ogres' Island
summary: Born from a giant peach found floating by a childless old couple, Momotarō grows strong and sets out for the ogres' island of Onigashima, carrying his mother's millet dumplings. On the road he recruits a dog, a monkey, and a pheasant, giving each a dumpling in exchange for their loyalty. Together the band storms the oni fortress, defeats the demons, and returns home with the ogres' hoarded treasure.
reenactment_hook: Recruit a party of neutral creatures by spending a small consumable — one ration each — to win their loyalty, then lead the recruited band to storm and clear an enemy stronghold, dividing the loot fairly at the end.

epithet_grants.trigger: Recruit three or more neutral creatures with rations and clear a stronghold with the recruited band

sources: Ozaki, Japanese Fairy Tales (1903); Thompson, Little Peachling (Hasegawa series, 1885) — PD

title: The Demon King's Poisoned Cup
summary: Shuten-dōji, the demon king of Mount Ōe, feasts on the blood and flesh of abducted nobles. His hunters come disguised as wandering mountain priests and accept his lavish hospitality, repaying it with jinbenkidoku-shu — a divine poison sake granted them by the gods, along with a protective helmet. Paralyzed by the sacred drink, the demon is beheaded; but his severed head flies up, still fighting, and bites down on the helmets stacked on the slayer's crown before it finally dies.
reenactment_hook: Infiltrate a boss's lair disguised, passing as a guest to avoid combat; feed it a quest-item poison to inflict a paralysis/exhausted stack; behead it — then survive the second phase, where its severed head keeps attacking, by blocking its bite with a stacked-defense item you carried in for exactly that moment.

epithet_grants.epithet: the Guest Who Poured the Cup
epithet_grants.trigger: Clear a two-phase boss via disguised infiltration, a quest-item poison, and surviving the severed-head phase with a stacked-defense item, on a VIP table or higher

sources: Otogi-zōshi corpus; Ōeyama picture scrolls (Nanbokuchō–Muromachi, verified)

title: The Tengu King's Secret Teaching
summary: In the deep valleys of Mount Kurama dwelt Sōjōbō, king of the tengu and unrivaled master of the sword. He took the boy Ushiwakamaru, hiding at the mountain temple, as his secret pupil, drilling him night after night in Sōjōgatani until the child surpassed every human swordsman alive. The tengu teach only the worthy, and only in secret; the arrogant they instead abduct and humiliate — tengu-kakushi, 'spirited away by tengu'.
reenactment_hook: Find a hidden trainer NPC on a secret map tile and prove your worth by clearing a skill-check trial without arrogance — for example, winning it while using no consumables — earning a permanent new combat skill that persists for the rest of the run.

epithet_grants.trigger: Complete a hidden trainer's worthiness trial and gain a permanent new skill within the run

sources: Sōjōbō of Kurama sword-teaching legend (yokai.com; Kurama-dera tradition, verified)

title: Quetzalcoatl steals the bones of the dead from Mictlan
summary: To make the humanity of the new age, Quetzalcoatl descends into Mictlan (accompanied by his dog-twin Xolotl) to fetch the bones of the previous people. Mictlantecuhtli agrees but sets a rigged trial — sound a conch that has no holes — which Quetzalcoatl beats by having worms bore holes and bees hum inside. Fleeing with the bones he is ambushed by quail, falls into a pit, and the bones shatter into different sizes; the gods then grind them and bleed onto the meal, and humanity is reborn from the mix of relic and self-given blood.
reenactment_hook: Beat a rigged, unwinnable-as-stated trial by an unconventional workaround (not brute force), carry a fallen ally's remains out of a death-zone floor, then permanently spend a resource of your own (bleed HP / sacrifice an item) to revive them, and clear the run.

epithet_grants.trigger: out-cheat a rigged 'impossible' trial and revive a fallen ally by permanently spending your own resource, on a VIP table or higher

sources: Leyenda de los Soles (Codex Chimalpopoca; Bierhorst, History and Mythology of the Aztecs)

title: The mirror and the drink: Quetzalcoatl's fall from Tollan
summary: Tezcatlipoca sets out to unseat the priest-king Quetzalcoatl. He shows Quetzalcoatl his own aged, ruined face in a smoking mirror and then tempts the ascetic with pulque; drunk and disgraced on camera, Quetzalcoatl loses his standing. In shame he burns his palaces, buries his treasures, and departs east on a raft of serpents, vowing one day to return.
reenactment_hook: Topple a stronger, higher-ranked contestant using only a staged temptation and psychological pressure — show them a 'mirror' of their worst moment and offer the tainted gift — bringing about their public downfall without dealing a single point of damage.

epithet_grants.trigger: cause a stronger contestant's on-camera downfall through a staged temptation, dealing zero damage yourself

sources: Florentine Codex Book III (Sahagún; LOC digitization)

title: The tearing of Cipactli: earth and sky from the sea-monster
summary: Before the world had form, the primordial caiman-monster Cipactli swam the endless sea, mouths gnashing at every joint. Tezcatlipoca lowers his own foot into the water as bait and loses it to her bite; then he and Quetzalcoatl, in serpent form, seize her and tear her body in two — one half becomes the sky, the other the earth. Her sundered body remains alive and hungry, and must be fed to stay still.
reenactment_hook: Deliberately stake one of your own body parts (limb-HP) as bait to lure a colossal terrain-boss into exposing its weak point, then finish it with a coordinated two-point 'tear' executed from opposite edges of the arena.

epithet_grants.trigger: stake one of your own limbs as bait and bring down a colossal terrain-boss by a coordinated two-point tear

sources: Histoyre du Mechique (via Garibay, Teogonía e historia de los mexicanos); Tlaltecuhtli/Cipactli conflation flagged in dossier

title: The Five Suns: the ages of the world and their ruin
summary: The cosmos has passed through successive 'Suns' or ages, each ruled by a god and each destroyed in a distinct cataclysm — devouring jaguars, hurricane winds, a rain of fire, a world-flood. Tezcatlipoca presided over the first age, Tlaloc over the third; the present Fifth Sun was kindled by Nanahuatzin at Teotihuacan and, like the others, is doomed to end. The show that is the world only continues so long as the gods and humanity keep feeding it.
reenactment_hook: Survive every distinct era-cataclysm floor modifier in a single run (jaguar swarm, wind-strip, fire-rain, flood) — adapting your build to each in turn — and reach extraction without a party death.

epithet_grants.trigger: survive every era-cataclysm floor modifier in one run without a party death

sources: Leyenda de los Soles; Historia de los Mexicanos por sus Pinturas

title: The birth at Coatepec: Huitzilopochtli born armed
summary: Pregnant by a ball of feathers, Coatlicue is set upon by her daughter Coyolxauhqui, who leads the Four Hundred Southerners up Coatepec to kill her. At the summit Huitzilopochtli is born fully grown and fully armed; he seizes the fire-serpent Xiuhcoatl, dismembers Coyolxauhqui so her body tumbles broken down the mountain, and routs the four hundred. It is the daily victory of the sun over the stars, re-won each dawn.
reenactment_hook: Win a birth-ambush defense while badly outnumbered — hold a summit or choke against descending waves — and deliver a decisive dismembering blow to the wave-leader with a signature charged weapon.

epithet_grants.trigger: hold a summit outnumbered three-to-one or worse and dismember the wave-leader
land a full-charge dismembering sweep with the Xiuhcoatl relic to end the ambush

sources: Florentine Codex Book III ch. 1 (Sahagún; hemi.nyu.edu text; davidbowles.us translation)

title: Tlaloc's grief and the rain of fire
summary: Tlaloc rules the Third Sun as the indispensable giver of rain. When his wife Xochiquetzal is carried off by Tezcatlipoca, the grieving storm-god withholds his water and the world burns with drought; and when the people at last beg him, he answers not with life-giving rain but with a rain of fire that ends the age. Of his four jars, only one pours blessing — the others pour fire, blight, and cutting flint.
reenactment_hook: After a personal loss on-camera (an ally taken or a resource stripped), withhold your remaining power for a beat, then convert the setback into a single area-wide fire/storm devastation that clears the floor.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a storm floor by turning a withheld resource into an area-wide fire-rain finish after losing an ally on-camera

sources: Historia de los Mexicanos por sus Pinturas / Leyenda de los Soles; four-jars codical tradition (mexicolore Tlaloc PDF)

title: Five Flower presides over the game of patolli
summary: Macuilxochitl, 'Five Flower,' is the god of games and gambling, shown in the Codex Magliabechiano presiding over a patolli match in progress. Winnings at the bean-dice board were treated as his gift and the god was invoked on every throw. Under his patronage players staked ever higher — copper bells and jade, then homes and land, and at last their own freedom, gambling themselves into slavery.
reenactment_hook: Stake loot of real value at an in-dungeon game of chance, let the result stand (no reload, no take-back), and go on to clear the floor on the back of the outcome.

epithet_grants.trigger: stake real loot at a dungeon game of chance, let it ride, and clear the floor

sources: dossier §Macuilxochitl (graded folk_tale per its honesty note — ritual/practice account, not narrative)
Codex Magliabechiano; Durán, Book of the Gods and Rites; Sahagún Florentine Codex Book I

title: The flight of Xolotl from his own sacrifice
summary: When the newborn Fifth Sun demanded the death of all the gods to set it moving, Xolotl wept and refused to die. He fled, changing shape to hide — into a double maize stalk (xolotl), then a twinned maguey (mexolotl), and at last into the water-dwelling axolotl — but he was hunted down and sacrificed anyway. The etiology explains the doubled plants and the axolotl that still bears his name.
reenactment_hook: Evade a mandatory death-trigger or unwinnable fight by repeatedly changing form, stance, or loadout to slip each attempt, surviving the floor by flight rather than confrontation.

epithet_grants.trigger: survive a forced-death or unwinnable-fight floor by changing form/loadout at least three times to escape

sources: Sahagún Florentine Codex Book VII; Leyenda de los Soles

title: Nanahuatzin leaps into the fire and becomes the Sun
summary: In darkness at Teotihuacan the gods build a bonfire to make a new sun. The rich Tecciztecatl offers quetzal feathers and gold but balks four times at the flames; the poor, pustuled Nanahuatzin offers only reeds, maguey spines, and his own scabs — then shuts his eyes and jumps, becoming the Sun. Tecciztecatl leaps second into the cooled ash and becomes the dimmer Moon; the new sun then refuses to move until the assembled gods yield their own blood.
reenactment_hook: Make an irreversible, all-in commitment that a wealthier rival just declined — permanently spend your rarest item or a slice of your own being on a single decisive attempt — and take the largest payout on the board.

epithet_grants.trigger: commit an irreversible all-in that a rival contestant declined, on a VVIP or Forsaken table, and win the pot

sources: Sahagún Florentine Codex Book VII; Leyenda de los Soles

title: Heart of Sky speaks the world and shapes humanity from maize
summary: Huracan, Heart of Sky — the one-legged storm triad — and Sovereign Plumed Serpent (Q'uq'umatz) speak the earth into being over the primordial water. Their first people of mud slump and dissolve; their second people of wood move but cannot remember or thank their makers, and are wiped out. Only at the last, from ground maize, are true, grateful humans finally shaped.
reenactment_hook: Clear a floor's objective by construction and problem-solving rather than brute force — iterate through failed configurations (mud, then wood) and succeed on the corrected third attempt without a combat wipe.

epithet_grants.trigger: complete a floor objective by building/solving after two failed configurations, using no brute-force combat

sources: Popol Vuh (Christenson electronic translation, Mesoweb; Goetz–Morley text)

title: The flood of the wooden people and the revolt of the tools
summary: Judged for their ingratitude, the wooden people are destroyed by Heart of Sky in a black resin-rain and flood. Their own possessions rise against them — grinding-stones, water-jars, dogs, and turkeys all turn and take revenge for their misuse — while Camalotz the snatch-bat tears off their heads. The few that survive become the monkeys of the forest.
reenactment_hook: Wipe a floor's enemies using their own weapons, constructs, or summons turned against them — hack a spawner, reprogram a construct, or reflect a boss's own arsenal — so the tools do the killing.

epithet_grants.trigger: clear a floor by turning the enemies' own weapons or constructs against them

title: The Hero Twins bankrupt the Lords of Xibalba
summary: Summoned to the underworld like their murdered father, Hunahpu and Xbalanque survive the trial-houses (Dark, Razor, Cold, Jaguar, Bat) and send a mosquito ahead to sting each lord so the others cry out his name — learning the lords' true names and exposing the mannequin decoys. After Camazotz beheads Hunahpu (a carved squash serving as a replacement head), the twins let themselves be killed, are ground to bone and thrown in the river, and return disguised as vagabond performers whose act is sacrificing and reviving each other — until One Death and Seven Death beg to be done next, and the twins sacrifice them for good. Xibalba's worship is formally revoked.
reenactment_hook: Beat a boss-pair that cannot be out-damaged: scout their hidden true names / win-condition via a decoy or summon, deliberately lose a rigged exchange to bait them, then win by inducing them to trigger their own defeat.

epithet_grants.trigger: defeat a boss-pair by learning their true names and inducing them to volunteer their own defeat

sources: Popol Vuh Parts III–IV (Christenson; Tedlock rendering)

title: The blowgunning of the false sun Seven Macaw
summary: Before the true sun exists, the vain Seven Macaw (Vucub-Caquix) sets himself up as sun and moon on the strength of his jeweled teeth and glittering eyes. Hunahpu blowguns him from his perch but only breaks his jaw and wrenches off Hunahpu's arm in return; so the twins pose as humble travelling healers and, 'curing' him, pry out the gemstone teeth and eyes that were his radiance. Stripped of his false brilliance, the pretender dies.
reenactment_hook: Down a boastful boss's pride first: destroy its trophy adornments or buff-source with a precise ranged shot, then finish stripping its power by posing as a helper/healer before the kill.

epithet_grants.trigger: defeat a boastful boss by first destroying its trophy/adornment buffs with a precision ranged shot, then a helper ruse

title: The descent of the Cihuateteo at the crossroads
summary: Women who died in first childbirth are honored as fallen warriors and become the Cihuateteo, the Divine Women. On five marked day-signs they descend to haunt the crossroads, where roadside shrines received offerings to appease them; unappeased, they strike children with seizure and wasting and travelers with madness. The rest of the time they keep their eternal duty, escorting the sun from noon to sunset.
reenactment_hook: On a calendar-flagged floor, meet the crossroads apparitions and sacrifice a real equipped item as an offering to earn their escort (guaranteed safe extraction) — or refuse and brave a madness curse for the rest of the run.

epithet_grants.epithet: The Honored Ones of the Crossroads
epithet_grants.trigger: appease the crossroads apparitions with a real offering and complete the run under their escort

sources: Sahagún Florentine Codex Books I & IV; azteccalendar.com/god/Cihuateteo

title: The eagle on the cactus: the sign that founded Tenochtitlan
summary: Huitzilopochtli commands the wandering Mexica to seek a promised home, marked by an eagle perched on a nopal cactus. When his sister's son Copil rebels, the god has him killed and his heart cast far into Lake Texcoco; from that heart-rock the cactus grows. Generations into the migration the Mexica at last see the eagle on that cactus in the marsh and found Tenochtitlan on the spot — the omen that is now Mexico's national emblem.
reenactment_hook: Lead your party through a long multi-floor trek, reading an in-run omen or sign to locate a hidden 'promised' objective, and claim it by planting your standard exactly where the sign appears.

epithet_grants.trigger: follow an in-run omen across the run to the hidden promised location and claim it

sources: Durán, Historia de las Indias de Nueva España; Crónica Mexicayotl (Tezozomoc)
mexicolore.co.uk 'Tenochtitlan founding — the signs'; en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Huitzilopochtli (verified one level down)
TOP-UP: not in dossier's forward-declared table — added so Huitzilopochtli has ≥2 myths

title: The four-year road of the dead through the nine levels of Mictlan
summary: The ordinary dead — those not taken by battle, childbirth, or water — must walk four years through the nine hazard-levels of Mictlan to reach Mictlantecuhtli and final rest. At the first river, Apanohuaya, the soul is ferried across by a xoloitzcuintli dog burned with it; further stations set clashing mountains, an obsidian wind, and other trials, passed only with the talismans and charms buried alongside the body. At the ninth level the soul finds rest.
reenactment_hook: Clear a nine-stage underworld hazard-gauntlet floor without dying, carrying the correct prepared items/charms to pass each obstacle in turn, and using a companion or summon to ferry you across the impassable river-stage.

epithet_grants.trigger: clear a nine-stage underworld gauntlet without dying, using the correct prepared talismans

sources: Sahagún Florentine Codex Book III appendix (nine levels of Mictlan)
mexicolore.co.uk 'The Nine Levels of Mictlan'; wilderutopia.com 'Mythological Journey to the Aztec Lands of the Dead'
TOP-UP: not in dossier's forward-declared table — added so Mictlantecuhtli has ≥2 myths

title: Five Flower's feast and the affliction of the fast-breakers
summary: The feast of Macuilxochitl demanded a four-day fast with strict sexual abstinence before the fifth-day celebration. Those who defiled the fast were said to have 'brought their fasting to naught,' and the god sent them merited, humiliating punishments — boils, piles, hemorrhoids, and venereal disease. Making vows and fasting to Five Flower again was the path to relief.
reenactment_hook: Hold a self-imposed challenge condition — a 'fast': no consumables, or no repeated tactic — through a full floor despite on-camera temptation, and win; breaking it visibly saddles you with a humiliating debuff for the cameras.

epithet_grants.trigger: keep a self-imposed fast/challenge condition through a full floor and still win

sources: Sahagún Florentine Codex Book I (Macuilxochitl feast, fasting taboo, and disease-affliction)
Miller & Taube, Illustrated Dictionary of the Gods and Symbols of Ancient Mexico and the Maya; nahuatl.wired-humanities.org/content/macuilxochitl
TOP-UP: distinct from the patolli record — added so Macuilxochitl has ≥2 myths

title: Inanna descends to the Great Below and pays her way back with a substitute
summary: Inanna descends to the underworld, and at each of the seven gates a piece of her regalia is stripped away until she arrives naked and powerless before her sister Ereshkigal. The Anunnaki judge her, kill her, and hang her corpse on a hook. Enki fashions two sexless beings from the dirt of his fingernails who revive her with the food and water of life, but the Below demands a substitute in her place — and Inanna, finding her husband Dumuzid unmourning on his throne, hands him to the galla.
reenactment_hook: Walk an approach corridor that forces you to permanently surrender one equipped item at each threshold, arrive at the boss stripped of your gear, be downed, and still win the run — then, when the run demands a death, transfer that debt onto another party member or a sacrificial NPC rather than paying it yourself.

epithet_grants.trigger: Complete the strip-at-every-gate approach, be downed once, and return to win the encounter on a VIP table or higher
Also pass the run's death/penalty onto a substitute so you personally take zero permanent loss, on a VVIP or Forsaken table

sources: ETCSL t.1.4.1 Inana's Descent; Akkadian Descent of Ishtar (Dalley); web-verified 2026-07-18

title: Inanna drinks Enki under the table and sails off with the me
summary: Inanna sails to Enki's temple at Eridu and challenges the lord of wisdom to a drinking contest. Once he is thoroughly drunk, she coaxes from him the me — the sacred decrees underpinning all of civilization — and loads them aboard the Boat of Heaven. Sobered and furious, Enki sends wave after wave of monsters to seize the boat, but Inanna's minister Ninshubur fends off each one, and the me remain with Uruk for good.
reenactment_hook: Win a high-stakes social/endurance contest against a superior NPC by out-lasting them (a wager or consumable-endurance challenge), take their signature item or power as the pot, then hold onto it through the retaliation wave the loser sends after you before you can extract.

epithet_grants.trigger: Win the contest, take the opponent's signature asset, and survive every pursuit wave with the prize still in hand on a VIP table or higher

sources: ETCSL t.1.3.1 Inana and Enki; web-verified 2026-07-18

title: The Bull of Heaven and the haunch thrown in a goddess's face
summary: When Gilgamesh spurns Ishtar's marriage offer and recites her ruined former lovers to her face, she storms to heaven and demands the Bull of Heaven, threatening to smash the gates of the underworld and raise the dead unless Anu complies. The Bull descends on Uruk, its snorts opening pits that swallow hundreds. Gilgamesh and Enkidu corner and slay it, and Enkidu tears off its haunch and hurls it in Ishtar's face.
reenactment_hook: Grapple-slay a rampaging large/colossal beast in melee with no ranged weapon and no party member landing the kill, then immediately perform a defiant on-camera taunt (throw a trophy piece) at the god or table sponsor who sent it against you.

epithet_grants.trigger: Land the killing blow on the beast in melee, then taunt its sender on camera in the same encounter, VIP table or higher

sources: Standard Babylonian Gilgamesh VI; ETCSL t.1.8.1.2 Gilgamesh and the Bull of Heaven

title: Enki warns the reed wall and the wise man survives the flood
summary: The gods cast lots to divide the cosmos, then create humankind from clay and a slain god's blood to carry their labor. When humanity grows too numerous and loud, Enlil sends plague (which withdraws when all offerings are directed to the shamed plague-officer alone), then drought, then a world-drowning flood. Bound by oath not to warn any human, Enki speaks his warning to a reed wall behind which the wise Atrahasis sleeps, who builds a boat and rides out the deluge.
reenactment_hook: Obtain smuggled foreknowledge of a run-wide wipe/reset mechanic from a lore object or a leaking NPC, and pre-build the one countermeasure that lets you alone survive the event that clears the board — or, against a hazard-officer enemy, redirect all its aggro/'offerings' onto itself so it withdraws instead of firing.

epithet_grants.trigger: Survive a board-clearing wipe event by acting on smuggled foreknowledge, on a VIP table or higher
Do so having built the countermeasure before the wipe was ever telegraphed to other contestants, on a VVIP or Forsaken table

title: Adapa breaks the South Wind's wing and refuses the bread of life
summary: Adapa, the sage of Eridu and Enki's servant, snaps the wing of the South Wind when it capsizes his fishing boat, and is summoned before the sky-god Anu to answer for it. Enki coaches him to accept nothing offered, warning that the bread and water set before him will be of death. But the impressed Anu offers the bread and water of life instead — and by obeying his patron's counsel Adapa refuses immortality, and dooms humankind to death.
reenactment_hook: Cripple a flying/storm hazard by breaking its 'wing' (disable a flying enemy's mobility), then, when offered a powerful consumable buff mid-run, refuse it on an NPC advisor's warning — passing up the run's single best boon by deliberate choice — and still clear the run.

epithet_grants.trigger: Disable a flying hazard by its wing and then decline the run's best offered consumable on advice, winning the run anyway

title: Enki's clay turtle digs a pit for the greedy champion
summary: After Ninurta recovers the stolen Tablet of Destinies, dropping it into Enki's watery Abzu, Enki praises him lavishly — but the war-god, unsatisfied, covets the Tablet for himself and begins plotting against his host. Enki fashions a turtle from the clay of the Abzu; it seizes Ninurta's tendon from behind, scrapes open an evil pit, and the hero tumbles in. Enki gloats over the humbling of the proud champion.
reenactment_hook: Defeat a stronger, over-confident enemy purely by luring it onto a trap or into terrain you prepared (a pit, a collapse, a hazard), landing zero direct damage yourself — the engineer's kill against a foe that out-muscles you.

epithet_grants.trigger: Down a physically superior enemy via a prepared trap or terrain with no direct-damage instances from you

sources: ETCSL t.1.6.3 Ninurta and the Turtle; web-verified 2026-07-18

title: Marduk names his price, then slays Tiamat and builds the world
summary: When the primordial waters stir to war, Ea slays Apsu, and the mother-sea Tiamat raises eleven venom-filled monsters led by Qingu, whom she crowns with the Tablet of Destinies. The young god Marduk agrees to face her only after the divine assembly grants him supreme, irrevocable command as his price. He wedges her maw open with the winds, shoots an arrow into her heart, splits her body into sky and earth, and fashions humankind from the blood of the executed Qingu.
reenactment_hook: Negotiate a wagered supreme reward from the sponsor BEFORE engaging, then solo-kill a colossal boss whose exterior is invulnerable by forcing its mouth open with a wind/gale effect and striking the exposed interior in that window — landing the finisher yourself, and delivering on every clause of the pre-fight bargain.

epithet_grants.trigger: Complete the negotiate-then-slay-the-invulnerable-primordial deed on a VVIP or Forsaken table
Also honor every clause of the pre-fight wager within the same run

sources: L. W. King, Seven Tablets of Creation (1902 PD), Enuma Elish I-VI; web-verified 2026-07-18

title: Erra talks Marduk off his throne and burns the world for the thrill
summary: Erra, god of war and plague, grows restless for slaughter and persuades Marduk to step down from his throne in Babylon so his tarnished regalia can be cleansed. With cosmic order left unguarded, Erra looses the Sibitti — his seven merciless warriors — and devastates the land with war and pestilence. Only his vizier Ishum, with patient counsel, finally talks him down and lets order be restored.
reenactment_hook: Talk or bait a boss (or the reigning table sponsor) into leaving its post/arena through a persuasion or lure play that removes the strongest enemy from the field, then rampage through the now-undefended encounters and clear them unopposed before order can be restored.

epithet_grants.trigger: Lure the strongest enemy off the field by persuasion/bait, then clear the undefended encounters in the same run, VIP table or higher
Do so having a damage-over-time condition ticking on every enemy you kill during the rampage

title: Nergal storms the underworld and takes its queen at knifepoint
summary: Nergal insults the underworld's herald Namtar by refusing to rise for him at the gods' banquet, and is sent down to answer for it, armed with fourteen demons. Posting two demons to hold each of the seven gates open behind him, he storms the throne room, drags Ereshkigal from her seat by the hair, and raises his blade. She buys her life by offering marriage and a share of her realm — and later, when he flees back to heaven, she threatens to raise the dead to outnumber the living until he is returned to her for good.
reenactment_hook: Assault a boss's throne-arena through its successive gates, holding or spiking each chokepoint you pass so it cannot be sealed behind you, then overpower the boss in melee and convert the win into a binding alliance (recruit or pact) instead of a kill.

epithet_grants.trigger: Breach and hold every gate of a throne-arena, subdue the boss in melee, and take it allied rather than dead, VIP table or higher

sources: Nergal and Ereshkigal, Amarna + Sultantepe versions (Dalley); web-verified 2026-07-18

title: The seven auras of Humbaba, traded away and then betrayed
summary: Gilgamesh and Enkidu march to the Cedar Forest to kill its guardian Humbaba and win eternal fame. Humbaba is wrapped in seven auras of terror that no weapon can pierce, but he can be coaxed into trading them away one by one for offered gifts, and Shamash raises thirteen winds that pin him fast once the last aura is gone. Humbaba begs for his life on his knees; the heroes behead him anyway, and Enlil rages at the killing of his appointed guardian.
reenactment_hook: Strip a guardian boss's stacked invulnerability auras one at a time by offering it tribute or promises (each accepted offer sheds one layer) instead of trying to damage it, use a summoned environmental effect to pin it once the last layer is gone, land the finisher — then choose whether to spare it when it begs on camera.

epithet_grants.trigger: Shed all of a guardian boss's invulnerability auras by tribute and finish it in the pinning window, VIP table or higher

sources: ETCSL t.1.8.1.5 Gilgamesh and Huwawa A (auras traded one by one); SB Gilgamesh V

title: Dumuzid's death-dream and the gazelle's flight from the galla
summary: The shepherd-god Dumuzid dreams of his own death and wakes to find the galla-demons closing in to drag him to the underworld. He prays to the sun-god Utu, who transforms his hands and feet into a gazelle's so he can bolt and hide. He slips the demons again and again, but they catch him at the last and take him down to the Great Below.
reenactment_hook: When a target is marked for death (a doom-timer or a pursuing enemy pack locks on), grant that target — yourself or an ally — an escape burst of speed/evasion and break line of sight to slip the pursuers at least once before they can execute the mark.

epithet_grants.trigger: Break a lethal doom-lock off a marked target with a granted escape and evade the pursuit at least once in the same encounter

title: Anzu steals the Tablet and is undone at 'wing to wing'
summary: The thunderbird Anzu, trusted to guard the door while Enlil bathes, snatches the Tablet of Destinies and flies off, and with it he can unmake any attack — arrows revert to reed and feather in mid-air. The gods send Ninurta, who cannot land a blow until Ea reveals the trick: scatter Anzu's own wing-feathers on a gale, and when the bird calls 'wing to wing' to recall them, his summons draws Ninurta's arrow in with the feathers. The bird falls and the Tablet is recovered.
reenactment_hook: Defeat a boss that negates or reverts your attacks (an invulnerability or reflect phase) by discovering the brief self-recovery/recall window when its defense drops — disarm or scatter its defense with a gale/disruptor, then time your strike to land exactly during that recall animation, and recover the stolen objective it holds.

epithet_grants.trigger: Kill a decree-reverting boss during its recall/recovery window and return the stolen objective to its owner, VIP table or higher

title: Ninurta slays Asag and judges the army of stones
summary: The demon Asag, spawned in the mountains and served by an army of living stones, threatens the ordered world, and Ninurta marches out with his talking mace Sharur, who scouts ahead and counsels him. Ninurta slays Asag, then reorganizes the world he has won — damming the destroying waters into the Tigris and passing judgment on the stones, blessing the useful ones and cursing the rest for having sided with Asag.
reenactment_hook: Beat a boss that endlessly spawns a stone/minion army by converting the adds instead of out-damaging them — flip a portion of the summoned mob to your side (or repurpose them as cover/tools) and neutralize the rest — after fully scouting the arena first, then finish the boss.

epithet_grants.trigger: Down an add-spawning boss by converting rather than grinding its minion army, having scouted the arena before engaging, VIP table or higher

sources: Lugal-e, ETCSL t.1.6.2 (Ninurta's Exploits); game bible already uses Asag/Offspring of Asag

title: The gods' invoice: one of the pair must die
summary: In council the gods rule that because Gilgamesh and Enkidu together slew both Humbaba and the Bull of Heaven, one of the pair must die — and it will not be the two-thirds-divine king. Enkidu, the clay-born wild man, dreams of the dark house of dust that awaits him, then falls sick and wastes away over twelve days. He dies cursing and then blessing the fate that made him, and Gilgamesh's grief sends him onto the road to seek deathlessness.
reenactment_hook: After a party lands a shared/co-op kill that incurs a penalty (a curse, a debt, a doom-timer), have one member voluntarily absorb the entire penalty for the group — taking a real, permanent loss such as a downed slot or a stat drain — so the rest of the party continues the run clean.

epithet_grants.trigger: Solo-absorb a party-wide penalty from a shared kill and let the run be completed by the surviving members, VIP table or higher

title: The Plant of Heartbeat, won at the world's edge and lost to a snake
summary: Shattered by Enkidu's death, Gilgamesh journeys to the edge of the world, past the scorpion-guarded gate of Mashu and the alewife Siduri, and crosses the Waters of Death with the ferryman Urshanabi to reach Utnapishtim, sole survivor of the Flood. He fails the test to stay awake for seven days, but is told of a thorny plant on the sea-floor that restores youth. He dives and wins it — then a snake eats it while he bathes, and he returns to Uruk empty-handed but wise.
reenactment_hook: Undertake a long, high-attrition journey past a gate that turns other contestants back, win a unique consumable prize by retrieving it at real risk (a dive or hazard grab), then either guard it to the run's end or lose it to a theft event — completing the run wiser even if you finish empty-handed.

epithet_grants.trigger: Complete the full pass-the-impassable-gate, win-the-prize, then-guard-or-lose-it arc and finish the run, on a VVIP or Forsaken table

sources: Standard Babylonian Gilgamesh IX-XI (George; Thompson 1928 PD)

title: Odin pledges his eye at Mímir's well
summary: Odin comes to the well beneath Yggdrasil whose waters hold wisdom and understanding, guarded by the wise Mímir. Mímir grants a single drink only for a price: Odin drops one of his own eyes into the well and drinks. He is half-blind ever after, and wiser than any being alive.
reenactment_hook: Permanently sacrifice a stat, skill, or item of real value in exchange for hidden information (a boss's win condition, the run's secret objective, hidden map intel), then win the run using that knowledge.

epithet_grants.trigger: permanently sacrifice a stat, skill, or item of real value for hidden information and then win the run on a VIP table or higher

title: Odin hangs nine nights on the world-tree to win the runes
summary: Wounded by his own spear and 'given to Odin, myself to myself,' Odin hangs on the wind-torn tree Yggdrasil for nine nights with neither bread nor horn. At the last, peering down and screaming, he seizes the runes and falls back — winning the secrets of magic through pure self-inflicted endurance.
reenactment_hook: Voluntarily enter a severe self-imposed deprivation state (a disabled limb, no items, no healing, suspended in a hazard zone) and endure it for a set duration to unlock a hidden rune-skill or the run's secret, then win with it.

epithet_grants.trigger: endure a full self-imposed deprivation state to its timer, unlock the hidden reward, and win the run

title: Odin wagers his head against the giant Vafþrúðnir
summary: Disguised as the wanderer Gagnráðr, Odin enters the hall of the wisest giant Vafþrúðnir and stakes his head on a contest of lore. They trade cosmological questions until Odin asks what only he can know — what he whispered in the ear of his dead son Baldr on the pyre. The giant concedes he has wagered against Odin himself, and forfeits.
reenactment_hook: Stake something irreversible on a knowledge contest with an NPC dealer or boss, survive the riddle-gauntlet, and win by deploying a piece of intel only you could possess — a run-secret you discovered earlier that no opponent can hold.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a wager-backed knowledge duel against a boss or dealer using a secret only you discovered this run

title: Odin steals the mead of poetry
summary: The mead brewed from the blood of the wise being Kvasir is hoarded by the giant Suttung under the mountain Hnitbjörg and guarded by his daughter Gunnlöd. Odin bores through the rock as a serpent, spends three nights with Gunnlöd for the price of three sips, then drains all three vessels in three draughts. He turns into an eagle and flies the mead home to Asgard with Suttung, also eagle-shaped, in pursuit.
reenactment_hook: Infiltrate a sealed vault by a non-combat route, charm or bargain past its single guardian for a strictly bounded grant, then take far more than the deal allowed and escape by a fast mobility route before the owner triggers.

epithet_grants.trigger: charm past a vault's lone guardian and escape with the full prize (not just the bargained share) on a VIP table or higher

title: Ragnarök — the doom of the gods
summary: The gods know the day is fixed: the wolf Fenrir breaks free and swallows Odin, whom his son Víðarr avenges; Thor and the World Serpent Jörmungandr kill each other, Thor walking nine steps from the venom before he falls; Tyr and the hound Garm drop together; Loki and Heimdall slay one another. Surtr's fire drowns the world, which afterward rises renewed. The seating chart of who kills whom is published in advance, and the gods march to it anyway.
reenactment_hook: With the run's fatal ending pre-revealed (a doom timer or an unsurvivable boss), commit to the fight anyway and complete the objective before you fall — a true Forsaken all-in with your contestant's continued existence as the stake.

epithet_grants.trigger: complete a run whose fatal outcome was revealed in advance, finishing the objective before elimination
stake your contestant's continued existence on a Forsaken all-in and clear the table

title: The rigged contests of Útgarða-Loki
summary: Thor and his companions accept a series of contests in the hall of the giant-king Útgarða-Loki and lose every one: Loki is out-eaten, Þjálfi out-run, and Thor cannot drain a drinking-horn, lift a grey cat off the floor, or wrestle down an old woman. Afterward the king reveals each was a rigged illusion — the horn's far end was the sea, the cat was the World Serpent, the crone was Old Age herself — and that they came far closer than any could. Then he and his castle vanish before Thor's hammer can fall.
reenactment_hook: Against a rigged-contest boss whose challenges are unwinnable as presented, refuse the losing frame: inspect the props, identify the disguised true nature of each challenge, and NAME it on camera to collapse the illusion and pay out — victory by identification, not performance.

epithet_grants.trigger: defeat a rigged-contest boss by correctly naming its disguised challenges rather than out-performing them

title: Thor fishes for the World Serpent
summary: Thor rows far out to sea with the giant Hymir, baits an enormous hook with the head of Hymir's greatest ox, and lands the World Serpent Jörmungandr, hauling its venom-blowing head to the gunwale so hard his feet punch through the boat. As Thor lifts his hammer for the killing blow, the terrified Hymir cuts the line, and the serpent sinks back into the deep — its death deferred to Ragnarök.
reenactment_hook: Bait an unreachable, arena-scale boss out of its safe zone with a lure (an ox-head decoy), then win a raw strength or hold-check to drag it into the kill zone before your support panics and cuts the line.

epithet_grants.trigger: bait an arena-scale boss out of an unreachable zone and drag it into the kill zone with a raw strength check

title: Thor in bridal veil recovers Mjölnir from Þrym
summary: The giant Þrym steals Thor's hammer and demands the goddess Freyja as his bride in ransom. Freyja refuses in a fury so violent her necklace Brísingamen bursts, so Thor himself goes disguised as the bride with Loki as his handmaid. At the wedding, when the hammer is laid in 'her' lap to hallow the union, Thor seizes it and kills Þrym and the whole giant wedding party.
reenactment_hook: Recover a stolen legendary item by infiltrating the thief's stronghold in disguise (accept a humiliating cover identity on camera), retrieve your signature item at the ceremony's key moment, then turn it on the room and win the fight.

epithet_grants.trigger: recover a stolen legendary item from a boss's stronghold while disguised, then win the fight that follows

title: Loki wagers his head to the dwarf smiths
summary: To cover a prank, Loki bets his own head that the dwarf smiths Brokkr and Sindri cannot out-forge the Sons of Ívaldi. They forge Mjölnir, the ring Draupnir, and the boar Gullinbursti, and the gods judge Mjölnir the greatest — Loki loses. He escapes decapitation on a technicality (he wagered his head, not his neck), and the furious dwarf sews his lips shut instead.
reenactment_hook: Accept a wager with a body-part or permanent-asset stake, lose the contest, then escape the full penalty by exploiting the literal wording of the deal — and still finish the run at a net profit.

epithet_grants.trigger: lose a body-part or permanent-stake wager but escape the penalty through the deal's literal wording, and still win the run

title: The death of Baldr and Hel's ransom
summary: Baldr the beloved dreams of his death, so his mother Frigg exacts an oath from all things not to harm him — overlooking only the mistletoe. Loki guides the blind god Höðr to throw a mistletoe dart, killing Baldr. Hermóðr rides to Hel to ransom him; Hel agrees only if every thing in creation weeps — and one giantess, Þökk (Loki in disguise), refuses: 'let Hel hold what she has.' Baldr stays among the dead, and the gods' doom is sealed.
reenactment_hook: Defeat an enemy flagged invulnerable by discovering and exploiting the single overlooked exception to its protection (the one thing that never swore the oath); or, as the house side, hold a 'everyone must X' escrow and win by being the lone holdout who voids it.

epithet_grants.trigger: kill an enemy flagged invulnerable by exploiting the single overlooked exception to its protection
as the sole holdout, void a 'unanimous' escrow condition and keep the entire pot

title: The binding of Loki
summary: After Baldr's death and the flyting, the gods hunt Loki, who hides as a salmon but is caught in the very fishing-net he invented. They bind him across three stones with the entrails of his son Narfi, turned to iron, and Skaði fixes a venomous serpent to drip poison on his face. His wife Sigyn holds a bowl to catch the venom; when she turns to empty it, the drops make him writhe so violently the earth quakes. There he lies until Ragnarök.
reenactment_hook: Submit to a fully-bound, immobilized state under continuous unavoidable damage (a venom-drip DoT you cannot escape) and survive it to the encounter timer, relying on one teammate to intermittently soak the drip (Sigyn's bowl) — endurance of a trap you cannot undo.

epithet_grants.trigger: survive a fully-bound helpless state under continuous unavoidable damage until the encounter clock runs out

title: Loki's flyting — the roast of the gods
summary: At the sea-giant Ægir's feast, Loki forces his way in and delivers a devastating insult to every god and goddess present — exposing Odin's unmanly magic, Freyja's affairs, Gefjon's bargain, and all the rest, matching each boast with a shaming truth. None can silence him with words; only Thor's arrival and the threat of the hammer finally drives him from the hall.
reenactment_hook: Win a flyting / insult camera-duel: in a social-hype boss encounter, out-taunt a whole table of rivals in a turn-based war of words, landing a true shaming hit on each and banking audience hype, and exit on your own terms before the 'hammer' lands.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a turn-based flyting duel against a full table of rivals, landing a hit on each one

title: The binding of Fenrir and Tyr's hand
summary: The wolf Fenrir grows so fast the gods fear him and try to bind him for sport twice; he snaps both chains. For the third fetter — Gleipnir, silk-thin and dwarf-forged from six impossible things — the wary wolf agrees only if a god places a hand in his jaws as a pledge of good faith. Tyr alone does so, knowing the pledge is a lie; the fetter holds, and Fenrir bites off Tyr's right hand. Everyone laughs except Tyr, who pays without a word.
reenactment_hook: Permanently sacrifice a body part / disable a limb-slot as the price of neutralizing an un-killable beast-boss: assemble the fetter's impossible components, then volunteer a limb to a run-permanent disabled condition so the bind holds — locking the boss out of the fight instead of losing a damage race.

epithet_grants.trigger: permanently disable one of your own limb-slots to bind an un-killable boss, then win the run
bind rather than kill a beast-class boss by turning its own trust into the binding

title: Freyja weeps tears of red gold for Óðr
summary: Freyja's husband Óðr departs on long journeys and leaves her behind. In her grief she weeps tears of red gold and travels under many names among strange peoples searching for him. Her sorrow is the origin of the poets' kenning for gold as 'Freyja's tears.'
reenactment_hook: Convert your own losses into value: carry a grief-scaling relic ('Freyja's Tears') whose worth grows each time you take a real loss (a downed ally, a sacrificed limb, a broken item), press on across the whole map despite mounting losses, and cash it out richer at extraction.

epithet_grants.trigger: finish a run having converted repeated real losses into a net gain via a grief-scaling relic

title: Gefjon ploughs Zealand out of Sweden
summary: King Gylfi rewards the wandering Gefjon with as much land as four oxen can plough in a day and a night. She fetches four oxen — her own sons by a giant — and drives the plough so deep and hard that it cuts the land loose; she hauls the whole tract into the sea, names it Zealand, and leaves a lake where it had been. She beat the grant by controlling what 'four oxen' meant.
reenactment_hook: Turn a bounded reward into an outsized haul by exploiting its literal wording: accept a reward capped by a vague unit ('as much as X can do'), satisfy that definition with disproportionately powerful means you prepared in advance, and extract a payout far beyond the house's intent.

epithet_grants.trigger: exploit the literal wording of a bounded reward to extract an outsized payout, then survive to extraction

title: Sigurd slays Fáfnir and tastes the heart-blood
summary: Advised by a disguised old man (Odin), Sigurd digs a trench in the dragon Fáfnir's path and drives his sword up into the beast's soft underside as it crawls over him. Roasting the heart for Reginn, he burns his thumb and sucks it, and the taste of the heart-blood lets him understand the birds — who warn that Reginn means to kill him. Sigurd beheads Reginn and takes the cursed hoard, which is doomed to destroy him.
reenactment_hook: Ambush a beast-class boss from concealment below its path (dig in or hide, strike the unarmored underside on its pass), then consume part of the slain boss to unlock hidden intelligence — revealed enemy telegraphs/intents for the rest of the run — and act on a revealed betrayal before it lands.

epithet_grants.trigger: ambush and kill a beast-class boss from concealment, then consume it to reveal hidden enemy intel and win the run

title: Beowulf fights Grendel bare-handed
summary: The monster Grendel has terrorized the hall Heorot for years. Beowulf vows to meet him with no sword or shield, since Grendel bears no weapons, and grapples him bare-handed when he attacks by night. In the struggle Beowulf tears the fiend's arm from its socket; Grendel flees to die in the fens, and the arm is hung in the hall as proof.
reenactment_hook: Defeat a fiend-class enemy with no weapon equipped, deliberately matching the enemy's own handicap (unarmed vs unarmed) — a pure audience-hype sporting play — and win the grapple.

epithet_grants.trigger: defeat a fiend-class boss with no weapon equipped, matching its handicap, and bank the fair-fight hype

title: Beowulf's last fight against the dragon
summary: Fifty years a king, the aged Beowulf goes to face a fire-drake that a stolen cup has enraged. His retainers flee; only the young Wiglaf stands with him. Together they kill the dragon, but its venomous bite is mortal — Beowulf wins the treasure and the victory, and dies of the winning.
reenactment_hook: On a retired or maxed-out contestant, enter one final run with the fatal odds revealed, secure the objective (the treasure / win condition), and accept elimination as the price — a mortal-side Forsaken all-in, ideally with a single loyal ally standing when the rest flee.

epithet_grants.trigger: win a final run's objective on a doomed contestant and accept elimination as the cost, with one ally still at your side when others flee

title: The Norns allot the fates
summary: Beneath the world-tree at the Well of Urðr sit three maidens — Urðr, Verðandi, Skuld — who carve laws and allot the lives and fates of all beings, gods included. They water Yggdrasil daily and weave the fate-threads of newborn heroes. No appeal reaches them; even the gods must ride the odds the Norns post.
reenactment_hook: Draw an immutable fate-thread (a run modifier no patron boon can reroll or buy off), accept it without complaint on camera — a harmful one earns audience-sympathy hype — decline every offered reroll, and clear the run under the handicap.

epithet_grants.trigger: accept an immutable harmful run-modifier with no reroll, refuse every reroll offered, and still win the run

title: Odin casts Hel into Niflheim and gives her the dead
summary: When the gods learn that Loki's three monstrous children are being raised in Jötunheimr, Odin has them brought to him and casts Hel down into Niflheim, granting her authority over nine worlds to apportion lodging to all who die of sickness or old age. She rules from her hall Éljúðnir ('Sleet-Cold'), her dish named Hunger and her knife Famine, half her body flesh-coloured and half blue-black — the licensed keeper of the ordinary dead.
reenactment_hook: Claim and hold a fixed zone of the dungeon as your own domain: convert every enemy defeated inside it into a tribute stream, and impose a non-negotiable toll on any contestant or boon that tries to enter or extract from it, refusing all renegotiation for the rest of the run.

epithet_grants.trigger: claim and hold a dungeon zone under a non-negotiable toll for the rest of the run

title: Māui snares the sun to slow the day
summary: Māui and his brothers plait strong flax nooses and lie in ambush at the pit from which the sun rises. As Tama-nui-te-rā climbs out they snare him fast, and Māui beats him with the enchanted jawbone of Muri-ranga-whenua until, crippled, the sun agrees to crawl slowly across the sky — giving people long days to work, fish, and cook.
reenactment_hook: Neutralize a run's countdown or enrage timer not by racing it but by physically trapping and crippling the mechanism that drives it (snare and beat the boss's haste, jam the clock), then complete the timed objective at your own pace.

epithet_grants.trigger: disable a timed objective's clock by force and clear the objective with the timer neutralized, on a VIP table or higher

title: Māui fishes up the land
summary: Refused bait by his brothers, Māui strikes his own nose and smears his blood on the great hook Manaiakalani, pointed with the jawbone of Muri-ranga-whenua. He casts into the deep and hauls up a vast fish — the North Island, Te Ika-a-Māui. Told to wait while he appeases the gods, his brothers instead hack at the catch, and its writhing throws up the mountains and valleys of the land.
reenactment_hook: Bait a terrain-raising relic or objective with your own blood — self-inflict a real HP/bleeding cost — then haul a new platform, causeway, or landmass up out of water or void to open an impossible route, and win the run using the ground you made.

epithet_grants.trigger: raise decisive terrain by paying a blood cost and complete the run via the new ground, on a VIP table or higher

title: Māui steals fire from Mahuika
summary: Māui quenches every fire in the village, then begs his ancestress Mahuika for more. She draws fire from a burning fingernail; he secretly drowns it and returns for another, again and again, until she has torn out nine. Realizing the con, she hurls her last fire after him; the world blazes, Māui flees as a hawk, and only rain saves him — the seed of fire taking refuge in the kaikōmako and other trees, which is why fire is now drilled from wood.
reenactment_hook: By repeated feints and sweet-talked requests, bait a boss or resource-keeper into spending its power charge by charge until only its last remains, then survive the all-out retaliation it unleashes the moment it realizes it has been conned.

epithet_grants.trigger: drain a boss's resource pool to its final charge by deception and survive the ensuing retaliation, on a VIP table or higher

title: The death of Māui in the body of the Night
summary: To win immortality for humankind, Māui tries to reverse birth and death by passing through the sleeping Hine-nui-te-pō — entering between her thighs to emerge from her mouth. His bird companions must keep silent, but the pīwakawaka (fantail) laughs; she wakes and crushes him between her obsidian thighs. Māui becomes the first being to die, and death stays in the world forever.
reenactment_hook: Attempt the run's ultimate all-in — stake your own permadeath to seize a prize the house calls impossible (true immortality or a one-time anti-death lock) — by executing a flawless silent infiltration of a sleeping death-tier boss; a single noise from any ally fails the whole gambit.

epithet_grants.trigger: complete a silent infiltration kill on a sleeping death-tier boss and claim an immortality/anti-death boon without a companion breaking silence, on a VVIP table

title: Pele and Kamapuaʻa fight to a draw and split the islands
summary: The fire goddess Pele and the hog-man Kamapuaʻa wage war across the islands — she with lava and her fire-keeper Lonomakua, he with storm and rain. Neither can win: if he drowns her fire he can never have her, and if she burns him she starves without his rains. At the stalemate they divide the land, the wet windward Koʻolau sides going to Kamapuaʻa and the dry leeward Kona sides to Pele.
reenactment_hook: Fight a rival contestant or team to a mutually-survivable stalemate — neither able to finish the other — then broker an on-camera division of the arena's territory and each hold your half to the run's end.

epithet_grants.epithet: Ka wahine ʻai honua (the earth-eating woman)
epithet_grants.trigger: end a run holding a territory won by fighting a rival to an on-camera draw and controlling your half, on a VIP table or higher

title: Pele, Hiʻiaka, and the burning of the lehua groves
summary: Pele sends her devoted sister Hiʻiaka on a long, perilous errand to fetch the chief Lohiau, each swearing a bond: Hiʻiaka to return within a set time, Pele to spare Hiʻiaka's beloved lehua groves and her friend Hopoe. Pele breaks faith first — impatient, she burns the groves and kills Hopoe. Learning of the betrayal, Hiʻiaka claims Lohiau in full view of Pele, and the sisters' war resets the balance of power among the gods.
reenactment_hook: Accept an escort or retrieval contract sealed by a mutual on-camera oath, deliver the target across a hostile map within the sworn deadline — and if your patron breaks their half of the oath, seize the prize for yourself in open view.

epithet_grants.trigger: deliver an oath-bound escort target across a full map inside the deadline while your patron breaks faith, on a VIP table or higher

sources: Emerson, Pele and Hiiaka: A Myth from Hawaii 1915 (PD)

title: The children of Rangi and Papa force the sky and earth apart
summary: Rangi the Sky and Papa the Earth cling so tightly that their children are crushed in darkness between them. The sons debate: Tū would kill the parents, but Tāne prevails — bracing his shoulders on the earth and his legs against the sky, he strains until Rangi and Papa are torn apart and light floods the world. Tāwhirimātea alone opposes the deed and flees upward to make war with storms.
reenactment_hook: Brace against a closing crush hazard — collapsing walls, a vice arena, two converging bosses — and force it permanently open by raw strength and leverage, creating the light and space the rest of the run needs to survive.

epithet_grants.trigger: force apart a crush hazard by strength and hold it open to the run's end, on a VIP table or higher

sources: Grey 1855, 'The Children of Heaven and Earth' (PD)

title: Tāne climbs to the highest heaven for the three baskets of knowledge
